To ask the Minister of Education whether the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill (NIA Bill12/22-27) applies to pre-school settings.
answer_text: No, the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Act (Northern Ireland) 2026 (the Act) intentionally does not apply to pre-school settings.   Section 11 of the Act defines the schools which must adhere to the School Uniforms Guidelines i.e. grant-maintained and independent primary and post-primary (secondary) schools.  In legislative terms, section 13 of the Act provides that nothing in the guidelines is to be taken to apply with respect to pre-school settings.  In order to future-proof the legislation, section 14 enables regulations to be made if it becomes necessary to apply the guidelines to pre-school settings.  However, as I stated at the second stage debate on 3 March 2025, the information to date indicates that it is at the post-primary and then primary level where cost pressures are being felt by parents; it does not suggest there are the same cost, comfort or practicality concerns in uniform requirements at pre-school level.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 43393/22-27, to detail the reason why her Department’s contribution to G4S reduced from £59,677 in 2022–23 to £46,553 in 2024–25, given indications that the site for which her Department is responsible, was subject to trespass and arson attacks.
answer_text: Security costs at the St Lucia site were previously shared between my Department and the Ministry of Defence (MOD).  During the 2022-23 year, the MOD made a decision to remove their contribution towards security and as a result the level of security on site was amended to cover only the DfI areas of the site. To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 43396/22-27, whether under the proposals to raise the minimum age of criminal responsibility to 14, whether a child aged 13 suspected of grievous bodily harm with intent, could be arrested or prosecuted.
answer_text: The arrest and prosecution of children following any potential increase in the minimum age of criminal responsibility (MACR) in Northern Ireland will depend on what arrangements are ultimately put in place, including any exceptions for certain offences.  There are currently two amendments tabled to the Justice Bill in respect of raising the MACR.  The first proposes an increase from the current age of 10 years to 14 years with exceptions for the following four offences: murder; manslaughter; rape and assault by penetration. The second amendment proposes an increase to 16 years with no exceptions.Should either of these amendments be supported by the Assembly, they would prevent a child aged 13 who is suspected of committing grievous bodily harm with intent from being arrested or prosecuted in relation to that particular offence.  However, as explained in my previous answer, this does not mean that the concerning behaviour demonstrated by the child would be ignored.  Should any increase to MACR be supported, my Department would not commence the necessary legislative provisions until such a time as an appropriate framework for addressing such behaviour had been developed in partnership with key stakeholders.  Subject to the outcome of future engagement, this could include restorative approaches to help address the impact such behaviour has had on the victim. Such a framework would seek tackle the root causes of the behaviour, providing the best opportunity to prevent reoffending and consequently ensure fewer victims.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has made any assessment of the sustainability of early years support services currently reliant on short-term funding.
answer_text: The Budgets for 2026-27 have not yet been set by the Executive, however, to provide clarity for affected organisations, I have agreed an Interim Resource Budget for the first three months of the 2026-27 financial year for early years services. I am not currently in a position to provide any commitments or timescales to further funding. I am aware of the impact this uncertainty and how working with short-term budgets has on early years support services in terms of planning, staffing, and service delivery, and I recognise the strain it places on organisations committed to supporting children and families.  No formal assessment of the impacts of the interim funding position for the 2026-27 financial year has been undertaken at this stage.Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026-27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to his Department’s commitment to ensure that inclusive design standards are adopted in all new publicly funded spaces and facilities in The Northern Ireland Disability Strategy 2025–2035, whether this commitment will extend to beaches and coastal areas, including the provision of accessible infrastructure.
answer_text: The Disability Strategy, the final contents of which will be subject to Executive agreement, includes a commitment to ensure that inclusive design standards are adopted in all new publicly funded spaces and facilities, which includes, where appropriate, in beaches and coastal areas. The Strategy also commits to adopt inclusive design standards (BS8300) in government procurement policies and to develop reasonable accessibility requirements for buildings, with a focus on providing equal access to facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when the Civil Service’s policy on workplace harassment and bullying was (i) introduced; and (ii) updated, across Departments.
answer_text: The Civil Service first introduced its Dignity at Work policy in 2008, to promote a workplace free from inappropriate behaviour including bullying, harassment and discrimination and to provide a framework for employees to raise a complaint where they feel their dignity at work has been affected.The Dignity at Work policy was recently reviewed and in January 2026, the revised policy was incorporated into the Civil Service Standards of Conduct policy (Section 3 and Annex 1) to highlight the importance the Civil Service places on acceptable behaviour and its ongoing commitment to creating and sustaining an inclusive and harmonious working environment where everyone is treated with respect and dignity, free from any form of unacceptable behaviour. The revised policy applies to all Civil Service Departments with effect from 12 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has had any discussions with the Minister of Finance regarding ensuring that any Barnett consequential arising from the UK Government’s December 2025 domestic abuse funding announcement is allocated to frontline domestic abuse support services.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assurances he can provide that any additional funding arising from the UK Government’s December 2025 domestic abuse funding announcement will be directed to specialist domestic abuse services and not absorbed into general departmental budgets.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has considered supplementing or rebalancing Local Growth Fund allocations to address any shortfall in resource funding for employability and community-based provision.
answer_text: The Resource/ Capital split for the Local Growth Fund (LGF) was set by the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG). The Executive have no power to amend or rebalance this split. Due to delays caused by MHCLG in implementing this fund, the Resource element of the LGF will be administered directly by MHCLG in the financial year 2026/27.The situation currently faced by Community and Voluntary Sector (CVS) organisations delivering employability and community-based provision is the result of a series of poor decisions made by the British Government. Despite repeated representations from myself and the Executive, most recently on 31 March, calling for a fairer Resource allocation which better suits local needs, the Westminster approach has not changed. At the same time, the Capital investment available through the LGF offers opportunities that must be fully realised. Delivery responsibility for the LGF will transfer to the Executive, initially for the Capital element (from 2026/27 onwards) and subsequently for the reduced Resource (from 2027/28 onwards). It is therefore vital that careful consideration is given to how both elements can be put to best use, maximising impact, and focusing on opportunities in order to secure positive and deliverable results. To this end, my officials are engaging with NIO and all Executive Departments.I have also asked Departments to assess whether other support could be made available to mitigate some of the impact of the MHCLG cuts. However, it is unrealistic, in the context of an extremely challenging budgetary position, for Westminster to expect a devolved Government to step in and make up their shortfalls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, without reference to school governors or guidance, to detail any steps he will take to ensure that a pupil's right to wear a coat to school is respected.
answer_text: The School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Act (Northern Ireland) 2026 (the Act) places a duty on my Department to issue guidelines to schools about their uniform policies.  The Act also places a duty on grant-aided and independent primary and post-primary schools to adhere to these guidelines.  The guidelines that formally issued on 23 February 2026 require all pupils to be allowed to wear a generic, non-branded coat.  To be clear, these guidelines are statutory and must be adhered to – they are not ‘guidance’.My Department will be monitoring schools’ adherence to the guidelines.  In line with section 8 of the Act, any school assessed as not adhering - including in relation to coats - will be directed to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of the amount of (i) time; and (ii) money, spent preparing the TransformED myth-buster document, along with the accompanying statement of 25 March 2026.
answer_text: The TransformED myth-buster document and the accompanying statement were produced within existing staffing and budget arrangements. Approximately two hours of staff time were required to draft and finalise the materials. No additional expenditure was incurred.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 40622/22-27, whether there is any mechanism where ongoing incidents of this nature can be reported.
answer_text: It is the policy of my Department that each school Board of Governors should establish formal written procedures for dealing with complaints. Complaints in relation to decisions made by schools or Boards of Governors are directed to the school Board of Governors in the first instance. If following this procedure, a person remains unsatisfied with the decisions made, they have the right to ask the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman to consider unresolved complaints about maladministration, which is generally taken to mean poor administration or the wrong application of rules in all publicly funded schools in Northern Ireland. This means that any pupil or parent who believes that they have been treated unfairly as a result of the actions or decision of a school’s Board of Governors will be able to bring their complaint to the Ombudsman after the school’s complaints process has been exhausted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, without reference to school governors or guidance, to detail any practical steps he is taking to achieve a greater gender balance among the teaching workforce.
answer_text: The Department of Education is not taking any practical steps to achieve a greater gender balance among the teaching workforce. I do not consider there are any obstacles or barriers to men entering the teaching profession.We can collectively raise and promote the public profile of teaching as an attractive career prospect for all, both males and females.Research has shown that academic motivation and engagement are not significantly influenced by teacher gender, with no clear advantage for boys being taught by male rather than female teachers. It is the quality and effectiveness of teaching that has the greatest impact on pupils’ outcomes and wellbeing.  The priority, therefore, is to ensure that teaching in Northern Ireland remains an attractive and accessible profession for high-quality graduates.I want to make teaching a profession that both males and females aspire to be members of. Significant improvements have been made through the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) to address teacher and school leader workload concerns. Terms and conditions for teachers continue to improve with the reduction of workload.In addition to improving the terms and conditions for teachers and addressing workload concerns, beginning teachers are now paid £32,916 which is an increase of 35% over the last 5 years.My Department, along with teacher employers, recognises the importance of a teaching workforce in Northern Ireland that is reflective of society as a whole.  Increasing the number of males entering the teaching profession is, therefore, an ongoing priority for our Higher Education Institutions (HEIs), which deliver Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes. In recent years, HEIs have taken specific steps to attract more male applicants, including ensuring there is equal gender representation and a balanced presence of male and female students when promoting teaching as a career, for example on publicity materials and when attending face-to-face information events or school visits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of proposals to raise the age of criminal responsibility to 14, whether a child aged 13, who is suspected of committing grievous bodily harm, could be arrested or prosecuted under any circumstances.
answer_text: Three amendments have been tabled to the Justice Bill in respect of raising the minimum age of criminal responsibility (MACR) in Northern Ireland.  The first amendment proposes an increase from the current age of 10 years to 14 years with exceptions for the following four offences: murder; manslaughter; rape and assault by penetration. The second amendment proposes an increase to 12 years with exceptions for the aforementioned offences.  The third amendment tabled proposes an increase to 16 years with no exceptions.You referred specifically to the proposed amendment to increase MACR to 14 years (which includes exceptions for serious offences).  Should this be supported by the Assembly, my Department would not commence the necessary legislative provisions until such a time as an appropriate framework for addressing such behaviour had been developed in partnership with key stakeholders.  Subject to the outcome of future engagement, offending or risk-taking behaviour would be addressed through the provision of a welfare-based, rather than punitive, response using existing statutory provisions as would be employed were such behaviours exhibited by those younger than the current MACR.Currently, where there is a concern over a child’s offending behaviour, a referral can be made to the Children’s Diversion Forum, which is a multi-agency panel available on a region-wide basis in Northern Ireland. These panels, or other such partnership arrangements, will be considered as potential models to work with children under any new age of criminal responsibility, should the Assembly support any amendment to the Justice Bill to increase the Minimum Age of Criminal Responsibility.Whilst any new arrangements are yet to be defined and agreed, they will be aimed at working with the child to help them understand the impact and consequences of their behaviour; to take responsibility for their actions; and to support them to make changes and avoid future offending. Often young children displaying such concerning behaviour have also experienced past adversity and trauma, and present with many and often complex unmet needs. A number will have been groomed, coerced, or exploited as victims themselves. Taking these factors into account, it is essential that our response is proportionate to individual circumstances, age appropriate, and delivered through a coordinated, multi-agency approach that addresses the root causes of a child’s behaviour rather than focusing on criminalisation.In doing so, we can ensure the best outcomes for children, families, communities and victims, whilst also preventing recurrence and reoffending.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has made any assessment of the impact that the proposal to increase the age of criminal responsibility in the Justice Bill (07/22-27) to 14, will have on violence against women and girls.
answer_text: The minimum age of criminal responsibility (MACR) in Northern Ireland, currently the lowest in Europe at 10 years of age, sets the age at which a child’s offending behaviour can be dealt with through formal justice channels.  It does not mean that no action is taken to address concerning behaviour displayed by children under that age, and this will remain the case should there be any increase in MACR.  Ignoring such behaviour is not in anyone’s interests and could potentially lead to more serious offending at a later date.  However, research has demonstrated that keeping children out of the justice system and addressing their particular needs and the underlying causes of offending behaviour using a welfare-based approach is more effective, including in terms of reducing future offending.Given this, my Department has not made an assessment of the impact that the proposals to increase the minimum age of criminal responsibility to 14 years will have on violence against women and girls.  Whilst any proposed increase in MACR would mean that children under the new age limit would not be held criminally liable, from a public safety perspective they would still need be held accountable for their actions.  Consequently, any increase in MACR would not be implemented until my Department had developed a suitable framework for this.  Such a framework would ensure that incidents of concerning behaviour could be appropriately reported and addressed, through the provision of tailored support to help children to change.  Ultimately, this approach would help to prevent future victims, as well as enhancing compliance with international child rights requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prosecutions that have been brought under legislation relating to regulated football matches, in each of the last five years.

Further to this, offences at regulated football matches may be covered by more generic legislation such as the Offences against the Person Act 1861 and the Public Order Act 1986.  Information on the number of prosecutions and convictions at courts in the years 2021 to 2025 for offences at regulated matches has been provided, although this may include offences at matches other than football matches. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to ensure continuity of MRI provision, given the short supply of global liquified helium following the outbreak of war in the Persian Gulf.

To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the report in the Newsletter on 24 March 2026, regarding football banning order regulations not being updated, whether there is any risk that the banning orders currently in place are unlawful.
answer_text: Having secured legal advice on this matter, I am satisfied that the continued operation of the Football Banning Order powers, and associated sports ground offence provisions contained in the 2011 Act, is lawful by virtue of the “always speaking” provision of the Interpretation Act (NI) 1954. This operates to prevent statutes from becoming outdated, including in instances such as this where organisations and sports championships are renamed.Under the devolution settlement, it falls to the Department owning the overarching policy area – safety in sports grounds, in this instance to consider whether there are gaps in the law or amendments required to existing legislation and, where that is the case, responsibility for any subsequent legislative changes remains the responsibility of that Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has sought or received legal advice regarding the validity of football banning orders, currently in force.
answer_text: As detailed in my answer to AQW 43145/22-27, legal advice has been secured on the matter and I am satisfied that the continued operation of the Football Banning Order powers, and associated sports ground offence provisions contained in the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, is lawful by virtue of the “always speaking” provision of the Interpretation Act (NI) 1954.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any contingency plans in place should funding pressures impact the delivery of summer schemes.
answer_text: The primary risk to the operation of special school summer schemes related to the safety and safeguarding of pupils, in particular those with healthcare needs.   Lack of funding was not a determining factor in the Education Authority’s decision to cancel summer schemes on 26 March.Following my direction, officials in the Education Authority liaised with the Department of Health and concerns regarding on-site nursing provision to support the safe running of Special School Summer Schemes for 2026 have now been addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any assessment has been made of the impact of the draft budget for 2026-2027 on youth services.
answer_text: Your question has been transferred to me from the Department of Finance. As you will be aware, Executive departments’ budgets for 2026-27 and beyond have not yet been formally agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive.The Finance Minister, without first obtaining agreement from Executive colleagues, published a consultation on 6 January 2026 in respect of a proposed budget for 2026-30.My initial assessment of the impact of this draft budget on Education was outlined in my Written Ministerial Statement of 6 January 2026.  Whilst the level of savings my department would be required to make have subsequently been revised slightly downwards, my initial assessment remains fundamentally unchanged, and the potential impacts on Education, including youth services, will be significantly detrimental.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any measurable outcomes that have been achieved since the launch of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework in 2024.
To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of the number of youth clubs and centres at risk of closure, due to funding uncertainty beyond June 2026.
answer_text: As you are aware, Executive departments’ budgets for 2026-27 and beyond have not yet been agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive and therefore my department is unable to provide a commitment in respect of funding in 2026-27, either for existing activities or for any proposed additional actions, until the Executive has formally confirmed the final opening Budget for 2026–27.  The Education Authority (EA) took a decision to award successful applicants, to the EA Funding Scheme for Regional and Local Voluntary Youth Organisations, 3/12ths of their requested allocation to cover the first quarter’s work commencing on 01 April 2026.  No assessment has been made by my department or the Education Authority (EA) of the risk of youth clubs and centres closing after June 2026 due to funding uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any company on the procurement list for the supply of climbing frames to schools has reported issues in meeting demand for orders.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised it is not aware of any ongoing supply issues with the current list of suppliers on its Framework for Outdoor Play Equipment. However in 2025, problems arose with one provider and their capacity to meet the demand of projects they had accepted orders for. The EA initiated poor performance procedures with this supplier. Following the commencement of these formal procedures, the supplier chose to remove themselves from the framework contract.The EA is working closely with the small number of schools affected to progress any outstanding projects resulting from this supplier’s performance issues.There are several other suppliers on the current Framework for Outdoor Play Equipment which are capable of meeting the current level of demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why GP surgeries do not have the same access to encompass as hospitals.
answer_text: The encompass system has been fully implemented across all five HSCNI Trusts, providing electronic patient records for secondary, social, and community care services. Hospitals and Trust-based services have direct access to encompass, enabling comprehensive patient management within those settings. However, extending this level of access to GP surgeries involves several significant challenges. Most General Practitioners operate as independent contractors within HSCNI and retain autonomy regarding their Practice Management Systems. The "GP System of Choice" (GPSoC), part of the General Medical Services contract, allows practices to choose their own accredited clinical systems, rather than adopting a single, Trust-wide solution. The encompass supplier, Epic, does not currently offer a Practice Management System compatible with GPSoC requirements. As a result, GP surgeries cannot have the same integrated access to encompass as hospitals, due to differences in contractual arrangements, technical compatibility, data migration and stewardship requirements, and information governance. Nevertheless, integration with primary care is a priority for encompass, and GP representatives have contributed to its design. GP practices across Northern Ireland are provided with read-only access to encompass through EpicCare Link, which offers a role-appropriate view of patient records and allows GPs to refer, order tests, and communicate securely with Trust colleagues. This access is not identical to that of hospitals, which benefit from full integration, but it ensures that GPs can view essential patient information and interact with secondary care services. Out of Hours and urgent care providers are being further supported, with lessons learnt and insights from the prison health pilot guiding next steps. However, a broader rollout of encompass to GP surgeries is not planned due to ongoing structural, technical, and contractual differences between primary care and Trust-based services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that, when consultants prescribe a long-term course of off-label medication, GP practices and pharmacists provide it in a timely manner.
answer_text: My Department has taken a number of steps to ensure that when consultants prescribe a longterm course of offlabel medication, GP practices and community pharmacists can continue that treatment in a timely and safe manner.A key measure is the publication of best practice guidance on prescribing responsibilities across primary, secondary, and tertiary care. This guidance emphasises the need for clear, timely communication between hospital consultants and primary care teams, noting that unclear responsibilities are a common reason as to why patients experience delays in obtaining medicines. It requires seamless coordination so that patients “obtain the medicines they need, when they need them,” supporting continuity of care and enabling efficient transfer of prescribing responsibility where appropriate. Off-label prescribing refers to when a clinician prescribes a medication for a clinical use that is not included in its marketing authorisation.  This is common in areas where evidence exists but formal licensing for the reason it is being prescribed has not been pursued.An offlabel request from secondary care should be treated the same as any other prescribing request. The GP must be willing to accept full clinical responsibility for any medication they prescribe and will base their decision to prescribe on the evidence base, justification of the prescription and that the prescription remains within their competence as well as the communication received from the specialist. A GP may decline an offlabel prescribing request, just as they can decline any other recommendation from secondary care, at which point clinical responsibility for the prescribing off-label remains with the consultant specialist making the off-label recommendation.Additionally, existing nonmedical prescribing frameworks—which cover nurses, pharmacists, and other qualified professionals—are designed to improve patient access to necessary medicines, support the management of longterm conditions, and ensure that prescribers operate within robust professional and statutory standards. These frameworks include clear provisions for prescribing medicines outside their licensed indications (offlabel) and help ensure that necessary medicines can be supplied consistently and appropriately in primary care following specialist initiation. Together, these frameworks and guidance aim to strengthen shared care arrangements, ensure clarity around prescribing responsibilities, and reduce delays in supply so that patients on longterm offlabel treatments receive uninterrupted access to their medication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has considered (i) the future of classroom assistants, working in foundation stage, given the multiplicity of tasks they perform; and (ii) whether the JE1 pay remains commensurate to their real-life contribution.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) evaluates its posts using the GLPC (Greater London Provincial Council) Job Evaluation Scheme. Foundation Stage Classroom Assistants have been evaluated as JE1 using this scheme and this currently remains the appropriate level for the role. The EA is currently progressing a strategic review of the Classroom Assistant employment model, and whilst this is aligned to the SEN Transformation Programme, the scope of this review will include Foundation Stage Classroom Assistants. The review will include job descriptions, contracts and a range of other employment-related matters including qualifications, professional standards and professional development for Classroom Assistants. The outcome of a such a review will better support workforce stability, enhance recruitment and retention, and ultimately deliver improved outcomes for staff and children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether special school summer schemes are at risk due to a lack of funding.
answer_text: The primary risk to the operation of special school summer schemes related to the safety and safeguarding of pupils, in particular those with healthcare needs.   Lack of funding was not a determining factor in the Education Authority’s decision to cancel summer schemes on 26 March.Following my direction, officials in the Education Authority liaised with the Department of Health and concerns regarding on-site nursing provision to support the safe running of Special School Summer Schemes for 2026 have now been addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 42151/22-27, (i) to detail the organisations and officials met during the study visit to China; and (ii) to place in the Assembly Library any (a) briefing papers; (b) risk assessments; (c) itineraries; and (d) post-visit reports, associated with the trip.
answer_text: The study visit was organised by the Controlled Schools Support Council (CSSC) as part of CSSC’s Building Capacity Programme aimed at driving improvement in controlled schools by providing opportunities for school leaders to learn from international best practice.   The itinerary included an educational conference hosted by Hubei Normal University (HBNU), followed by visits to schools, polytechnic colleges, universities and a Municipal Centre for Teacher Development across various municipal authorities in order to observe and learn from their education system.The Departmental official participating alongside school leaders met with a range of experts and professionals in the field of education including leaders of the various institutions visited and respective municipal authorities.A copy of the briefing paper, which includes the itinerary, that CSSC provided to all participants including the Departmental official, has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the proposed extension of notice-to-quit periods in the private rented sector aligns with the current statutory definition of being threatened with homelessness.
answer_text: The Homelessness duties are in the Housing Order (NI) 1988, including the ‘threatened with homelessness’ definition at Article 3 (which extends homelessness protections to a household), and the separate advice duty at Article 6D.  The advice duty applies to any household throughout a notice to quit period, whatever its length, and regardless of their eligibility for homelessness protection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department intends to use or review, Article 4 directions, or other planning controls, to limit second-home development in areas with high housing demand such as the Causeway Coast.
answer_text: A cross departmental group led by DfC and including DfI & DfE has been established to consider this issue.There are currently no plans for my department to use or review Article 4 directions, specifically for the purpose of limiting second-home development. Decisions on whether to pursue an Article 4 direction rest with individual councils in the first instance and were it is expedient to restrict permitted development rights taking account of local circumstances where there is evidence that such action warrants this response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has made any assessment of whether tenants who receive extended notice-to-quit periods in the private rented sector are still unable to seek assistance from the Housing Executive until the final weeks of their tenancy, under the current statutory definition of being threatened with homelessness.
answer_text: One of the objectives behind the Assembly’s decision to extend notice to quit periods was to provide private tenants with more time to seek alternative, suitable accommodation. This has the potential to reduce the number of households that will need assistance from the Housing Executive.  		The Housing Executive has a statutory duty to secure that advice about homelessness, and the prevention of homelessness, is available free of charge to any person in Northern Ireland.  This duty is not restricted to households that are likely to become homeless within 28 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance issued to school governors in relation to the right of teachers to request flexible working and job sharing arrangements, including any safeguards to ensure that teachers are free to exercise this right.
answer_text: Guidance in relation to both Flexible Working and Job Sharing have been developed and issued to schools by the Teacher Negotiating Committee (TNC).As you are aware TNC comprises of representatives from the employing authorities, the Department of Education and the five recognised trade unions and provides guidance on teachers’ terms and conditions as well as salaries.Flexible WorkingTNC issued Circular 2009/6 (TNC-2009-6-Flexible-Working-Scheme) – Flexible Working Scheme detailing how schools and teachers can benefit from flexible working.The scheme enables eligible teachers to request a change to the hours they work and also a change to the times when they are required to work.The scheme applies to all teachers as well as Principals and Vice Principals and aims to assist teachers with caring responsibilities to balance these along with their role at the school.The scheme also recognises that other teachers may also wish to make a request for flexible working and the Board of Governors are encouraged to give each case serious consideration.The right for teachers to request and avail of flexible working is set out in the Flexible Working (Procedural Requirements) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003.Job Sharing SchemeTNC issued Circular 2009/4 – (TNC-2009-4-Job-Share-Scheme-Teachers) - Teachers’ Job Sharing Scheme detailing job sharing arrangements for teachers, including Principals and Vice Principals.The objective of the scheme is to retain the skills and expertise of teachers and protect the school’s investment in recruitment and training, to develop greater flexibility in working patterns and to enable teachers to combine personal goals and circumstances with a continuing career.In considering applications and implementing arrangements for job sharing the Board of Governors are made aware of their obligations under equality of opportunity legislation which are detailed within the Circular.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41521/22-27, to detail (i) the number of meetings that have taken place between her Department and other relevant bodies, regarding security and security incidents at the St Lucia Barracks site, Omagh; (ii) any outcomes from those meetings; (iii) whether any additional security precautions have been introduced, following the reported incidents at the site; and (iv) any additional security arrangements that have been implemented on land owned by her Department, which provides access to the site.
answer_text: Since taking part ownership of the St Lucia site in 2016, my officials engage with the   British Ministry of Defence (MoD) to discuss the site. Adhoc meetings also take place with the MoD, PSNI and security providers.  Currently, security perform two daily patrols of the site and if breaches of the perimeter fencing are reported, my officials action repairs.Following an incident on the Department’s portion of the site in May 2025, my Department accepted an offer from the PSNI dog handlers to walk their dogs on site to deter anti-social behaviour.  My officials consider the current security arrangements to be sufficient for our areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has any plans to review the legislation governing the classification and rates applied to second homes, including potential changes to enable different rate values.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking a Strategic Review of all rating supports. There is scope for the issue of second homes and holiday homes to be assessed alongside the review of exclusions applied to the rating of empty homes. Officials in my Department have been monitoring similar policy interventions in other jurisdictions recently to see if there would be potential for shaping local proposals, and if any lessons can be learned from some of the issues that have arisen with such measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the initiatives for which he is providing additional funding to the Community and Voluntary Sector.
answer_text: One of my priorities as Minister has been to try to better harness the opportunities offered by our community and voluntary sector. Members will be well aware of the expertise and the appetite to often do more that exists among many of these groups in our constituencies.  Through the Core Grant Scheme, my Department will be investing £1.8m in 76 charities in 2026/27 subject to the completion of financial checks. This is a marked increase in the number of organisations supported through the Scheme in previous years and represents 31% of the total number of applicants. The Cancer Charities Grant Scheme has seen an investment of £2m available funding this year, with £1m provided by the Department and £1m match funding very generously provided by Macmillan Cancer Support. I am pleased to announce that funding has now been approved for 16 applications from charitable organisations for grant funding, for projects focused on tackling health inequalities experienced by those impacted by cancer.My Department has also introduced the Support While Waiting Grant Scheme, which has seen investment of £500,000 to enable voluntary and community sector organisations to provide direct support to people awaiting treatment on Health and Social Care waiting lists.87 applications were received totalling over £2m for this budget of £0.5m, and approval of funding to 20 voluntary and community sector organisations has been granted. It is expected that over 2,400 patients and their families will receive early intervention and support through the Scheme. The services supported will empower patients to manage their conditions, optimise their health, and be as prepared as possible for treatment when it becomes available.I remain committed to working closely with and supporting the voluntary and community sector. These organisations make an invaluable contribution to supporting patients, families and communities across Northern Ireland, and I recognise and greatly value the work that they do.

To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 41240/22-27, to detail any criteria used by his Department to determine the weekly repayment amount in cases of benefit fraud, including instances where repayments are set at £1 per week.
answer_text: Overpayment recovery is subject to various legislative limitations and safeguards. There is an overall maximum amount that can be deducted from benefits to repay debts.  From 6 April 2026 the standard deduction rate from income related benefits is £14.40 per week, a higher rate of £24.00 per week is appropriate where the overpayment has arisen due to fraud.  For Universal Credit, the maximum deduction which can be taken from an individual’s standard allowance to repay benefit debt, including those cases of benefit fraud, is currently 15%.Where a customer is working and no longer in receipt of benefit, the Department can ask the employer to make salary deductions to repay a benefit overpayment. There is a standard and higher deduction rate calculated based on an individual’s net earnings, the higher rate will apply to debts classified as fraud. Individuals can also make a voluntary repayment and whilst there are no legislative limits on the rate of voluntary repayment the appropriate deduction from benefit rate would be used as a guide. The Department has measures in place to support individuals experiencing hardship repaying their social security debt, this includes reducing repayment rates to ensure they are affordable and sustainable. Any reduction in the repayment rate for benefit fraud debts would only be applied in very exceptional circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to reduce the number of injuries experienced by teachers, in primary schools.
answer_text: My department is not the employers of teachers. Any injuries, accidents or assaults on school staff are not reported to my department but instead are reported to the Education Authority (EA).We are aware of a number of mechanisms that EA have in place to assist and focus on accident and injury prevention in teacher day to day duties and they have provided more detail in this response.Firstly, the range of support available to primary schools include Health and Safety Policy that clearly defines the roles and responsibilities for School Leaders, Governors and Employees.This is supported by extensive additional health and safety guidance and training for School Leaders and Employees on how to meet their health and safety responsibilities under the relevant statutory provisions.An Accident Prevention Team has recently been established within the EA to focus on accident prevention, and which will adopt a process for conducting an overview of incidents, near misses, complaints and claims with a view to identifying trends, lessons learned and dissemination to appropriate stakeholders when appropriate.The EA have several consultative mechanisms which facilitate ongoing engagement with Trade Union colleagues. This allows them to share and discuss both current accident data and trend analysis and to take on board any additional concerns raised by teaching staff through their local representatives. The EA also communicate regularly with the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland and other statutory bodies on all matters regarding health and safety.All EA support services work collectively/collaboratively to ensure new and existing activities and processes are competently and regularly risk assessed and appropriate safe systems of work are implemented so that when on school premises their actions do not present significant risks to others.Schools are also encouraged to request support from the EA to avail of their Health and Safety Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 41240/22-27, whether he intends to review his Department’s policy on repayment rates for benefit fraud debts, including cases where repayments are set at £1 per week.
answer_text: The rate of deduction from an individual’s benefit is determined by legislative rules and policy guidelines, depending on the benefit in payment. If an individual is working and is not in receipt of benefit the Department can ask the employer to make salary deductions to repay a benefit overpayment. There is a standard and higher deduction rate based on an individual’s net earnings. The higher rate is applied to fraud overpayments. Any individual can also make a voluntary repayment. Any reduction in repayment rates for benefit fraud debt is only applied in very exceptional circumstances. Very low repayment amounts are subject to regular review with amounts increased when there is a change to an individual’s circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 41240/22-27, to detail the average estimated time taken to repay benefit fraud debts, where the repayment rate has been set at £1 per week.
answer_text: Overpayment recovery is subject to various legislative limitations and safeguards. There are many factors which can influence the length of time it takes an individual to repay a debt including, the method of repayment, the value of the debt, the repayment rate, the number of debts an individual may have and whether the individual is in receipts of benefit or not.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment that has been made of whether non-disclosure agreements should be used in churches or other religious organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with the UK Government following the publication of the Motor Insurance Taskforce final report in December 2025, regarding the impact of motor insurance costs on motorists in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: During the development of the Motor Insurance Taskforce, I engaged with the British Government on several occasions, taking the opportunity to raise a number of local issues impacting insurance premiums here. Firstly, I attended a meeting, alongside the Minister for Infrastructure, Liz Kimmins MLA, with the Minister for the Future of Roads on 8 July 2025. I then followed up in writing to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury and Secretary of State for Transport on 24 July, stressing that any proposed solutions or changes set out in the Taskforce’s report needed to protect and support consumers here first and foremost.I received a copy of the Taskforce's draft report on 5 November 2025 and responded on 10 November. I was disappointed that neither the draft, nor final, report explored in detail or set out any actions on an issue I previously raised, around the inability of insurance brokers and insurers to trade directly with clients who have operations both here and in the South of Ireland, following EU Exit. To ensure a competitive local insurance market in the interests of driving best value for consumers, I again asked for options to be explored to address this.In addition, issues raised with me directly by the insurance industry, regarding the potential impact that a lack of regulation for Claims Management Companies (CMCs) has on premiums, were also highlighted to the Taskforce. We both separately asked the sector to provide clear evidence to support this, to date no further written evidence has been provided to my Department. The British Government has indicated that, in collaboration with my department, it will keep the impact under review, once that evidence is provided. This will help inform my considerations around any need for legislative changes here.I am aware that the Minister for Infrastructure has also been engaging with the British Government on matters of relevance within her remit.Throughout my engagements, I have encouraged a strong collaborative approach on reserved matters that have impacts locally, to work together to help reduce premiums and safeguard our citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any assessment has been of the reason why motor insurance premiums in Northern Ireland remain higher, or are falling more slowly, than in Great Britain.
answer_text: My officials and I have engaged extensively with the insurance industry to understand the main factors behind rising insurance costs here.  This included my meetings with the Association of British Insurers (ABI) on 13 February 2025 and with the British Insurance Brokers’ Association (BIBA) on 5 August 2025. I highlighted my concerns regarding the impact that motor insurance premiums are having on citizens and pressed on the need for action, especially to protect our most vulnerable in society. I have also raised a number of these issues with the British Government as set out in my response to AQW 42148/22-27.My department continues to press on the industry to provide insurance premium data for the North to enable such an assessment to be carried out. Unfortunately, this is not yet available. However, the ABI have indicated to my officials that motor insurance premiums may be higher in the North compared to Britain due to a number of factors, including: higher bodily injury compensation awards; higher legal costs; a higher rate of deaths and serious injuries from road collisions; and the higher cost of providing replacement vehicles and cost of repairs, due in part to less competition in the garage network. Clearly greater transparency is required to fully understand what is driving insurance premiums in the North, and to ensure that customers here are paying a fair price for the cover they require. My officials and I will continue to engage with the insurance industry, the relevant authorities in London, and other departments and stakeholders locally given their various responsibilities in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance his Department has issued to schools regarding monitoring and improving classroom ventilation, including the use of CO2 monitors.
answer_text: Ventilation guidance was issued to schools in December 2021 that provides clear advice on the correct use of CO2 monitors. Although originally developed as part of the Covid-19 response, the guidance is fundamentally about improving ventilation and remains relevant to school settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether (i) his Department has reviewed Northern Ireland’s classroom ventilation guidance, in light of updated guidance issued in England; and (ii) he will consider adopting lower recommended CO2 thresholds in classrooms.
answer_text: My Department and the Education Authority (EA) have considered the updated guidance but continue to rely on the expert advice of the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) when considering matters such as ventilation. Therefore, the decision to adopt lower recommended CO2 thresholds in classrooms would depend entirely on the scientific advice from the PHA to determine the merits of its applicability in school and childcare settings in Northern Ireland.The EA issued ventilation guidance to schools in December 2021 and is reviewed on an ongoing basis. Although this guidance was developed as part of the Covid-19 response, it is fundamentally about improving ventilation and remains relevant to school settings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether, during St Patricks Day engagements in Washington DC, they intend to discuss the principle of self-determination for smaller nations, including Northern Ireland and the Kingdom of Denmark, with United States officials.
answer_text: We use our engagement with international partners to promote this region as a place where people want to live and work, to visit, to study and invest, and to build the relationships that can help us deliver the Executive’s priorities.Global outreach and inward investment are important factors in helping us to achieve these goals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when officials from the Historic Environment Division last visited the St Lucia Barracks site in Omagh; and (ii) whether any inspection reports were produced during visits carried out in 2025, and up to February 2026.
answer_text: Conservation Architects from my Department undertook their last inspection of the barracks in March 2024. That was with a heritage accredited surveyor engaged by the Ministry of Defence. The visit was to look at water ingress and levels of ventilation.  No inspection reports were produced during 2025, and up to February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) enforcement actions; or (ii) prosecutions, initiated by the Historic Environment Division against property owners for failure to maintain listed buildings, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There is no legal requirement upon property owners to maintain listed buildings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to Freedom of Information request 2026 0030, to detail any reason their office would not be in possession of minutes of meetings involving the Northern Ireland Bureau.
answer_text: The NI Bureau China supported and facilitated engagement during the Minister for the Economy’s visit to China in December 2025. The meeting was between the Department for the Economy and Chinese officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any action the Historic Environment Division is taking following the reported arson at Crevenagh House in Omagh; and (ii) whether officials from the Division have engaged with (a) the property owners; (b) the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service; and (c) the Police Service of Northern Ireland, regarding the incident.
answer_text: Officials within my Historic Environment Division attempted to contact the property owner by email on Mon 23 February – the day after the fire - and subsequently on Wed 4 March 2026 to seek engagement and provide advice on safeguarding this listed structure, and to make him aware that any works he intends to undertake requires Listed Building Consent. Officials have not yet received any reply from the property owner.HED officials also contacted Fermanagh and Omagh District Council’s Enforcement Officer on Tuesday 24 February 2026, to confirm contact details for the property owner and to seek further engagement.HED’s Senior Architect for this area met with that Councils’ Enforcement Team on Monday 9th March 2026 to discuss a way forward. The council has advised it will be contacting the PSNI and will be writing to the owner regarding unauthorised works to the building and the required work to stabilise the structure. HED advised that a conservation accredited Structural Engineer is required urgently to determine appropriate stabilisation actions.HED officials have not been in contact with the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service nor the Police Service of Northern Ireland regarding the incident. District Council officers take the lead on enforcement issues relating to Listed Buildings Under the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 and have statutory powers to act where a historic building has deteriorated to the extent that its preservation may be at risk.For enforcement cases, HED officials routinely advise councils on the appropriate remedial works required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to reduce waiting times for cataract surgery.
answer_text: Cataract surgery is one of the highest volume surgeries carried out across the UK, including in Northern Ireland. It is almost always daycase, not usually requiring general anaesthesia, and the intervention enjoys excellent Quality of Life outcomes. Although sight-limiting, it is reversible, and not a time-critical procedure.You will be aware that waiting lists and times are a Departmental and Ministerial priority, including those for ophthalmology. Extensive work has been ongoing, within the context of my Elective Care Framework (ECF), to manage ophthalmology waiting lists and reduce the waiting times. A network of three Cataract Day Procedure Centres (DPC), established since 2018/19, manages regional lists to equalise and reduce waits across Northern Ireland. These measures have seen regional urgent surgical cataract waiting lists fall from over 3,700 to almost zero. Additional initiatives include building capacity within ophthalmology delivery, and recent investments to deliver an additional 1,500 assessment and treatments for cataract, within South Eastern Trust Cataract DPC.Waiting times remain a key priority for my Department and, aligned to my Elective Care Framework, work continues to expand elective capacity across the HSC system. Through the Elective Care Framework (ECF), I am implementing a plan to deliver more sustainable elective care, including expanding capacity, additional clinical sessions, and continuing to utilise independent sector providers to ensure patients receive treatment as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether additional support measures are being considered for households impacted by recent increases in oil and gas prices.
answer_text: I refer you to the answer to AQW 41656/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has been made of the current regulatory arrangements and whether they provide adequate protection for consumers during periods of global oil and gas price volatility.
answer_text: I am very concerned about the rise in the price of heating oil following the attack on Iran. Gas and electricity prices are regulated by the Utility Regulator, providing consumer protections and oversight.Heating oil is not regulated here, in Britain, or indeed anywhere in Europe and is sold in a competitive retail market. However, oil suppliers are required to comply with a range of consumer protection laws.The Trading Standards Service works to protect consumers from illegal trading practices, such as unfair pricing, while supporting legitimate businesses. It provides guidance to oil retailers on their obligations and if consumers have a complaint about oil deliveries they should contact the Department for the Economy’s consumer advice helpline Consumerline on 0300 123 6262 or log on to Consumerline. I welcome the Competition and Markets Authority’s announcement of a market study into home heating oil. It will serve to strengthen competition, improve transparency and consumer protection, and ensure pricing reflects genuine costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any options her Department has considered to mitigate spikes in oil and gas price, including the potential establishment of a strategic oil reserve or further regulatory measures.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures her Department intends to take to ensure energy providers in Northern Ireland do not profiteer from global events.
answer_text: In the North, Power NI is the incumbent supplier of domestic electricity, while SSE Airtricity and Firmus Energy are the incumbent suppliers of domestic gas. Their tariffs are regulated by the Utility Regulator, which reviews and approves the maximum prices they can charge to safeguard affordability and protect consumers.  Competition with these regulated incumbent suppliers by additional third-party intermediaries then provide further options and value for consumers. 		Home heating oil is sold in a competitive retail market and Trading Standards Service plays a key role in protecting consumers from illegal trading practices, including unfair pricing, while supporting legitimate businesses. It also provides guidance to oil retailers on their obligations and advice to consumers on raising complaints.		I met with the Competition and Markets Authority on 19 March and welcome its formal study of the oil retail market. My officials in Trading Standards Service and the Consumer Council will provide data to support this work, and I will carefully consider any recommendations arising from its findings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to minutes released under Freedom of Information request FOI 2026-0024 by The Executive Office of their meeting with the Chief Constable on 13 January 2025, to detail the reason they indicated that they could not discuss the matter of the Police Ombudsman.
answer_text: As was stated in the minutes of the meeting on 13 January 2025, any concerns should have been raised following the appropriate process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) training; and (ii) professional development, provided to teachers to support the delivery of financial literacy education.
answer_text: Financial literacy is introduced from the Foundation Stage within the Mathematics and Numeracy Area of Learning and continues throughout primary school. Financial capability, including money management and saving, form part of the statutory Northern Ireland Curriculum and is embedded across all key stages, through classroom learning and pupils’ everyday experiences in and beyond the classroom.At post-primary level, financial capability remains a statutory component, particularly within Key Stage 3 Mathematics and Numeracy, where pupils develop understanding of personal finance issues and develop the skills to make informed and responsible financial decisions. It is further supported through wider learning, including Home Economics and Employability, covering consumer awareness, resource management, enterprise and economic decision-making. At Key Stage 4, Learning for Life and Work is compulsory and schools must provide access to at least one qualification route in this area, a number of which include financial literacy elements.As part of wider curriculum reform, a Curriculum Taskforce is also considering the future role of Personal, Social, Civic and Careers (PSCC) education, which will combine aspects previously delivered through Learning for Life and Work.This work is progressing, and opportunities to enhance the coherence and visibility of financial literacy within the revised curriculum are being explored as part of that process.CCEA supports teachers through curriculum guidance and an extensive range of primary and post primary resources, including professional learning materials, which can support both teacher professional learning and classroom delivery. CCEA also works with key partners such as the Money and Pensions Service and Young Enterprise Northern Ireland to maintain financial education resources and training materials and provide professional learning opportunities. This includes the ‘Your Money Matters’ post-primary resource, financial education guidance to help schools review and improve provision, and support for free teacher conferences in March 2023 and March 2026 (including SEN-focused provision).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has made any assessment of the sustainability of the funding model for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS), given that mainstream classes are receiving a fractional amount for their annual requisitions, compared to their SPiMS counterparts.
answer_text: On 4 February I published a Five-Year-Education Budget-Strategy, for public consultation in response to the significant and growing financial pressures facing the education system. Chapter 4, section IV of the Strategy sets out the Department’s assessment of the SEN support model for statemented pupils in mainstream schools.As part of the consultation the Department is seeking views on how Special Education Needs resources are deployed and how funding models can be improved to ensure both sustainability and value for money while meeting the needs of children and young people.  The consultation is open until 3 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) travel expenses; and (ii) other financial supports, that are available to nursing students.
answer_text: Nursing students on pre-registration degree programmes commissioned by my Department are able to claim travel costs for journeys to and from clinical placements when these exceed their normal daily travel expenses to their Educational Institution.  Claims should be based on the cheapest available method of travel. Eligible expenses include public transport, private vehicle mileage at 25.7p per mile, or preapproved taxi fares, up to a maximum of £25 per day.My Department pays tuition fees for eligible pre-registration nursing students on commissioned programmes. Students receive a nonmeanstested bursary of £5,165 per year (£430 per month) and may also be eligible for a range of means-tested bursaries including disabled students’ allowance, childcare support and maintenance allowance.Students experiencing financial hardship may also be eligible to apply for assistance through their University’s hardship programme. However, allocation of these funds does not fall under my Department’s responsibility.
