question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to the ongoing redevelopment of the Belfast cruise terminal, to outline any steps her Department is taking to promote Northern Ireland among the cruise industry.
answer_text: The promotion and marketing of all ports as cruise ship destinations are the responsibility of the ports themselves, with support from relevant cruise industry bodies. The ports which currently welcome cruise ships are Belfast, Foyle, Bangor and Warrenpoint.  These marketing activities are delivered through sales missions, promotional familiarisation visits to attract cruise ship calls and the provision of excellent visitor servicing to maximise the visitor experience for passengers arriving. In Belfast this is provided by Cruise Belfast, a partnership between Visit Belfast and Belfast Harbour, which delivers a collaborative sales, marketing and visitor servicing partnership to promote Belfast Harbour as a Cruise ship destination. Foyle Port delivers the marketing of its four docks - Lisahally, Fort George & Magilligan in the North and Greencastle in County Donegal. Foyle Port is supported in visitor servicing by Visit Derry.  Both Bangor and Warrenpoint Ports are also supported in delivering visitor servicing to cruise ship passengers by their relevant local authority councils, Ards and North Down and Newry and Mourne. In 2024, Cruise Ireland and Cruise Britain signed a memorandum of understanding to work collaboratively to market both the island of Ireland and Britain as a cruise destination. This collaborative effort was recognised in September 2025, with the award of ‘Destination of the Year’ presented to Ireland and Britain at the Seatrade Cruise Awards in Hamburg.Tourism NI supports the delivery of visitor servicing operations at the Belfast Cruise Welcome Hub via an annual Service Level Agreement with Belfast City Council. As approximately 50% of all arriving cruise passengers into Belfast do not have pre-arranged excursions in place before reaching shore, this is a vital mechanism in maximising the tourism opportunity and economic benefit of cruise visitors arriving here. Cruise ship visits to the north deliver approximately £20-25m annually to the local economy, dependent on the number of ships visiting, their passenger capacity and uptake of ticket sales. In 2025, this was estimated to be approximately £23.5 million, and in 2024 was approximately £20.5 million. Typically, over 97% of this economic benefit derives from passengers and ships arriving into Belfast Harbour.Whilst this tourism market is welcomed, in 2024 it represented just under 2% of the £1.1bn total economic benefit delivered by overnight visitors here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any funding allocated for play therapy and counselling, in primary schools, in each of the last three financial years, including how that funding was distributed.
answer_text: There has been no funding allocated specifically to primary schools for play therapy and counselling, in the last three financial years (23/24, 24/25, 25/26).Schools have flexibility to use their individual budgets to best meet the needs of their pupils, and this may include employing the services of a counsellor or therapist.  Schools in receipt of Extended Schools Programme funding may also use this to provide counselling and play therapy, expenditure on counselling services is not disaggregated.  In 23/24, 38 primary schools provided counselling services and 3 primary schools provided play and art therapy.  In 24/25, 42 primary schools provided counselling services and 7 primary schools provided play and art therapy.   Data for 25/26 is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given Ballymoney’s growing population and commuter demand, as well as the North Coast’s strategic importance for tourism, whether she will consider upgrading the entire A26 Frosses Road to a dual carriageway.
answer_text: The A26 Frosses Road forms part of the strategic transport network. The Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035. Once this is published, it will help to inform the Regional Strategic Transport Plan which will consider improvements to the strategic transport network. In the interim, my officials continue to keep safety on this important route under review, and the junctions of Kirk Road and Knock Road with the A26 Frosses Road will continue to be monitored.  I have also been advised that officials plan to undertake further traffic surveys at the junction of the Knock Road and A26 Frosses Road and will update you on the outcome in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has any plans to increase the capacity of our harbours at Warrenpoint, Portrush and Ballycastle, for the cruise industry.
answer_text: The ports which currently welcome cruise ships here are Belfast, Foyle, Bangor and Warrenpoint.  Decisions for these ports in relation to increasing their capacity are the responsibility of the ports themselves, with marketing and sales support provided from relevant cruise industry bodies. Over the past 3 years Warrenpoint has typically welcomed between 2 and4 cruise ships annually, bringing an estimated £50,000 - £100,000 to theregional economy, although none arrived in 2024.Cruise ship visits to the north deliver approximately £20-25m annually to the local economy, dependent on the number of ships visiting, their passenger capacity and uptake of ticket sales. In 2025, this was estimated to be approximately £23.5 million, and in 2024 was approximately £20.5 million. Typically, over 97% of this economic benefit derives from passengers and ships arriving into Belfast Harbour.Tourism NI supports the delivery of visitor servicing operations at the Belfast Cruise Welcome Hub via an annual Service Level Agreement with Belfast City Council. As approximately 50% of all arriving cruise passengers into Belfast do not have pre-arranged excursions in place before reaching shore, this is a vital mechanism in maximising the tourism opportunity and economic benefit of cruise visitors arriving.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether local councils currently have the power to impose higher rates on buy-to-let properties.
answer_text: Neither local councils nor my Department currently have the legislative power to impose higher rates on buy-to-let properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of new bus shelter requests that have been included within Translink’s current programme of works; (ii) any funding that has been secured to support delivery of the shelters; and (iii) the projected timetable for installation of the shelters, subject to planning approval.
answer_text: The number of new bus shelter requests that have been included within Translink’s current programme of worksTranslink has advised that its Phase 2 Bus Shelter improvement project includes the installation of 111 requested new bus shelters. Any funding that has been secured to support delivery of the sheltersTranslink has submitted an Outline Business Case to my Department seeking funding for these works and continues to engage with officials. Approval of a business case allows a project to proceed; however, progress of the project will be dependent on the capital allocation available to Translink and the prioritisation within their capital plan.  I have not yet set a budget beyond 2025/26. The projected timetable for installation of the shelters, subject to planning approvalSubject to business case approval and overall funding allocation, Translink intends that installation of the bus shelters will commence within 12 months. The programme is planned to be delivered over a fiveyear period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Ministry of Defence, as co-owner of the St Lucia Barracks site in Omagh, regarding site security and the prevention of security incidents.
answer_text: My Department has part ownership of the St Lucia site, officials have had numerous meetings regarding security at the site and have offered to assist with security arrangements within their areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Historic Environment Division has carried out an inspection of the listed buildings within the St Lucia Barracks site in Omagh, following reports of vandalism, theft and arson.
answer_text: Conservation Architects from my Department have carried out 14 visits to the barrack’s complex since 2007. Their last inspection of the barracks was in March 2024 and was with a heritage accredited surveyor who was engaged by the Ministry of Defence. That visit was to address water ingress and levels of ventilation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has reported any incidents of vandalism, theft or arson, at the St Lucia Barracks site, Omagh, to the Police Service of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has part ownership of the St Lucia site, with the Ministry of Defence having ownership of the historic core.  Departmental officials reported an incident of arson within the DfI owned area to the Police Service of Northern Ireland in May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the funding for Healthy Happy Minds has been utilised.
answer_text: Healthy Happy Minds was a primary school counselling pilot which commenced in the 2021/22 academic year and ended in March 2023.  The funding was allocated from the UK Government Covid-19 funding and this enabled the development and initial operation of the pilot.There is no departmental or central funding for primary school counselling provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to introduce a statutory homelessness prevention duty for the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Public Accounts Committee report on Homelessness, published on 26 February, recommends that the Department should work with the Housing Executive to explore options for strengthening homelessness prevention through the introduction of a statutory duty.I welcome the Committee’s Report.  As per protocol I will respond to its Recommendations formally through the Assembly process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has given any consideration to extending the statutory definition of being threatened with homelessness, from 28 days to 56 days, or more.
answer_text: I am very supportive of proposals looking at ways in which we can better protect and support people at risk of homelessness. However, I have concerns that extending the definition of ‘threatened with homelessness’ from 28 days to 56 days in isolation would result in unexpected and adverse consequences.Given the current constraints around housing supply which are preventing the Housing Executive from being able to discharge its homelessness duties, the unintended consequences are likely to be that more people will enter temporary accommodation earlier, and they would remain there for longer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment that has been made of the potential impact of extending the threatened with homelessness period on (i) homelessness presentations; and (ii) temporary accommodation usage.
answer_text: I am very supportive of proposals looking at ways in which our legislative framework can better protect and support people at risk of homelessness. However, I have concerns that extending the threatened with homelessness period in isolation would result in more people entering temporary accommodation earlier and remaining there for longer.		As you are aware the Public Accounts Committee report on Homelessness, published on 26 February, recommends that the Department should work with the Housing Executive to explore options for strengthening homelessness prevention through the introduction of a statutory duty.				I welcome the Committee’s Report.  As per protocol I will respond to its Recommendations formally through the Assembly process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the requirement for PGCE students to undertake 24 weeks of teaching practice, without pay.
answer_text: School placements during Initial Teacher Education (ITE) are an essential and valuable component of a student teacher’s course, enabling them to gain practical teaching experience and the competences they require before entering the teaching profession.The total amount of time student teachers are expected to spend in schools is detailed in DE Circular 2010/03. This may be scheduled as shorter placements initially, followed by longer placements to build confidence and competence as students progress through ITE. During their placements, student teachers are not permitted to lead a class on their own and must always be under the direct supervision of a qualified teacher.The purpose of placements is to enable students to gain the practical teaching experience they require as part of their PGCE course. It is not permissible to engage a non-qualified teacher in a grant-aided school for any period and remuneration for the school experience provided to student teachers during ITE would therefore not be appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has considered introducing financial support or remuneration, for PGCE students during extended teaching placements.
answer_text: School placements during Initial Teacher Education (ITE) are an essential and valuable component of a student teacher’s course, enabling them to gain practical teaching experience and the competences they require before entering the teaching profession.The total amount of time student teachers are expected to spend in schools is detailed in DE Circular 2010/03. This may be scheduled as shorter placements initially, followed by longer placements to build confidence and competence as students progress through ITE. During their placements, student teachers are not permitted to lead a class on their own and must always be under the direct supervision of a qualified teacher.The purpose of placements is to enable students to gain the practical teaching experience they require as part of their PGCE course. It is not permissible to engage a non-qualified teacher in a grant-aided school for any period and remuneration for the school experience provided to student teachers during ITE would therefore not be appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the article in the Sunday Life on 22 February 2026, to detail (i) whether the reported benefit fraud repayment plan of £1 per week is accurate; and (ii) the number of benefit repayment plans agreed where the weekly repayment amount is £1.
answer_text: In line with data protection legislation, including the Data Protection Act 2018, the Department cannot disclose personal information relating to an individual, as to do so would breach the data subject’s rights. The Department has a robust counter fraud strategy in place and works to continually strengthen its capability and effectiveness in order to protect the integrity of the benefit system and the public funds that it manages.20 individuals have repayment plans agreed at a weekly repayment amount of £1.00, this represents 0.01% of the current Debt Management caseload. Low repayment plans are rare and kept under review; individuals are contacted routinely to discuss affordability.Please note that the data provided in the answer above is taken from operational administrative systems and is not intended for publication. Therefore, the data itself is not quality assured to the standard of published Official Statistics. The data provided is as at January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of agency staff currently engaged across the Civil Service; and (ii) how this compares to the number engaged in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The use of agency workers is a necessary way to manage some time bound aspects of temporary work and to provide maximum flexibility to support the business needs of the Civil Service and its departments.  The engagement of agency workers ensures essential support for the delivery of public services, thereby minimising potential impacts or delays in critical NICS operations. This includes resourcing for the Department for Communities in fulfilling the Department for Work and Pensions contract, as well as addressing challenges related to EU Exit, Windsor Framework requirements, Lough Neagh, and high-priority Programme for Government objectives.While agency workers help maintain service delivery, our focus remains on building a resilient and permanent workforce through strategic planning and ongoing recruitment efforts.The table below details (i) the number of agency workers currently on assignment within the NICS (as at 31/01/26); and (ii) the number of agency workers on assignment as at the 31 March in each of the last 3 years: Number of Agency Workers on AssignmentTotal31/01/20265254*31/03/2025493931/03/2024401531/03/20232975*As at 31/01/26, a total number of 5254 agency workers were on assignment within the NICS; 3822 were assigned to the Department for Communities, 2428 of which were assigned to work for the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP).  Those assigned to DWP represent 46% of the total number of agency workers on assignment within the NICS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any assessment that has been made of the training and onboarding costs associated with agency staff, compared with permanent civil service recruits.
answer_text: There has been no formal assessment made, however, both agency staff and permanent staff go through the same training and onboarding procedure. Therefore, there is no difference in the training and onboarding costs associated with agency staff, compared with permanent civil service recruits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the expenditure on agency staff across the Civil Service, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The use of agency workers is a necessary way to manage some time bound aspects of temporary work and to provide maximum flexibility to support the business needs of the Civil Service and its departments.  The engagement of agency workers ensures essential support for the delivery of public services, thereby minimising potential impacts or delays in critical NICS Operations. This includes resourcing for the Department for Communities (DfC) in fulfilling the Department for Work and Pensions contract, as well as addressing challenges related to EU Exit, Windsor Framework requirements, Lough Neagh, and high-priority Programme for Government objectives.While agency workers help maintain service delivery, our focus remains on building a resilient and permanent workforce through strategic planning and ongoing recruitment efforts.The total expenditure on agency staff across the NICS for each of the last three financial years is shown in the table below:Financial YearTotal Expenditure2023 - 2024£64,800,0002024 - 2025£135,326,3522025 – February 2026£135,290,018Individual Departments are responsible for pay bill costs associated with agency workers deployment.  The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) absorbs the cost of agency workers for work carried out on their behalf.  My Department does not hold a breakdown of costs split between DfC and DWP.  This information is held by DfC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of candidates currently remaining on live Civil Service merit lists; and (ii) any assessment that has been made of the potential to fill vacancies from those lists rather than with agency staff.
answer_text: As at the end of January 2026, there were 2,223 candidates remaining on 109 live individual Civil Service merit lists. These merit lists cover a wide range of grades and disciplines including professional/technical, uniformed grade, senior civil service and general service.  NICSHR are currently in the process of allocating circa 900 general service candidates from these merit lists which will significantly reduce the supply available. These allocations will reduce the requirement for agency staff.  Candidates on merit lists can only be allocated where a vacancy has been submitted for the same specific grade and discipline. When a department identifies a vacant post that requires to be filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. For every vacancy, an assessment is undertaken to determine whether the post can be filled from a live Civil Service merit list for the relevant grade and discipline.  Where a suitable merit list is available, this is the preferred option, as it enables vacancies to be filled quickly and cost-effectively, supporting value for money by reducing recruitment costs and minimising reliance on temporary staffing solutions. Only where this is not possible are other options considered, including the use of Agency workers.

answer_text: I answered AQW 39528/22-27 on 24 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 39527/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail any assessment she has made of the personal safety of prison officers when off duty; and (ii) whether any consideration has been given to providing additional protective measures, comparable to those available to other frontline justice personnel.
answer_text: (i) The assessment of personal safety of prison officers when off duty is communicated, as necessary, to the Northern Ireland Prison Service by partner agencies in the Department of Justice.  The current threat assessment is marked ‘Substantial.’(ii) Protective measures appropriate to the threat level and/or a specific individual threat have been available for prison staff over a considerable period of time. Protection measures remain available on a case-by-case basis, post the wider reduction in threat level to ‘Substantial’ in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the Capital DEL Barnett consequentials of £0.4 million in 2022–2023, and £0.22 million in 2023–2024, arising from UK Government funding for the refurbishment and enhancement of public tennis facilities, were allocated.
answer_text: Funding received as a Barnett Consequential is unhypothecated and forms part of the Executive’s overall funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) whether the Capital DEL Barnett consequentials of £0.4 million in 2022–2023, and £0.22 million in 2023–2024, arising from UK Government funding for public tennis facility refurbishment, were ring-fenced for that purpose; and (ii) how those consequentials were allocated, in each financial year.
answer_text: Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, meaning it is for the Executive to allocate the funding in line with its own priorities. As consequentials form part of the overall funding allocated in either a Budget or In-year Monitoring exercises, it is not possible to identify where individual consequentials have been spent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 40621/22 27 and AQW 38556/22 27, to detail any steps his Department is taking, excluding reference to wellness hubs, to support substitute teachers who encounter toxic working environments arising from pupil behaviour, given his acknowledgement that his Department is deemed their employer for HMRC purposes only.
answer_text: As previously explained although the Department’s logo is on the pay slips of teachers the Department is not the employer of teachers.Teachers, including supply teachers, are employed by the Board of Governors of the school in which they are engaged and, where appropriate, the employing authorities Education Authority (EA) and Catholic Council for Maintained Schools (CCMS).The Department’s payroll team acts as a paying authority which means that they provide an outsourced payroll function acting on instructions from the employers of teachers including supply teachers. Support for all teachers is provided by their employer and in addition, as previously explained, they have access to the EA’s HealthWell Hub. Any supply teacher with concerns regarding the behaviour of pupils should raise these with their principal or head of department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister pursuant to the answer provided by the First Minister during topical questions on 16 February 2026, whether any advice was (i) sought from; and (ii) provided by, the Civil Service prior to her decision not to visit Washington D.C. for St Patrick’s Day engagements.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the promotion of Belfast as an inclusive and stable location for technology and financial services investment forms part of their Department's international engagement strategy, including engagements in the United States.
answer_text: We use our engagement with international partners to promote this region as a place where people want to live and work, to visit, to study and invest, and to build the relationships that can help us deliver the Executive’s priorities.Global outreach and inward investment are important factors in helping us to achieve these goals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any policy or guidance in place regarding the use of images of health and social care facilities, including the Children’s Hospital, in party-political publications or branding.
answer_text: I am not aware of any guidance specifically relating to this, but the latest document, ‘Local Government Elections 2023: Guidance on Conduct for the Northern Ireland Civil Service’, which can be found at https://www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/execoffice/local-government-election-2023-pre-election-guidance.pdf does provide some steer in paragraphs 13 and 14.  Most Health and Social Care (HSC) buildings are public spaces so in theory there would be nothing to stop political parties taking/using images of the outside of buildings, although it would be common courtesy to seek permission. Trusts will have guidance and warning signs in place to try to prevent photography/filming inside buildings where patient/service user and staff confidentiality could be compromised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Emergency Department at Causeway Hospital will remain part of his Department's long-term plans.
answer_text: The future of the Emergency Department at Causeway Hospital is secure and will remain part of my Department’s long term-plan. My Department has consulted on proposals for a Hospital Network in Northern Ireland. At its heart, this work is about how we can improve sustainability of hospital services and ultimately provide an assurance that none of our acute hospitals will close. I have been clear that every hospital is needed and Causweway Hospital will continue to have a crucial role in providing appropriate care to our population. We must also recognise that the health needs of our population are constantly evolving. We therefore also need a dynamic approach to maintaining our health and social care services. Specific changes to hospital services, either have been, or will be, the subject of a separate consultation process led by the individual HSC Trust or by my Department through its programme of regional service reviews.  The findings of the Hospital Network consultation along with my Department’s response are to be published shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has a standard process for informing MLAs of capital project announcements relating to schools in their constituency.
answer_text: For Major Capital and School Enhancement Programmes an announcement is made to the Assembly in the first instance.Following the announcement a Press Release is issued, and the managing authorities and relevant voluntary grammar schools are notified in writing of the outcome of their submitted application for funding. Assembly announcements are not typically made for Minor Works projects given the substantial number of works in any given period and I did not make an Assembly announcement regarding the successful applicants for the recently approved Curriculum Led Programme. My Department does not inform individual MLAs of capital project announcements relating to schools in their constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 38556/22-27, to detail the reason Department of Education Northern Ireland is displayed at the top of substitute teachers' payslips and on the HMRC app when they are not the employer of substitute teachers.
answer_text: Teachers’ Pay Team, within the Department of Education, is the paying authority responsible for processing payroll on behalf of the following education employers in Northern Ireland: Education Authority (EA), Catholic Council for Maintained Schools (CCMS), Grant Maintained Integrated Schools, and Other Maintained Schools including Irish Medium Schools. This means that while the employer is the school’s employing body, the salary payments are processed centrally by the Department, and this is reflected in payslips to teachers, including substitute teachers.For HMRC purposes, the "employer" (or paying authority) is deemed to be the entity legally responsible for operating Pay As You Earn (PAYE), deducting Income Tax and National Insurance contributions (NICs) from earnings, and paying these to HMRC, in this case the Department’s Teachers’ Pay Team. This is why the Department of Education is listed as employer on HMRC communications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance his Department provides to schools regarding the use of school logos, branding or images of staff in political, or party-political, materials.
answer_text: Schools are expected to adhere to the longestablished regulations governing the use of school premises for political purposes. These regulations state that:“A school shall not be used for political meetings, the transaction of any political business or for any purpose connected directly or indirectly with Parliamentary, Assembly or Local Government elections except as polling booths on the requisition of the officer responsible in accordance with the statutes governing such elections”.  A reminder is provided by issue of a Departmental circular in advance of political elections.  The most recent DE Circular 2024 09 issued on 24 May 2024.  This guidance does not restrict the wellestablished practice of schools inviting elected representatives to participate in curriculumrelated events or activities that support pupils’ learning and development, such as speaking to a politics class or at a school prize day. However, schools must take particular care in the period leading up to any election to ensure they remain politically impartial and that no materials or activities could be understood as supporting any political party or candidate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to increase competition in the electricity tariff market for electric vehicles.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure (DfI) is responsible for the decarbonisation of transport including electric vehicle (EV) uptake.Delivery of the Energy Strategy, The Path to Net Zero, is focused on reducing reliance on imported fossil fuels that leave us as a “price-taker,” ensuring all consumers, including electric vehicle (EV) users, have access to affordable energy.My department has consulted on the smart metering roll out and will publish the final design plan in the coming weeks. Smart meters will modernise the energy system by enabling the greater use of renewable energy, creating new tariffs and services that benefit all consumers, including EV owners, through smarter and more flexible pricing.My Department works closely with the Consumer Council for Northern Ireland, which provides a free, independent electricity price comparison tool to help consumers compare all available tariffs, including time-of-use options that can reduce the cost of overnight charging. Consumers are always encouraged to review their current energy tariffs to ensure they are on the most competitive offer.  Electricity price comparison tool | Consumer Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of extraordinary or emergency meetings she has convened in the last three months, to coordinate departmental responses to road maintenance issues, including pothole repairs, during periods of adverse winter weather.
answer_text: I have been receiving regular updates from my officials on the Department’s winter service activities and the condition of the road network over recent months. Throughout the recent periods of adverse weather, I have had ongoing engagement with senior officials regularly to ensure that our response to road maintenance issues, including pothole repairs, has been appropriately coordinated.These discussions have taken place through a combination of briefings and additional conversations convened as needed, depending on emerging conditions. This approach has allowed the Department to maintain oversight of the network and respond to issues as they arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his Department’s timeline for considering and introducing further Modern Service Frameworks; and (ii) whether respiratory conditions are under consideration as part of any future programme of work.
answer_text: Reference to ‘Modern Service Frameworks’ is cited in the NHS 10-year plan for England, Fit for the future.  This is not a term used in NI context, and I am not aware of any policy position / commitment to developing these in NI and therefore cannot respond on a timeline for implementation.I can reassure you that my officials engage routinely with other Departments for opportunities to support improved models of care. The establishment of the Northern Ireland Respiratory Forum, comprising clinicians, charities and officials, has provided a platform for cross-sector work. The Forum is tasked with identifying respiratory pathways requiring future system change or investment, and it contributes to ongoing consideration of how a modernised framework could support more consistent standards in respiratory care.The Forum explore and considers the most effective, evidence-based interventions, clinical standards, best practice pathways and support for consistent delivery of services across the region.  As part of this work, officials have participated in discussions regarding the role of diagnostics, digital tools and data-driven approaches to explore how these could help accelerate the safe adoption of evidence-based treatments and innovative service models, ensuring consistency of standards across Northern Ireland. This reflects the ethos of the Modern Service Framework and I can assure you that my Department and officials adhere to this in all aspects of the work they carry out whilst working to improve services in health across the region.My Department recognises the substantial impact that respiratory illness has on winter pressures across the health and social care system. Acute respiratory infections, including influenza, remain among the leading causes of unscheduled hospital attendance and admission during winter months.  In this context, improvements in respiratory care would contribute directly to alleviating winter pressures by reducing demand on emergency departments, freeing capacity in acute respiratory wards, and supporting better outcomes for patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps she has taken in the last three months, to reduce the time taken to process and settle compensation claims, relating to damage to vehicles and tyres caused by potholes.
answer_text: My Department has continued to progress public liability claims as efficiently as possible.The normal average timescale for dealing with a vehicle damage claim is around four months.Unfortunately, this year’s extreme weather — from belowaverage temperatures and snow toprolonged unsettled conditions and Storm Chandra — has severely impacted the road network,compounding the effects of longstanding underfunding. Although delays in claims processing are inevitable,my officials are working hard to deal with the significant increase and minimise backlogs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration that has been given to allowing schools to procure their own playground and play equipment, rather than relying solely on central procurement arrangements, in order to reduce costs.
answer_text: I launched the Outdoor Learning Project at the start of February 2025, as part of my Curriculum-led Capital Investment Programme, to enhance pupil experiences of outdoor learning.  I included a commitment to provide £4m capital investment directly to pre-schools, nursery and primary schools to purchase the outdoor play equipment and resources they needed most in the 2024/25 financial year.313 pre-schools, 93 nursery schools, 39 special schools and 766 primary schools have benefitted from the funding.The budgetary amount provided to each school was tiered based on the number of pupils enrolled at the school.  All expenditure must comply with Northern Ireland Public Procurement policy, therefore existing supplier frameworks are utilised on behalf of schools wherever possible, with individual procurement exercises taken forward where necessary.  Whilst I remain open to exploring and utilising delivery vehicles that will ensure the most efficient and best value delivery solution, I am not currently considering permitting schools to directly procure at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps being taken to ensure that procurement frameworks do not disproportionately disadvantage the community transport sector, when compared with commercial taxi companies.
answer_text: The Executive agrees Public Procurement Policy. Each Department will consider its own transport requirements and the best means of acquiring these services while implementing this policy.  My Department engages services from the community transport sector to provide the Dial-a-Lift and Disability Action Transport Scheme via grant funding.  The Department considers this more advantageous to the sector than the rigours of commercial procurement.  My Department is not aware of any disadvantage to the community transport sector from procurement frameworks compared with commercial taxi companies.  Any organisation seeking to bid for a competitive tender can do so if they feel able to meet the requirements as set out by procuring body.  It should be noted however that commercial taxi companies operate under a strict regulatory framework that is different to that of the community transport sector and therefore it is not possible to directly compare them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any financial (i) support; (ii) reliefs; or (iii) funding streams, administered by his Department that are currently available to the community transport sector.
answer_text: I have allocated grant funding in 2025/26 to provide £3.2m to support rural Dial-a-Lift Scheme community transport services through the Rural Transport Fund and £2.4m to support urban Disability Action Transport Scheme community transport services through the Transport Programme for People with Disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any consideration has been given to the introduction of longer-term funding models, to provide financial certainty and sustainability for community transport providers.
answer_text: I have previously acknowledged my commitment to support community transport services.  Any consideration towards adopting longer-term funding models to support community transport services will be dependent on the Executive setting a multiyear budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any circumstances under which current procurement policy allows for service level agreements, or other non-competitive mechanisms, to be used in place of open tendering for community transport services.
answer_text: The Transport Act (NI) 2011 provides my Department with the authority to pay grants towards expenditure incurred or to be incurred in the provision of services appearing to the Department to be wholly or mainly for the benefit of members of the public who have a disability or are elderly or live in rural areas.  This is the mechanism via which my Department currently funds community transport services. While open tendering is another option for acquiring services, the use of grants is the preferred method when dealing with the voluntary and community sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure that public car parking spaces are suitably sized for modern vehicles, particularly Sports Utility Vehicles.
answer_text: The dimensions and markings used for on-street parking are prescribed in the Traffic Signs Regulations (TSR) (Northern Ireland) 1997. Within the TSR(NI) 1997 there is a range of sizes for both the width and length of parking bays. Traffic Signs Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1997.A degree of flexibility is available when providing on-street parking as the width and length of individual streets can vary. Therefore, the Department must take account of the existing streetscape and other features present when providing on-street parking bays. The minimum sizes for parking bays that the Department currently applies align with those used in the UK as stated within the Traffic Signs Regulations and General Directions (TSRGD) 2016.The Parking Standards Planning document which was updated in October 2019 sets out the parking standards in assessing proposals for new developments, including those for residential developments previously published in ‘Creating Places – Achieving Quality in Residential Developments’. Responsibility for operating and managing public off-street parking bays (except Park & Ride / Park & Share facilities) was transferred to district councils who now own, manage and enforce most public off-street parking bays.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of her comments that each Assembly Question can cost up to £700 to answer, to detail how this figure is calculated.
answer_text: I did not determine the threshold cost for answering Assembly Written Questions, but referenced that they could cost up to the approximate threshold cost. The cost of answering individual questions will vary widely. The threshold cost was noted in an Inquiry into Assembly Questions carried out in 2009 by the then Committee on Procedures.  The figure was also referenced in a recent publication by the Assembly’s Research and Information Service: Assembly Research and Information Service Briefing Paper - Cost of Assembly Written Questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of times the Head of the Civil Service has had an annual performance review; and (ii) who these reviews were conducted by.
answer_text: Under long standing arrangements, the Civil Service Performance Management policy applies to all staff, including the SCS, but it does not prescribe a formal appraisal process for the Head of the Civil Service (HOCS).  The HOCS role is structurally unique and does not sit within an organisational line management or reporting chain.  Accordingly, there is no formal TEO appraisal line under the corporate process. The Performance Management policy is currently under review and part of that review will include consideration of performance management arrangements for the HOCS role that safeguards the political impartiality of the Civil Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has engaged with the community transport sector to explore sustainable, long-term partnership models for the delivery of education-related transport services.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport and I am advised that it has engaged with community transport providers to explore their capacity to support this service. EA contracts require providers to deliver scheduled transport for named pupils every morning and afternoon during school term time. For the community transport operators, this means that those drivers and vehicles would be unavailable to support their core client base for much of the day. Community transport groups provide an important service for people who are socially or rurally isolated and those approached were unable to divert their limited resources to the sustained, daily commitment required for school transport. The EA would welcome further engagement from the sector on the potential to provide home to school transport services on a consistent basis.Where operators cannot commit to daily services, they may still be able to support other educational transport needs, such as school trips, visits or swimming lessons. These arrangements are made directly between individual schools and transport providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Civil Service staff, (i) in numbers; and (ii) as a percentage of the workforce, who have (a) been placed on performance improvement plans; (b) been subject to any formal performance management interventions; and (c) had their performance assessed as unsatisfactory, in their annual appraisals, in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Performance Management Policy was revised in 2019 to strengthen guidance for line managers on the early identification and management of underperformance, with the primary aim of bringing performance up to the required standard. A further review is under way as part of the HR policy renewal strand of the People Strategy (2025–30).Line managers in all Departments are responsible for managing their staff’s performance, including any areas of underperformance. They are supported by the relevant HR policy and procedures, with training and onetoone support available from NICSHR.Performance Improvement Plans (PIPs) form part of the formal performance management procedure and commence only once informal interventions have proved ineffective and an unsatisfactory rating has been awarded. Accordingly, a low incidence of unsatisfactory performance ratings or PIPs is not, in itself, an indication of limited use of performance management practices.The management of PIPs and informal interventions is handled locally and is not recorded on the HR system; therefore, this information is not available centrally. The Civil Service is exploring how the incoming HR system (Integr8 programme) could more effectively support the Performance Management Policy.The number of staff who have had their performance assessed as unsatisfactory in the period 10 February 2025 to 9 February 2026 is shown below:   Review StageTotal9-month Probation Review9End of Year Review11In Year Review2Indicator of Performance1PPA/PDP Creation1Grand Total24Using the official NICS Headcount of 24,521 at 01 April 2025, 24 equated to 0.098% of staff receiving an unsatisfactory annual appraisal in this timeframe, which spans two reporting years (April 2024 – March 2025; April 2025 – March 2026).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) financial support; or (ii) contractual arrangements, currently in place between his Department, or its arm's-length bodies, and the community transport sector.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has full responsibility for the procurement and management of contracts for the provision of school transport services. The EA has advised that it operates two Dynamic Purchasing Systems (DPS) for Home to School Transport, enabling taxi and bus providers to register their interest and receive tenderers. When a transport tender is issued, all approved DPS suppliers are invited to bid. To join the DPS, providers must meet certain compliance requirements, including a private operating licence, public and employer’s liability insurance, and confirmation that any vehicles provided for school transport contracts will have the necessary public service and safety certifications. Drivers and escorts must also have appropriate licences and enhanced Access NI certification. Any supplier meeting these compliance requirements may apply to join the DPS at any time, including community transport providers. The EA has confirmed that no community transport operators have registered for the DPS or are engaged in tendering processes; the EA has no contracts or Service Level Agreements in place with community transport operators for home to school transport. As a result, the EA cannot make a comparative assessment of value for money, safeguarding or community impact between community transport providers and taxi companies.

As a result, the EA cannot make a comparative assessment of value for money, safeguarding or community impact between community transport providers and taxi companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the BBC news article on 4 February 2026 regarding his Five-Year Education Budget Strategy , to detail whether his Department has plans to reduce the number of classroom assistants.
answer_text: The Five-Year Education Budget Strategy is currently out for public consultation and responses will need to be considered before the Department begins any planning for how this is taken forward.  Any new model of support for children with a Statement of SEN will focus on ensuring that classroom assistants are deployed in a way which maximises impact and better meets the needs of children and young people with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any contact she had with the PSNI, directly or via officials, regarding an Ulster Unionist Party social media post and graphic, on 4 February 2026.
answer_text: The social media post by the Ulster Unionist Party included erroneous information and inclusion of the PSNI logo positioned on the post in a manner that suggested that it was endorsed by the PSNI.  On that basis, I alerted the PSNI on the evening of 4th February 2026 to the use of the PSNI logo.  PSNI colleagues were also alerted at official level on the same evening.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the BBC news article on 4 February 2026 regarding his Five-Year Education Budget Strategy, to detail his assessment of whether there is still a future for substitute teachers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The purpose of the Northern Ireland Substitute Register (NISTR) is to provide schools with a facility through which substitute teachers can be engaged for immediate, unplanned, short-term cover for a period not (normally) in excess of 6 months and also to fill vacancies which are short term and which at the outset are known to last no more than 6 months. In all circumstances substitute teachers must be sourced and appointed through the Northern Ireland Substitute Register (NISTR).My assessment is that there will still be a requirement for schools to utilise NISTR to source and engage substitute teachers for short-term cover requirements.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the action he is taking to tackle the outbreak of TB in the badger population.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to whom the Head of the Civil Service is responsible to in terms of their performance.
answer_text: The Head of the Civil Service does not have a person to whom they are responsible formally for performance. This issue is under review by the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has undertaken of the impact of competitive tendering on the sustainability of the community transport sector, particularly in rural and hard-to-reach areas.
answer_text: In Procurement Policy PPN 02/21 Procurement of Social and Other Specific Services | Department of Finance (finance-ni.gov.uk) Departments are advised to firstly consider grant as a more appropriate means of engaging with the third sector. As most community transport operators are registered charities and therefore part of the third sector, my Department  considers the current grant funding model to be the most appropriate method of providing financial support to the community transport sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the difference in her departmental approach, between procuring transport services through open tendering processes and through service level agreements.
answer_text: My Department must, subject to Part (1) of the Transport Act (NI) 2011 and Regulation (EC) No. 1370/2007, secure that most public passenger transport services continue to be provided by the Holding Company (NITHC) and its subsidiaries. My Department has authority under Part 1 (2a) of the Transport Act (NI) 2011 to enter into agreements with any operator for the provision of public passenger transport services. Under this provision public passenger transport services currently provided by Translink are managed under the Public Service Agreement.The only additional transport services that my Department currently secures are community transport services under the Dial-a-Lift scheme and the Disability Action Transport Scheme. These services are provided by the community and voluntary sector and my Department is able to fund these services via the award of annual grant funding under provisions in Part 5 of the Transport Act (NI) 2011.My Department does not currently secure any transport services via open tender.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any financial or contractual support her Department currently provides to community transport organisations, including (i) baseline funding; (ii) grants; and (iii) longer-term service level agreements.
answer_text: I have allocated grant funding of £3.2m in 2025/26 to eleven Rural Community Transport Partnerships to support delivery of the rural Dial-a-Lift transport service.  I have also allocated grant funding of £2.4m to Disability Action to support delivery of the urban Disability Action Transport Scheme.Grant funding is considered the most appropriate way to fund organisations in the voluntary and community sector. Grant funding is currently awarded to those organisations providing community transport on annual basis in line with the current budget process. The funding is awarded after an application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how transport services for education-related journeys are procured, including the role of (i) taxi companies; and (ii) community transport providers.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has full responsibility for the procurement and management of contracts for the provision of school transport services. The EA has advised that it operates two Dynamic Purchasing Systems (DPS) for Home to School Transport, enabling taxi and bus providers to register their interest and receive tenderers. When a transport tender is issued, all approved DPS suppliers are invited to bid. To join the DPS, providers must meet certain compliance requirements, including a private operating licence, public and employer’s liability insurance, and confirmation that any vehicles provided for school transport contracts will have the necessary public service and safety certifications. Drivers and escorts must also have appropriate licences and enhanced Access NI certification. Any supplier meeting these compliance requirements may apply to join the DPS at any time, including community transport providers. The EA has confirmed that no community transport operators have registered for the DPS or are engaged in tendering processes; the EA has no contracts or Service Level Agreements in place with community transport operators for home to school transport. As a result, the EA cannot make a comparative assessment of value for money, safeguarding or community impact between community transport providers and taxi companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether community transport organisations are engaged through (a) tendering processes; or (b) service level agreements; and (ii) any criteria used to determine the most appropriate procurement route.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has full responsibility for the procurement and management of contracts for the provision of school transport services. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any assisted mobility provisions, including the availability of mobility scooters or equivalent aids, currently provided at Belfast Grand Central Station, to support disabled passengers to move independently within the station; and (ii) whether any assessment has been made of the need for additional provision.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink. I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Regarding accessibility aids within the station, staff have wheelchairs which are available on request to help whilst inside the station.Regarding a passenger’s onward journey beyond the station, Translink’s Accessibility Manager met with Shop Mobility Belfast at their new premises within the Great Northern Mall and discussed how they could support each other and customers. Translink also have shop mobility support information on the website: www.translink.co.uk/using-translink/accessibility. which notes that, Shopmobility Belfast can provide passengers arriving at Belfast Grand Central Station with a free loan of a mobility scooter or manual wheelchair. This service must be booked in advance by contacting Shopmobility Belfast. Please visit the Shopmobility website (www.shopmobilitybelfast.co.uk), for more information or go straight to the Member Options page to join. Alternatively call 028 9080 8090 for help and advice. The staff are also aware to inform customers of this facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of graduates currently claiming (i) Universal Credit; (ii) New Style Jobseeker's Allowance; or (iii) other supports.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by my department.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the amount it provided for (i) legal advice requested by the Commissioner for Standards; and (ii) legal advice sought by the Committee on Standards and Privileges, in relation to reports by the Commissioner for Standards in the last five years.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has specific statutory duties in relation to the Assembly, Assembly committees and the Commissioner for Standards, as set out in more detail below.  In particular, the Assembly Commission is statutorily required to respect the independence of the Commissioner for Standards, including that office’s role to determine and secure the services it considers necessary itself.  Pursuant to the Assembly Members (Independent Financial Review and Standards) Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, the Commissioner for Standards is not a member of staff of the Assembly, and is an independent corporation sole under the 2011 Act. The 2011 Act requires that the Assembly Commission shall provide the Commissioner for Standards with staff, services and accommodation, as the Commissioner may reasonably require for the purpose of discharging the functions of the office.  The Legal Services Office provides legal advice in certain matters to the Commissioner for Standards upon request, and, as an in-house resource, no financial charge is made to the Commissioner for Standards.  Therefore the Assembly Commission does not record the cost of legal advice provided by the Legal Services Office to the Commissioner for Standards.  In relation wider expenditure by the Commissioner for Standards, the Assembly Commission is required under the 2011 Act to secure provision in consultation with the Commissioner in the context of the separation of the Commissioner's work from the business of the Assembly and Assembly Commission and the Commissioner's independence   Under the 2011 Act, it is for the Commissioner to determine and secure the provision of such goods or services as the Commissioner considers necessary for assisting in the exercise of the functions of the office.  Therefore, whether the Commissioner for Standards has requested external legal advice, and the expenditure on it, is a matter within the responsibility of the independent Commissioner.  It is of paramount importance that the Assembly Commission fulfils its duties to respect the independence of the Commissioner for Standards.  The Commissioner for Standards publishes annual reports and the Member may wish to contact the Commissioner directly.  The Northern Ireland Act 1998 sets out the role of the Assembly Commission to provide the Assembly with the property, staff and services required for the Assembly’s purposes.  The Legal Services Office provides legal advice to Assembly committees upon request.  As an in-house resource, no financial charge is made to committees.  Therefore, there is no record of the cost of legal advice provided by the Legal Services Office to committees.  The Assembly Commission has not provided funding for any committee to receive external legal advice in the past five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the rationale for not consulting with driving instructors regarding the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme; and (ii) any collaboration planned to take place between her Department and the sector going forward.
answer_text: During the development of the Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) proposals, my Department was in regular contact with the Northern Ireland Approved Instructors Council (NIAIC) as an individual organisation and as part of a wider GDL Stakeholder Group.  Their input helped shape the GDL provisions within the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.Officials continued to liaise with NIAIC on the 2017 consultation on the consequential changes to the practical driving test.  Since my announcement that GDL will be implemented from 1 October 2026, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has arranged a meeting with NIAIC for 12 February 2026.  Monthly meetings have also been scheduled with NIAIC to the end of September 2026 to update them on the practicalities of GDL.  To further ensure broad and representative engagement across the approved instructor industry, regional information workshops will also be held in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of heavy traffic and multiple road traffic collisions, whether she will consider the installation of a roundabout or traffic lights, on the Kirk Road junction at A6 Frosses Road, Ballymoney.
answer_text: The A26 Frosses Road, Ballymoney is a strategic route carrying a high volume of traffic and, as a result, some delays can occur during peak periods.A traffic survey was undertaken on 30 September 2025 between 8.00am and 9.30am. During this survey period, officials observed waiting times for right and leftturning vehicles of less than 60 seconds, which would not be considered excessive.Separately from this survey, officials are aware of two isolated occasions outside the survey period where waiting times of around four minutes were noted. While such instances can occur at peak times on strategic routes, officials will continue to keep the operation of this junction under review.The Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035. Once published, it will inform the Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan, including consideration of improvements to routes such as the A26 Frosses Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of large housing developments being added to the area, whether she will review the traffic management system in Ballymoney on the Milltown Road, particularly between the Ballybrakes Road and Main Street.
answer_text: I am aware that the B66 Milltown Road is a busy route, however the level of congestion on the road is considered to be broadly similar to that experienced by most arterial routes, particularly at peak times. Officials continue to monitor the route and the level of congestion.Further work in this area includes an active travel route along the Milltown Road as part of my Department’s draft Active Travel Delivery Plan and the addition of a pedestrian island and footpath extension proposed as part of a new housing development on the road at its junction with Ballybrakes Road which should be completed later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department is taking to tackle the growing crime of live-streamed child sexual abuse.
answer_text: The online exploitation and abuse of children, especially when they are forced into live-streamed sexual activity, is a sickening crime and one of the most serious and urgent safeguarding issues we face today.Telecommunications is a reserved matter for which the UK Government has responsibility which means I do not have the authority to legislate directly in this area.  However, the responsibility to keep our children safe online is one we all share.  This collective approach is reflected in the NI Executive’s Online Safety Strategy and Action Plan led by the Department of Health and the Safeguarding Board for NI which aims to ensure that young people can engage with the online world in a positive, safe and responsible manner.  My department plays an active role in this work with officials contributing to the cross-departmental implementation group responsible for driving forward the delivery of the strategy and ensuring its actions are effectively carried out.Preventing illegal child sexual abuse material from being accessed online is a key objective of the Online Safety Act 2023.  My Department engages closely with Ofcom which has responsibility for the implementation and regulation of the 2023 Act. I have written to the Home Secretary and the Minister for Safeguarding and Violence Against Women and Girls to raise concerns about this issue and to ask what further action the UK Government can take to strengthen regulation, enhance technological safeguards and ensure that law enforcement has the tools required to detect and prevent this form of abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average time taken to settle a compensation claim relating to pothole damage to vehicles, in the last 24 months.
answer_text: My Department maintains claim records by Financial Year. I can, therefore, confirm that from 1 April 2024 to 4 February 2026, the average time taken to settle compensation claims, which weren’t subject to legal proceedings, was 23 calendar days from the date the liability decision was reached. The normal average timescale for dealing with a vehicle damage claim is around four months. However, the investigation of claims can also be adversely affected by increased volumes of claims and staff resourcing pressures across the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any quality assurance processes in place to assess the standard of pothole repairs, carried out by or on behalf of her Department.
answer_text: My Department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.Actionable defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor.  On completion of the repair, inspections are undertaken on a percentage of completed road defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed. Checks on the work of the Department’s internal contractor are also undertaken.In addition to the contractual checks above, issues will also be recorded at the next routine inspection.If a repair fails to satisfy the required standards, officials will request that it be rectified and subjected to further inspection. This process will continue until the repair fully complies with all stipulated requirements.Officials use DEM 160/21 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service when determining actionable defects and the Manual of Contract Documents for Highways Works when determining a repair to be satisfactory.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of compensation paid by her Department, in respect of damage to vehicles caused by potholes, in the North Antrim constituency, in each of the last 24 months.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20202021-20242025.Table 7 provides details of Public Liability Claim expenditure for the last five full financial years broken down by Section Office, claim type and expenditure type. Please note Section Offices broadly align with Council areas. The North Antrim Assembly constituency falls within two distinct council areas which are covered by three Section Offices, namely Causeway Coast and Glens East and West; and Mid and East Antrim.Claim figures have been extracted from DfI's Claims Management System, whereas the financial data has been extracted from the NICS accounting system, Account NI.Unfortunately, as the NICS Accounting System does not record the cause of incidents that lead to compensation payments, it is not possible to disaggregate the amount of compensation paid out by defect type.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any mechanisms in place to ensure transparency and accountability in the work carried out by the Shared Environmental Service on behalf of his Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the basis upon which transboundary consultation requests relating to the land spreading of poultry litter are initiated; and (ii) how often they have been made, in each of the last ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all invoices issued by the Shared Environmental Service to his Department and to the Northern Ireland Environment Agency in the last ten years, including (i) the nature of the work carried out; (ii) the purpose of the work; and (iii) the value of each invoice.

The information was required to inform the wider scoring and assessment of proposed investments. SES was also consulted to inform the development of Tranche 2 of the Scheme.Twelve invoices were received for this work totaling £ 25,037.40: (£11,628.90; £2,646.50; £5,278; £2,054; £1,625; £680; £195; £65; £108.75; £130; £160; £466.25)SES has not undertaken any work on behalf of the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all communications, requests, or consultations initiated by (i) the Shared Environmental Service; (ii) his Department; or (iii) the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, with authorities in the Republic of Ireland concerning proposed transboundary movements or land spreading of poultry litter, in the last ten years.
answer_text: Where manure/slurry/litter/digestate is to be exported to the Republic of Ireland (RoI), NIEA requires a copy of a letter from Teagasc/Department of Agriculture Food and Marine (DAFM) advisor confirming that the recipient of the manure/slurry/litter/digestate is a bona fide farmer and has the capacity to receive the stated tonnage.  Upon receipt of this information, NIEA may contact the advisor to verify the authenticity of the approval.DAERA receives regular requests from DAFM to confirm the status of a potential exporter of poultry litter from NI to Ireland for land spreading. DAERA confirms the exporter details, as applicable, and DAFM then processes the NI exporter application for authorisation to dispatch animal by product and derived products (poultry litter) from NI to Ireland. DAFM will confirm this authorisation to DAERA directly on an ongoing basis. The meetings below were also held with DAFM colleagues with topic discussed through the course of the meeting:North/South ABP & TSE Meeting - Gateway Hotel, Dundalk – 25 November 2024 – Manure/litter exportsNorth/South ABP & TSE Meeting - Ballykelly – 11 June 2019 – Movement of manure/chicken litterNorth/South ABP & TSE Meeting - Crowne Plaza Hotel, Dundalk – 31 January 2019 – Movement of manure/chicken litterNorth/South ABP & TSE Meeting – Backweston, Celbridge – 16 October 2018 – Movements of chicken litterNorth/South ABP & TSE Meeting - Canal Court Hotel, Newry – 21 November 2017 – Chicken litter movementsNorth/South ABP & TSE Meeting – Crown Plaza Hotel, Dundalk – 8 February 2017 – Chicken litter movementsNorth/South ABP-TSE Working Group Meeting - Canal Court Hotel, Newry – 14 June 2016 – Chicken/poultry litterDAERA is aware that Shared Environmental Service (SES) has initiated some communications with authorities in the RoI concerning proposed transboundary movements or land spreading of poultry litter but, as SES is not a DAERA body, does not know the full extent of such SES communications, requests or consultations. On one occasion NIEA communicated with National Parks and Wildlife Service to encourage a speedy response to communications it had received from SES.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether nurses in patient-facing roles are expected to take either (i) unpaid leave; or (ii) annual leave, if they are unable to travel to work due to adverse weather.
answer_text: I would like to take this opportunity to once again place on record my sincere gratitude and appreciation to staff across the health and social care system as well as partner agencies for their continued hard work and commitment to service delivery and patient care during storms and adverse weather. Decisions regarding staff attendance and any associated leave during adverse weather are the responsibility of individual HSC employers.  However, I fully support a consistent, fair, and standardised approach to this matter including the notion that no disadvantage should accrue to HSC staff impacted by adverse weather or its consequences.To support that aim, HSC employers, health trade unions and my department are working in partnership to develop regional adverse weather guidance which incorporates the principle that if it is not possible for a staff member to travel to work (by car or public transport) during adverse weather, or they are required to leave early, the line manager should reasonably explore all options. Some examples of which include: Arrange for ‘off duty’ to be amended to allow time off to be given;Approve time off in lieu/flexi to be used or time made up;Work from home if appropriate*Whether role can be done at an alternative facilityApprove a request for annual leaveRequest for time as unpaid leave* If unable to work on a work computer, consider whether there is a possibility to complete online training/CPD/ print material in advance for home usage.If the staff member cannot avail of any of the options outlined above, they should discuss further with their manager.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the governance structure of the Shared Environmental Services, including (i) who holds decision making and oversight responsibilities; (ii) any (a) legislative; or (b) contractual, arrangements which underpin those powers; and (iii) any accountability mechanisms in place.

The Department for Communities has no role in its operation, governance or oversight and does not hold information on the decision-making, oversight, legislative or contractual arrangements agreed between councils.Governance arrangements for SES have previously been set out in AQW 37639/2227.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of Ballymoney's growing number of residents and commuters, whether she will consider expanding the parking facilities at Ballymoney Train Station.

Subject to funding and statutory approvals, it is estimated the facility could be operational in 2030.
