question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of recent military activity by China around Taiwan, whether she has reviewed the appropriateness of recent or planned ministerial trade engagements with China.
answer_text: As the world’s second-largest economy, building strong relationships with China is important as we work towards long-term, sustainable economic prosperity across the north. It is an increasingly important market for many world economies as we have seen with the recent visits to China by the Taoiseach, the Canadian Prime Minister and the British Prime Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the current status of the former Music School site at 23 Charles Street, Ballymoney; and (ii) any plans his Department has for the future (a) use; or (b) disposal of the site.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that the former Ballymoney Music Centre was disposed of in 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any foreign companies that have withdrawn planned investment in Northern Ireland, due to political disagreements with her, since 7 October 2023.
answer_text: Investment decisions take time, with multiple visits and evaluations, based on each company’s global commercial priorities. These change over time due to factors such as market conditions, corporate restructuring, shifts in customer demand or technology, or wider economic pressures. Even after a decision is made, it can take 3–5 years to realise the investment.  While Invest NI works to secure and deliver investment, the final decision on whether to proceed, expand or withdraw is an operational matter for each company and is subject to commerciality decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of whether it is appropriate for schools to publish the (i) name, and year group of a pupil; and (ii) the name of a pupil's parent, on school social media pages.
answer_text: At all times, the Board of Governors of a school must discharge their statutory duties under Articles 17 and 18 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 to promote and safeguard the welfare of their pupils.With the emergence of online technology and methods of communication, these duties now extend to the digital space, including the use of school social media platform and requires schools to exercise careful judgement when publishing any information that could identify a pupil or their family. There may be limited and appropriate circumstances in which a school chooses to recognise a pupil’s achievement or contribution publicly. However, decisions to publish pupil names, year groups, or references to parents must be taken by schools on a case-by-case basis, informed by safeguarding, data protection and consent considerations. Decision making in this regard, rests with individual schools and their Boards of Governors, who are best placed to assess local context and risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to reduce waiting lists for paediatric ophthalmology.
answer_text: The  Regional Paediatric Ophthalmology Service are continuously looking at ways to actively reduce waits for our patients. The service has delivered several additional clinics on evenings and weekends to reduce the urgent waiting time to within 12-26 weeks.  Work is ongoing to organise and deliver more of these clinics over the remainder of 2025/26 to further reduce waiting times and deliver paediatric ophthalmic care to our patients in a more timely manner.  My Department have provided funding in 2025 for an additional paediatric ophthalmology theatre list to improve waiting times for children waiting on surgery.  This has helped reduce the numbers waiting on inpatient and day case procedures by 40% since February 2025.There has been a particular focus on Paediatric Motility. The aim from outpatient appointment to treatment is 13 weeks and we can confirm that by 31 March 2026 no patients will be waiting longer than 13 weeks for their surgery relating to paediatric motility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of AI's ability to manipulate photos of children, for his assessment of whether it is still appropriate for schools to publish photos of pupils on their social media accounts.
answer_text: The safety and wellbeing of children and young people is my priority.Advances in AI, including the manipulation of images, underline the need for schools to take a careful, risk-based approach to publishing photographs, particularly on social media.Decisions about whether and how images are used rest with individual schools and their Boards of Governors, within existing safeguarding and data protection frameworks, including parental consent.My department will keep guidance under review to ensure it reflects emerging technological risks while respecting a school’s autonomy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any options being explored for a long-term, sustainable funding model for childcare, that does not have an inflationary effect on costs.
answer_text: The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy released for public consultation on 17 December 2025 proposes that the Northern Ireland Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) is used as the foundation of the long-term approach to a sustainable funding model that improves childcare affordability for families.While the NICSS seeks to increase the support available to parents over the lifetime of the strategy, it does not have any direct impact on the increase/inflation of costs.  Increases in provider fees are linked to a variety of issues such as taxation, business rates, wage increases, cost of food and heat etc as well as private businesses having the right to determine profit margins, all of which are outside the control of my Department.I have included a proposal in the draft strategy to introduce fee publication requirements for those providers operating the NICSS and will continue to monitor trends in cost which will be used to inform the operation of the NICSS going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority has considered staggering the start and finish times of schools, so that buses and taxi operators can maximise service and reduce cost.
answer_text: The responsibility for setting school start and finishing times rests with each individual school rather than with my department or the Education Authority (EA). In determining these times, all schools must comply with the statutory requirements for the minimum number of hours pupils must be taught each day, noting that these requirements differ for children aged under eight and those aged eight and above. The EA Transport Service has advised me that it will work with schools and transport providers in a local area where it is agreed that using staggered start and finish times could help improve transport arrangements for pupils attending schools in that area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when officers in the Education Authority will receive an uplift in their pay scales; and (ii) any steps being taken to offer permanent contracts to all staff.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) can confirm that the 2025/2026 Pay Award for all non-teaching staff, (i.e. staff paid on NJC, Youth & Community Workers and Soulbury pay scales) has been implemented for the 2025/2026 financial year in line with the nationally agreed pay settlements for the relevant staff groups. Work is currently being untaken by the National Employers in relation to a pay offer for 2026/2027. This work remains ongoing, and no final outcome or agreement has yet been reached.  The Education Authority is taking a series of coordinated steps to increase stability and permanency for staff across our schools and services, as outlined below.A streamlined temporarytopermanent process for classroombased staffThe EA has been working closely with trade unions to develop a more efficient process for making longterm temporary classroom assistants, general assistants and nursery assistants permanent, where they meet the fouryear qualifying period for permanency.This work is designed to bring greater certainty for both staff and the schools in which they work, helping to stabilise our classroombased workforce. The process is being delivered over a period of months from November 2025 to March 2026. This expedited approach will be a positive step in recognising the vital contribution nonteaching staff make to the education and wellbeing of children and young people.Introduction of a new temporary engagement protocolFrom the 2025/26 academic year, a new temporary engagement protocol has been introduced to reduce reliance on temporary engagement of staff. Hiring managers are being encouraged to fill posts lasting longer than six months through open recruitment. This shift is intended to increase the use of fixedterm and permanent contracts.Ongoing permanency requests through businessasusual processesAlongside the new initiatives, the EA will continue to process permanency requests for all staff as part of our established businessasusual arrangements whereby schools and services can submit requests for staff to be considered for permanency where they believe the criteria has been met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, specifically regarding teachers, to detail any steps being taken to ensure that selection criteria is not developed in a way that gives advantage to internal candidates.
answer_text: Each of the employing authorities, Education Authority (EA) and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) and the remaining schools have agreed teaching appointment schemes which detail the process to be followed in the appoint of teachers to schools.Recruitment is a delegated function to Boards of Governors, and it is in the best interest of the school to recruit the best available teacher using the merit principle.The merit principle is a foundational human resources concept, ensuring recruitment, selection and promotion are based on competence, qualifications, and performance rather than any form of favouritism, or discrimination. It promotes fair, open competition, equal opportunity, and high professional standards.Training on recruitment and selection is provided to all governors and it is recommended that all those involved in selection processes should have undertaken appropriate recruitment and section and equal opportunities training.Governors are advised that the methods of assessment chosen by the selection panel must be objective and relate directly to the job description and the criteria contained within the person specification. They should be commensurate with the grade and responsibilities of the post and should be administered in accordance with the teaching appointment scheme and any guidance issued by their employing authority.Detailed step-by-step guidance for each stage in the selection process is provided by the employing authorities.In all appointments the board of governors are required to take account of all guidance issued in relation to teaching appointments, including guidance relating to equality of opportunity in employment.Governors are advised that in the event of a legal challenge relation to the setting and application of selection criteria that they will be required to justify they actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the current status of Causeway Academy; and (ii) whether his Department will consider offering extra transport provision for parents who prefer to send their children to the new shared education campus at Ballycastle High School.
answer_text: Causeway Academy will open in September 2026. The school will initially operate across three campuses to ensure a smooth and supportive transition. Key Stage 3 students (Years 8-10) will begin their journey at the Carthall Road Campus (previously Coleraine College) and a new Sixth Form (Year 13) will be launched at the Cloyfin Campus (previously North Coast Integrated College). Students in years 11, 12 and 14, including those at Dunluce School, will remain on the campus of their legacy school to complete their studies and examinations. (ii) The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport, including route planning and assessing pupils’ eligibility in accordance with my Department’s Home to School Transport Policy. The EA determines the most appropriate form of transport assistance for eligible pupils, having regard to efficiency and economy as required by legislation. It is not required to introduce new bus services for small numbers of pupils, and the provision of direct services depends on demand from eligible pupils. Pupils may need to use connecting services and in some cases, where no suitable services exist, a parental payment may be offered instead.Parents may choose which school they wish their child to attend, however bypassing a suitable school within qualifying distance may result in a pupil being ineligible for transport assistance; selecting a further away school may result in having access to fewer or less convenient transport options. Before making a school application, I would strongly encourage parents to check the detailed eligibility information provided on the EA website and to seek advice from the EA Transport Service if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps being taken to retain experienced teachers who are contemplating leaving the workforce due to stress.
answer_text: Any teacher who is contemplating leaving the workforce due to stress should discuss the situation with their head of department, principal or employing authority to determine what action can be taken to alleviate the stress and to support the teacher to continue in work.The Education Authority (EA) has developed a Health Well Hub.  The Hub is an external digital solution that provides a range of initiatives and information in relation to Health and Wellbeing.Within the Hub – Health Well, staff can access a range of mental health and wellbeing interventions within the Employee Assistance Program – Lena (by Inspire).In addition, principals can contact EA’s Health and Wellbeing Service directly via their Helpdesk to discuss further bespoke support in relation to health and wellbeing interventions and training.Significant progress has also been made with the 26 TNC workload commitments from the 2024-25 teachers' pay agreement and you will be aware that I also commissioned an Independent Review of Teachers' Workload. The independent panel established for this review has submitted a detailed report to me with recommendations and my officials have been examining the report’s findings to develop an action plan.  I want to assure you that active steps are being taken to resolve the longstanding issue of excessive teacher workload and this in turn should reduce stress levels of many teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has in place to protect our economy, in the event of the European Union deciding to proceed with counter-tariffs against the United States of America.
answer_text: The global trading environment remains highly uncertain, and although the US President’s most recent threat of additional tariffs related to Greenland have receded, the risk of EU countertariffs persists.	I continue to monitor the situation closely and have regular engagement with British and Irish trade Ministers, supported by realtime intelligence from my Tariff Working Group. My primary objective is to ensure our unique circumstances are fully considered by both the British Government and the EU. Support and guidance for businesses is available and will be monitored and updated on a regular basis as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when teachers will receive their backdated pay rise.
answer_text: Agreement on the Teachers’ Pay Award for 1 September 2025 was reached between the Minister and the teaching unions on 13 January 2026. The Department has confirmed that the backdated pay increase for all teachers on the Teachers’ Pay Team payroll will be issued in February 2026.Temporary teachers will receive payment on 17 February 2026, and permanent teachers will be paid on 27 February 2026. The backdated award will also be applied to any teacher who has left the profession since 1 September 2025.Please note;Answer refers to teachers processed through the Teachers Pay Team’s payroll system and does not include Voluntary Grammar schools.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any plans are in place for them to visit Washington D.C. for St Patrick's Day celebrations.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister attended St Patrick’s Day events in Washington DC.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 38029/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 38029/22-27 was answered on 18 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any external support available for substitute teachers who find themselves in a toxic work environment, in terms of the behaviour of disruptive pupils.
answer_text: The Department of Education is not the employers of substitute teachers and have asked the Education Authority (EA) to provide the necessary details for the response.  EA has advised that all EA staff, whether temporary or permanent are able to access the EA’s HealthWell Hub.  The Hub is an external Digital Solution that provides a range of initiatives and information in relation to Health and Wellbeing.Within the Hub – Health Well, staff can access a range of mental health and wellbeing interventions within the Employee Assistance Program – Lena (by Inspire).In addition, Principals can contact EA’s Health and Wellbeing Service directly via their Helpdesk to discuss further bespoke support in relation to Health and Wellbeing interventions and training.EA has confirmed that the HealthWell Hub is accessible to all substitute teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the reason why the cost of mileage claimed by teachers attending off-site training is met from individual school budgets; and (ii) whether his Department is aware of any impact this has on teachers’ ability, or willingness, to claim such expenses.
answer_text: All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme.  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools (LMS) arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  The amount of funding delegated to each individual school under the CFS, is intended to meet a range of expenditure items as outlined in Annex A1 of the Scheme, this is expected to include training costs and travel and subsistence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the current status of the former Ballymoney Technical College site, following the relocation of provision to Coleraine; and (ii) any plans his Department has for the future (a) use; or (b) disposal of the site.
answer_text: I can advise that (i) the current status of the former Ballymoney Technical College site is it continues to be owned and managed by Northern Regional College; and (ii) as this site is surplus to requirements, there are no plans for future use by my Department, with the College currently progressing the disposal of the site through the Department of Finance’s Land and Property Services’ D1 process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has identified any gaps in accessibility provision at major public facilities and transport hubs, including those relating to assisted mobility and independent access, as part of its work on the Disability Strategy Action Plan.
answer_text: The Disability Strategy provides a long-term cross-departmental framework to improve outcomes for Deaf and disabled people in Northern Ireland by tackling barriers, promoting inclusion and supporting independence and participation. This will include identifying how best to address gaps in accessibility provision. Officials will continue to work with Executive departments, Deaf and disabled people and their representatives to make sure that the final content of the Disability Strategy and supporting Action Plan is affordable, deliverable and makes a lasting difference. I encourage everyone with an interest and/or an issue to highlight to contribute to the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to (i) issue guidance; or (ii) provide funding, to councils to support the delivery of accessibility measures under the Disability Strategy, including access infrastructure and the publication of accessibility information.
answer_text: The Executive will agree the details of the final content of the Strategy, including any guidance needed and the funding required for delivery, in the light of input received through the current public consultation. All departments, including the Department for Communities, will be required to ensure that any actions they contribute to the Strategy are adequately resourced and that the final content of the Strategy is affordable and deliverable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department will take to ensure that the Action Plan accompanying the Northern Ireland Executive Disability Strategy 2025–2035 includes specific, time-bound and measurable actions to address barriers faced by disabled people in accessing transport, the built environment and public spaces.
answer_text: My officials have engaged extensively with Deaf and disabled people and their representative organisations to identify and address the issues and barriers faced by disabled people, including those in accessing transport, the built environment and public spaces. This co-design work, alongside the feedback received during the current consultation, will inform work with the full range of departments to ensure that the Disability Strategy action plan will have clear delivery milestones and targets, putting actions in place to ensure that the Executive is delivering.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions planned within the Disability Strategy Action Plan to improve access to cultural, leisure and recreational facilities for people with disabilities, including beaches and outdoor public spaces.
answer_text: My officials have engaged extensively with Deaf and disabled people and their representative organisations to identify and address the issues and barriers faced by disabled people, including those around access to cultural, leisure and recreational facilities for people with disabilities. This co-design work, alongside the feedback received during the current consultation, will inform work with the full range of departments to ensure that the Disability Strategy action plan will have clear actions in place to ensure that the Executive is delivering effectively.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following reports in the Belfast Telegraph on Tuesday 6 January 2026, to detail (i) the reason for the meeting which took place between the Chief Constable, the First Minister, and the deputy First Minister; (ii) where the meeting took place; (iii) the exact date of the meeting in January 2025; and (iv) whether the Executive Office will release records of the meeting.
answer_text: The First Minister and deputy First Minister hold regular meetings with the Chief Constable. Ministerial meetings are published on a regular basis via the Open Data NI website. A record of the meeting was released under FoI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to his response to the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30, for his assessment of the likelihood of redundancies within the teaching workforce.
answer_text: As previously stated, the proposed budget would force the unimaginable step of making around 6,700 teachers and 3,100 non-teaching staff redundant in order to achieve savings of £650 million for 2026-27. This would require redundancy funding of approximately £510 million, which is not available. Such action would have catastrophic consequences for our education system.The proposed budget is simply undeliverable.Education has faced historic underfunding for many years, with an inadequate baseline that has only been sustained through in-year allocations. This structural weakness cannot be ignored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether faith-based organisations will continue to have a role in the delivery of Relationship and Sexuality Education in school settings.
answer_text: Schools can engage with a range of organisations, including faith-based organisations to support pupils’ learning. All grant-aided schools must have a Relationship and Sexuality Education (RSE) policy, developed in consultation with parents and pupils, and it is for the Board of Governors to ensure that a comprehensive programme is in place which meets the needs of its pupils and reflects that policy.  Where a school chooses to use an external provider, including to deliver any aspect of RSE, the content provided must be consistent with the school’s own RSE policy, safeguarding requirements and aligned to the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update regarding pay negotiations with barristers.
answer_text: It is important to note that members of the Criminal Bar Association are not employees of the Department involved in pay negotiations but rather are self-employed individuals.  Legal aid is provided for the benefit of the citizens being represented at court, who might otherwise not be able to afford to access justice. A sixteen percent uplift in criminal legal aid fees was recommended by His Honour Judge Burgess following his Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid. That uplift, backdated to 1 December 2024, and applied to all legal aid fees, has taken effect in recent weeks at cost of £6.15m; £1.8m of which went directly to Barristers. Judge Burgess was clear that he had evidence to support only that 16 percent uplift and that further evidence would be required to support further fee changes. A Working Group, under the direction of the Judge, was established in March 2025 to develop the necessary evidence base. That evidence is critical as expenditure from public funds cannot to subject to negotiation but must instead be based on robust data that can withstand public scrutiny. Work has been ongoing and has been fruitful where evidence has been forthcoming, allowing, for example, additional changes to be introduced to PACE and parole fees. The work of the Group continues, and I met with new Chair and Vice Chair of the of the CBA again on 15 January 2026 and proposed a means of navigating the current impasse which builds on work done to date. I believe my proposed approach will both allow the concerns of the CBA to be addressed at pace and fulfil our requirements in terms of appropriately managing public money. Crucially, the proposed approach will also involve the Solicitors Criminal Bar Association, who have remained engaged and committed to the agreed process and who have continued to deliver services to their clients throughout. As the proposed approach is a matter of ongoing discussion, it would not be appropriate to comment further at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any contingency plans in place if a multi-year budget cannot be agreed by the Executive.
answer_text: In the absence of an agreed multi-year budget by the Executive, I will set an Interim Resource Budget.  This will provide a clear planning framework for schools, the Education Authority, my Department’s other Non Departmental Public Bodies and relevant third-party organisations to the end of the current Academic Year. My Department will monitor expenditure closely and make adjustments as necessary within the constraints of available funding when an Executive agreed budget is in place.  This approach would ensure that essential services can continue without disruption while awaiting confirmation of a final budget allocation.The draft multi-year budget for Northern Ireland for the four-year period from 1 April 2026 to 31 March 2030 is now out for public consultation, albeit without the draft budget first being agreed by the Executive. Readers will note that it does not provide the full context against which the draft multi-year budget is to be set.  The consultation only details the proposed departmental allocations, with no reference to the quantum of inescapable bids that Executive departments submitted to the Department of Finance (DoF) to inform the development of the draft multi-year budget.  In order for my Department to live within its proposed budget allocation, I would need to make savings of c£0.9 bn in 2026-27, c£1.01bn in 2027-28 and c£1.15bn in 2028-29. These are simply not practically deliverable.While acknowledging the significant challenges involved in setting a budget, if one were prepared to accept the devastating cuts implicit in the draft budget, as a practical matter, such a budget would be undeliverable.  By not making this point clear, the Minister for Finance is effectively seeking consultation input from stakeholders who will know less than half the story.This House must know that the current consultation exercise is wholly meaningless without a detailed comparison of the proposed allocations against the requirements departments submitted to DoF.Education continues to face unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations that the proposed budget does not fully address.Given that c82% of the education budget is expended on staffing costs, there is no reasonable prospect of my Department being able to live within the proposed budget in the short-term without mass compulsory redundancies which would devastate educational outcomes and undermine the very fabric of our system, as well as requiring dedicated in-year redundancy funding of a similar level to the necessary savings.If this budget was to proceed, as well as having to implement significant compulsory redundancies, I would be left with no choice but to cut major schemes that families and communities rely on, including home-to-school transport, free school meals, school uniform grants, youth services and extended schools. The Northern Ireland Childcare Scheme would also become unsustainable. This stark reali

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given that the Police Ombudsman left office on 31 December 2025, to detail (i) whether any of the statutory functions of the Police Ombudsman can be delegated to another person within the organisation; (ii) any legal authority that is currently relied upon for the continuation of investigative activity by the organisation; and (iii) whether legal advice has been sought in relation to these arrangements.
answer_text: The Office of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland (OPONI) was established under the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1989.  Schedule 3, paragraph 9 of the Act, provides that “Any functions of the Ombudsman under this Act may be performed by any officer of the Ombudsman authorised for the purpose by the Ombudsman.”  Following legal advice, the CEO of the OPONI determined that the Office can continue to function during the period of vacancy under a scheme of delegation.  Details of the scheme can be found on the Police Ombudsman’s website:  https://www.policeombudsman.org My Department engages with OPONI on matters of governance and resourcing.  However, OPONI has independence as to how they exercise their statutory functions.  I would suggest that any further detail on this matter should be referred to the Chief Executive of OPONI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the governance, oversight and departmental accountability arrangements for Shared Environmental Services in Northern Ireland, including which department holds responsibility for its remit, funding, and performance in relation to environmental planning advice provided to councils.
answer_text: No Northern Ireland Executive department has direct responsibility for the operation or performance of Shared Environmental Services (SES), including its role in providing environmental planning advice to councils.SES is a local government shared service, established to facilitate a number of Northern Ireland district councils. Accountability for the governance, oversight, funding and performance of SES rests with the participating councils. SES is based in Mid and East Antrim Council.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all gritting treatments that took place on the A26, specifically the Crankill Road and Frosses Road sections, between 3 January 2026 and 5 January 2026, including (i) times; (ii) locations; and (iii) the type of treatment applied.
answer_text: The A26 Frosses Road and A26 Crankill Road form part of my Department’s salted network and I can confirm that they were, as well as the rest of the salted network, treated many times during the recent cold spell.  A list of the gritting action commencement times and subsequent treatments, over the requested dates, are detailed below. ActionSaturday3 JanuarySunday4 JanuaryMonday5 January20g rock salt gritting action01:0020g rock salt (boosting to 30g where required)04:3018:0001:3005:0013:0019:0001:0005:0018:30In relation to location, I can advise that the Frosses Road and Crankill Road are on the same route with one direction treated from Ballykeel depot, Ballymena and the other from Northbrook depot, Coleraine.  The commencement time for each gritting action on these dates were the same for both depots.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale of why gritting treatment on the A26, specifically the Crankill Road and Frosses Road sections, has been less frequent during this recent winter period of from 3 January 2026 to 5 January 2026, compared with previous years.
answer_text: The A26 Frosses Road and A26 Crankill Road form part of my Department’s salted network and I can confirm that, along with the rest of the salted network, they were treated many times during the recent cold spell.The frequency of gritting actions is ultimately determined through the analysis of special winter transport weather forecasts which are variable year on year.It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there are any plans or proposals for the disposal of the former Northern Regional College site in Ballymena.
answer_text: Northern Regional College’s new Ballymena campus combined provision from the College’s three previous locations in the town onto the larger Farm Lodge site. Former premises at Trostan Avenue and the Lamont Building are now surplus to college requirements.The College has advised that it is engaging with the Department of Finance’s Land and Property Services to progress the disposal of the Trostan Avenue campus under the D1 public sector disposal process.  The Lamont Building is currently being leased by Mid & East Antrim Borough Council for its use, and any subsequent disposal of this asset will also be completed through the D1 process in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when the Racial Equality Strategy will be signed off.
answer_text: We are currently considering a draft Framework for Race Relations and initial delivery plan. We plan to launch a public consultation early this year with a view to bringing a final Framework to the Executive for consideration shortly thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department has taken to ensure that all roads, included in the winter service plan, are gritted as required during the current period of cold weather, given reports that some routes have not been treated.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to its winter service programme and every night, from the end of October, up to 300 people will be on standby ready to salt main roads, helping drivers across the north to cope with the wintry conditions. Contracts are also in place to enable contractors and farmers to help to clear roads during periods of prolonged snow. The current winter service policy and procedures operated by my Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes and can be viewed on the Department’s website below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed. Route treatment is primarily based on traffic volume not road classification.My Department is not aware of any roads on the Primary Salting Network which were not treated over this recent period of cold weather, however, officials would be happy to investigate if the Member provides details of specific routes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the steps he has taken to ensure that GPs prescribe the most cost-effective brand when prescribing relevant medication to patients.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether her Department’s current winter service arrangements rely on staff undertaking overtime to carry out gritting duties; and (ii) any contingency measures in place where overtime is unavailable.
answer_text: The Department delivers a winter service programme which endeavours to mitigate, as far as is reasonably possible, the effects of adverse winter weather. At times of ice and snow we deploy around 300 staff and a fleet of 130 gritters to salt around 7,000 kilometres of the main road network. The winter service is not provided by overtime, rather by staff who volunteer to be placed on shift rotas, undertaking roles including driving, supervision and decision making on gritting actions.  Staff working on a shift rota are paid both a standby allowance and a set hourly rate when actions are called.Contingency rotas are in place should staff on the main gritting rotas become unavailable.  The contingency rota is also made up of staff who volunteer to be contacted on a catch as you can basis in exceptional circumstances.Both the winter service gritting and catch as you can rotas are supplemented by agency drivers where required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the five taxi operators that received the most money from the Education Authority for delivering the Home to School Transport Scheme 2023-24, broken down by how much each operator received.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that the five private hire taxi operators receiving the highest payments for home to school transport contracts in 2023–24 were Lockview Transport, Enterprise Taxis, Marshall Taxis, Ace Cabs & Couriers, and Brian Coulter Transport. The EA has advised that while it holds detailed payment information for each of these operators, it is unable to release this data as disclosure could adversely affect the commercial interests of the parties involved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current level of grit stocks held by his Department; and (ii) any actions taken to replenish or augment those stocks, during the current winter period.
answer_text: As the delivery of Winter Service is ongoing, the grit levels are constantly fluctuating with use and replenishment, so it would not be possible to give an accurate level of current stocks held.I can confirm that our salt reserves were filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of Winter Service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required. Re-orders are set at 90% of full capacity to ensure stock levels are maintained.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the School Uniform Guidelines have been revised to create a legal right for a pupil to wear a coat of their choice to and from school.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any changes have been made by her Department to gritting (i) routes; (ii) treatment frequencies; or (iii) priority levels, during the current winter period.
answer_text: The Department delivers a winter service programme which endeavours to mitigate, as far as is reasonably possible, the effects of adverse winter weather.As part of the winter service, the Department salts the main through routes which carry more than 1,500 vehicles per day. In exceptional circumstances, where no alternative or parallel routes are available, roads with difficult topography carrying between 1,000 and 1,500 vehicles daily will also be salted. This means that 28% of the road network will be salted, directly benefiting around 80% of road users.During periods of prolonged adverse weather, decisions on both the level and frequency of gritting actions are taken by experienced engineers, after consideration of available information and Met Office forecasts.Prior to the commencement of a Winter Service season, requests to have a road considered for inclusion on the salted network are reviewed and assessed as part of Winter Service Provision RSPPG EO22.It must, however, be emphasised that despite our efforts, we cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey. During this recent cold spell, staff across all our roads divisions have been working tirelessly day and night, in some very difficult conditions, to keep traffic moving safely. We owe a huge debt of gratitude to our gritter drivers and snow blowers, who are often the first traffic on many roads, leaving their own families behind to ensure we can travel safely. Their hard work and persistence ensured tangible results for our road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current average waiting time for red flag referral mammogram appointments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how this compares to the recommended clinical target.
answer_text: While your questions refer to ‘red flag referral mammogram,’ we have interpreted this to mean symptomatic breast assessment appointments, and our response has been framed accordingly.(i) The most recent Accredited Official Statistics on median waiting times are available for the quarter ending June 2025.  Please refer to Table 5 in the ‘Patients seen following an urgent breast cancer referral’ section of the Cancer Waiting Times bulletin published on 2 October 2025:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesPlease note that Table 5 excludes Southern or Western HSC Trusts, as validated data for these Trusts were not available at the time of publication due to Encompass implementation. As the regional breast cancer waiting list was launched on 8 May 2025, breakdowns by individual Trust are no longer applicable and are not provided. The next quarterly publication of official statistics for quarter ending September 2025 is scheduled for 15 January 2026. (ii)  There is no recommended clinical target for red flag mammogram appointments, however there is an operational performance target set by my Department which states that 100% of patients should be seen by a Breast Cancer Specialist within 14 days following an Urgent Referral. As noted above the current 14-day performance is unacceptable, response to your AQW 36975 outlines what is being done to address this.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, in relation to official portraits of Speakers of the Northern Ireland Assembly, whether any consideration has been given to using more cost-effective alternatives such as a professional photograph.
answer_text: Since the Northern Ireland Assembly was established in 1998, the Assembly Commission has commissioned portraits of all those who have formerly held office as Speaker, First Minister and deputy First Minister as the senior offices of the Assembly and the Executive respectively.The Assembly Commission agreed a formal policy in 2021 to capture its previous decisions on official portraits and reviewed this policy in 2025.  When reviewing this policy in 2025, the Assembly Commission agreed to remove the option for portrait subjects to choose to have a photograph produced rather than a painted portrait.In 2021, the Assembly Commission agreed that official portraits would be displayed within the ground floor and first floor galleries of the Great Hall.  As Parliament Buildings is itself an iconic and historic building, the Assembly Commission is mindful of the need to ensure that the artwork it commissions for display is of a quality in keeping with the significant status of the building.   Following experience when subjects explored the option of photography, the Assembly Commission agreed that painted portraits were more suitable for the environment of the Great Hall.The process of commissioning official portraits is undertaken in line with a maximum negotiating budget agreed by the Assembly Commission each mandate.   If the commissioning of a particular preferred artist cannot be secured within that limit, the subject is asked to indicate another artist.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department holds a record of any communications via WhatsApp or other encrypted messaging platforms between its Ministers and the Police Ombudsman since 22 September 2023.
answer_text: The Executive Office holds no records of any such communications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the recent increase in the National Minimum Wage, to detail the steps that will be taken to avoid a disproportionate hike in fees by childcare providers who are registered with the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: My Department does not regulate childcare fees or have the authority to impose direct price controls on a sector largely made up of private providers.   I am aware that the issue of capping fees alongside the NICSS has been called for by some, and I have carefully considered this as part of the draft Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy.  However, I am not proposing this approach at this stage, as to do so would require substantial public investment, which is unaffordable in the current climate, as would carry significant regulatory and operational implications.The draft ELC Strategy, now out for consultation, sets out actions to help ensure that providers do not increase their fees disproportionately, this includes requiring providers to publish their fees, something which is not currently consistently done and the introduction of a Childcare Business Advisory Service to help providers manage cost pressures and operate efficiently.My department will closely monitor fees as part of the NICSS to ensure the benefits continue to be passed on to parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has assessed whether increases in landfill tax could lead to a rise in illegal waste disposal or fly-tipping.
answer_text: The Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) of NIEA continues to investigate and prosecute the most serious and persistent waste criminality in Northern Ireland. 		The ECU focuses its resources on those cases which present the most significant risk to the environment and public health.  ECU utilises powers contained within the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to pursue enforcement around illegal depositing of waste and where appropriate, progress criminal prosecutions against those responsible. Such prosecutions are often coupled with confiscation proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, aimed at depriving those convicted of the proceeds generated from their crimes whilst asserting the ‘Polluter Pays Principle’. 		Individuals who illegally depose of waste, such as through fly tipping, often do so to avoid lawful disposal costs. The Environmental Crime Unit acknowledges that increases in landfill tax makes the legal disposal of controlled waste more expensive. Consequently, this rise in cost may heighten the risk of illegal waste depositing or fly tipping, as some may seek to circumvent these financial charges. The agency will continue to address this threat through its ongoing collaboration with law enforcement partners.		The NIEA continues to work in close partnership with local councils to address fly tipping through a working protocol. Both the NIEA and District Councils have access to various provisions under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, enabling them to undertake enforcement action where appropriate.	NIEA implements a multi-agency approach, promoting coordinated and proactive engagement with a number of partner agencies. By working together with partner agencies, the NIEA takes a comprehensive approach in tackling illegal waste criminality through effective inter-agency collaboration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether all local councils pay Landfill Tax, either directly or indirectly, through contracted landfill operators.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information, as the payment of landfill tax is a matter for His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs (HMRC).

answer_text: On 10 December I pre-empted the announcement in error and publicly corrected that statement the same day.However, I am pleased to confirm that funding was secured, as anticipated, on 17 December for funding the PSNI data breach claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the risk that bluetongue-infected midges may be transported into Northern Ireland within imported straw; and (ii) whether his Department will consider temporarily suspending the importation of straw from bluetongue-affected regions as a precautionary measure.
answer_text: DAERA considers that straw presents a very low risk of bringing midges into Northern Ireland due to its nature and where we import from.  		Midges are not attracted to straw, and cereal fields do not have high populations of midges.  My Department therefore has no plans to ban the import of straw.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of occasions on which a controlled school principal's request to expel a pupil has been denied by the Education Authority's expulsion panel, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: To clarify, under the Education Authority’s (EA) Scheme for Suspensions and Expulsions, principals do not have the authority to request the expulsion of a pupil. Instead, the Scheme sets out arrangements whereby the Board of Governors of a controlled school may agree to write to EA to recommend the expulsion of a pupil from a controlled school. Upon receiving such a recommendation, the EA convenes an Expulsion Committee to consider the case and make a determination.Taking this clarification into account, the table below sets out the number of occasions in each of the last five years where a controlled school’s Board of Governors recommended the expulsion of a pupil, but the Education Authority’s Expulsion Committee did not approve the recommendation.School YearNumber of pupils for whom a controlled school’s Board of Governors recommended expulsion, but whose expulsion was not approved by the Education Authority’s Expulsion Committee2020/21 02021/22 22022/23 22023/24 22024/25 12025/2026 – (to date) 0Further details on the process, including a copy of the Scheme, can be accessed on the EA website: EWS - Suspensions & Expulsions | Education Authority Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the procurement process for commissioning official portraits of Speakers of the Northern Ireland Assembly, including (i) whether competitive tendering is undertaken; and (ii) the criteria used to select artists.
answer_text: Since the Northern Ireland Assembly was established in 1998, the Assembly Commission has commissioned portraits of all those who have formerly held office as Speaker, First Minister and deputy First Minister as the senior offices of the Assembly and the Executive respectively.  The Assembly Commission agreed a formal policy in 2021 to capture its previous decisions on official portraits and reviewed this policy in 2025. The commissioning of official portraits has always been undertaken using adirect award procurement pathway.  Procurement by direct award for the commissioning of official portraits is in accordance with Schedule 5 (4) of the UK Procurement Act 2023 which confirms that a direct award can be made where:‘The public contract concerns the creation or acquisition of a unique work of art or artistic performance.’The subject is asked to identify a preferred artist and is provided as a starting point with details of known artists as suggested by the National Museums NI and the Arts Council, as well as artists who have previously produced portraits for the Assembly Commission.  Assembly officials then seek to secure the services of the preferred artist within a negotiating budget agreed by the Assembly Commission for portraits within each mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why MRI scanning is not available at Antrim Area Hospital during weekends; and (ii) whether there are any plans to introduce weekend MRI provision at that site.
answer_text: MRI scanning is currently only available in Antrim Area Hospital one day each weekend. question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the expenditure incurred on official portraits of Speakers of the Northern Ireland Assembly since 1998, broken down by the cost of each individual portrait.
answer_text: Since the Northern Ireland Assembly was established in 1998, the Assembly Commission has commissioned portraits of all those who have formerly held office as Speaker, First Minister and deputy First Minister as the senior offices of the Assembly and the Executive respectively.By marking the contribution of former senior office holders, the Assembly Commission’s policy on the commissioning of official portraits seeks to: (i) 	Record the history of the Assembly within Parliament Buildings(ii) 	Contribute to the Assembly Commission’s Good Relations obligations by reflecting different political traditions represented in the Assembly(iii) 	Add to the experience of visitors and the Assembly Commission’s display of items and images related to the parliamentary history of the building.In addition, the production of official portraits also provides an opportunity for the talent of local artists to be demonstrated and highlighted within Parliament Buildings.It should be noted that the commissioning of portraits was paused for a period of approximately ten years.  This was because it became increasingly difficult to commission portraits within the budget originally permitted for each portrait and the Assembly Commission decided not to produce any portraits during the time when the Assembly wasn’t sitting between 2017 and 2020. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any additional (i) funding; or (ii) staffing, allocated to radiology departments to reduce delays in red flag referral mammogram appointments.
answer_text: While your questions refer to ‘red flag referral mammogram,’ we have interpreted this to mean symptomatic breast assessment appointments, and our response has been framed accordingly. With regard to non-recurrent funding and additional money set aside for red flag assessment, including mammography, it is not possible to specify what proportion of the funding was utilised specifically for mammography funding or staffing alone. Patients attending the fast lane breast assessment clinics may have mammography, ultrasound or a combination of both. This level of detail would be held by the Trusts.  However, see response to AQW 36975 below which outlines additional investment into breast care services to improve red flag breast assessment capacity. The most recent Accredited Official Statistics on median waiting times are available for the quarter ending June 2025.  Please refer to Table 5 in the ‘Patients seen following an urgent breast cancer referral’ section of the Cancer Waiting Times bulletin published on 2 October 2025:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesPlease note that Table 5 excludes Southern or Western HSC Trusts, as validated data for these Trusts were not available at the time of publication due to Encompass implementation. As the regional breast cancer waiting list was launched on 8 May 2025, breakdowns by individual Trust are no longer applicable and are not  provided. The next quarterly publication of official statistics for quarter ending September 2025 is scheduled for 15 January 2026. There is no recommended clinical target for red flag mammogram appointments, however there is an operational performance target set by my department which states that 100% of patients should be seen by a Breast Cancer Specialist within 14 days following an Urgent Referral. As noted above the current 14-day performance is unacceptable, response to AQW 36975 below outlines what is being done to address this.In September 2024 in response to regional disparities in waiting times I announced the development of a single regional waiting list for breast cancer assessments.  This system became operational on 8 May 2025 and was designed to ensure that all patients, regardless of their home Trust, are offered the earliest available appointment across the region. While this regional approach promotes fairness and consistency, it does not immediately reduce the overall waiting time and there is a need to build sustainable capacity.   Therefore £5 million recurrent funding has been allocated under the Elective Care Framework (ECF) to support and expand breast services across Health and Social Care Trusts.My Department is working closely with Trusts to ensure Red Flag referrals are prioritised within existing core capacity to ensure timely access for the most clinically urgent cases and in response to immediate pressures, to roll out additional evening and weekend clinics. In October 2025, I introduced a new Waiting List Initiative for Breast Ass

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider including a reference requiring schools to provide facilities for pupils to store or hang their coats during the school day in the School Uniform Guidelines.
answer_text: I have previously stated my view that I consider it is important our young people can get to school and be comfortable in their uniform, including in relation to schools permitting overcoats.  I have instructed my officials to update the reference in paragraph 45 of the School Uniforms Guidelines to ensure that any concerns about the clarity of wording is addressed.  The guidelines will therefore state that pupils are to be permitted to wear a non-branded coat – to be clear this was always the intention of the guidelines. In relation to storage, my answer to AQW 35388/22-27 explained that the Department’s School Building Handbook sets out relevant provisions about cloakrooms and storage for schools.  This remains the appropriate basis for addressing such matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider amending the School Uniform Guidelines to state that all schools must permit pupils to wear a generic, non branded coat to school.
answer_text: I have previously stated my view that I consider it is important our young people can get to school and be comfortable in their uniform, including in relation to schools permitting overcoats. This remains the appropriate basis for addressing such matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the cattle in the herd associated with the recent bluetongue case had received a bluetongue vaccine.
answer_text: Cattle in the herd affected with Bluetongue (BTV-3) were not vaccinated for BTV-3.  It should be noted that it is not mandatory to have your livestock vaccinated for BTV-3, it is a decision for the livestock keeper to make in conjunction with advice from their private veterinarian.  		It should also be noted that BTV vaccines do not provide cross protection for other serotypes of the virus.  For further information, including what Bluetongue is and helpful information on BTV-3 vaccination please visit: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) why the Chief Veterinary Officer has not been the principal spokesperson for his Department in relation to the current bluetongue outbreak; and (ii) any steps being taken to ensure clear and authoritative communication with the farming community.
answer_text: The Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) remains the senior official responsible for animal health and my Department’s response to this recent incursion of Bluetongue (BTV). Given the significant range of urgent animal health priorities at present including BTV, Avian Influenza, and Bovine tuberculosis (bTB), it is appropriate that the Deputy Chief Veterinary Officer is made available to support the Department in media and Committee briefings. My officials have taken forward detailed communications with all stakeholders and the wider industry regarding this situation and will continue to do so.  In addition, they remain in close contact with counterparts in the Republic of Ireland and the rest of the United Kingdom as next steps are considered
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the available scientific evidence regarding the safety and efficacy of bluetongue vaccines in (a) pregnant sheep; and (b) other livestock; and (ii) any advice been issued to veterinary practitioners on their use.
answer_text: Since July 2025, vaccination for Bluetongue serotype 3 (BTV-3) can be used in Northern Ireland and there are currently three BTV-3 vaccines available.  It is not mandatory to have your livestock vaccinated for BTV-3; it is a decision for the livestock keeper to make in conjunction with advice from their private veterinarian.  It should be noted that BTV vaccines do not provide cross protection for other serotypes of the virus.  Available scientific evidence on current available BTV-3 vaccines suggests that they are intended to reduce the severity of clinical signs and mortality in affected animals, though they do not prevent transmission of the virus.Information that has been shared with private veterinary practitioners and livestock keepers in Northern Ireland can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/bluetongue-btv-3-vaccination-further-information For further information, including what Bluetongue is and helpful information on BTV-3 vaccination please visit: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongueDevelopments on the Bluetongue situation in Northern Ireland will be provided at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether the 44 cattle reported on 3 December 2025 as having bluetongue antibodies all originate from the same herd as the index case; and (ii) any assessment his Department has made of whether vaccination may account for these antibody detections.
answer_text: I can confirm that the 44 cattle reported on 3 December 2025 were from the same herd.  They were not vaccinated for Bluetongue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to provide reassurance to the farming community in relation to his Department’s handling of the bluetongue outbreak, given the significant concerns expressed by livestock farmers.
answer_text: The oral statement I made in the Assembly on Monday 01 December 2025, of which you will have received a copy, details the actions my department have taken with respect to responding to this suspect case of Bluetongue.  Furthermore, it provides detail on next steps that are being progressed.	In addition, I attended the Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (AERA) Committee on Thursday 4 December 2025 where I was also able to provide an update on actions taken.  A recording of this session can be found at: https://niassembly.tv/committee-for-agriculture-environment-and-rural-affairs-meeting-thursday-3-december-2025/ 	You will be aware that this is an evolving picture, including further developments as per the press release issued by my department on Saturday 6 December 2025 regarding a further suspect case of Bluetongue under investigation. This is available here: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/news/new-suspected-case-bluetongue-virus-under-investigationUpdates to this developing situation will be made available on the DAERA website at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-irelandMy officials have taken forward extensive communications with stakeholders regarding this situation and will continue to do so.  In addition, they are in close contact with their counterparts in the Republic of Ireland and the rest of the United Kingdom as next steps are considered.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether there will be any transitional arrangements to mitigate financial hardship for property owners, who are actively engaging with planning authorities, or investors, to bring vacant premises back into use.
answer_text: In my statement of the 18 November, I stressed the need for a sufficient lead in time for any change to allow owners to respond to this future shift in policy direction. Likewise, I see this as a key policy step to address the blight of vacant units which also has the potential to unlock a further £20 million of vital revenue between central and local government.If the member has any evidence of ‘financial hardship for property owners’ he has the opportunity to bring it forward during the consultation period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the impact of raising vacant property rates, when many property owners already face significant planning, infrastructure, or market barriers, preventing redevelopment or reoccupation.
answer_text: In my view work now needs to be undertaken to move the Non-Domestic Vacant Rate from 50% to 75% and then to 100%  to tackle the high prevalence of vacant units. If fully implemented it is estimated that those changes would have the potential to unlock a further £20 million of revenue to fund central and local government spending.As I noted in my Statement of 18 November, acknowledging the views expressed during the Strategic Review of this area, there will be a need to provide a sufficient lead in time for any change to allow owners to respond to this future shift in policy direction

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether it is his intention to increase the non-domestic vacant rating from 50  per cent to (i) 75 per cent before the end of the current Assembly mandate in 2027; and subsequently to (ii) 100 per cent, during the next mandate.
answer_text: I have instructed my officials to take forward policy work to secure Executive agreement on this area. It would be my preference on implementation timings to secure an increase from 50% to 75% liability for such properties prior to the end of this Assembly mandate, and a further increase to 100% at the beginning of the next mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current position of the Shared Environmental Services in relation to outstanding agricultural planning applications within the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council, including any actions being taken to reduce processing times and move forward outstanding applications.
answer_text: The Shared Environmental Services falls outside my department’s area of responsibility.  It is based in Mid and East Antrim Council offices and can be contacted by emailing SES@midandeastantrim.gov.uk .
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when the reference that pupils should be able to wear non-branded coats to school was added to the School Uniform Guidelines.
answer_text: The requirement for school uniform policies to enable pupils to wear non-branded outdoor coats has been included since the first draft of the School Uniforms Guidelines was produced.  As you know, these guidelines will become statutory once the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill comes into operation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact of undeveloped, zoned housing land, including land affected by fragmented ownership, limited access or unrealistic valuations, has on the delivery of housing, particularly social housing, within the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough.
answer_text: It is a matter for local councils, working closely with the Housing Executive Place Shaping Teams, to address locally specific issues that may impact on housing requirements in an area. This includes through the delivery of Local Development Plans, which are responsible for establishing the zonings and policies to ensure the spatial growth requirements of the local area can be met.   More widely, the Department for Infrastructure has a role in the NI planning system including relevant legislation, regional policies and strategies and supporting practical guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with housing providers, and developers, regarding barriers to progressing approved housing applications, particularly in areas with acute social housing need.
answer_text: As part of the Housing Supply Strategy and to inform the Action Planning process, my Department has maintained continuous engagement with housing providers, including Housing Associations, developers, and the wider construction sector. These discussions have focused on identifying and overcoming key barriers to deliver housing supply in all areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether the Police Ombudsman’s national security vetting has been reviewed in the last 12 months: and (ii) whether the office holder still has access to material classified as Top Secret.
answer_text: The Police Ombudsman is independent and appointed, as a Corporation Sole, by HM The King on the recommendation of the First and deputy First Minsters, acting jointly. Neither I, as Minister, nor my Department, have any role in the Police Ombudsman’s national security vetting status or the level of papers to which she has access. The terms under which the Ombudsman is appointed and carries out the functions of the office have been made abundantly clear on several occasions. Accordingly, you may wish to contact TEO or OPONI for further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any (i) steps his Department is taking to address delays in the processing of agricultural planning applications; and (ii) assessment made of the impact of these delays on farm development and animal welfare.
answer_text: To address delays in the processing of planning applications the Northern Ireland Environment Agency is developing and implementing a Planning Improvement Plan. Officials are aware of some delays associated with a number of specific agricultural cases and are engaging with relevant authorities to try to seek a resolution to these matters.The Department has not undertaken an assessment of the impact of delays in the planning system on farm development or animal welfare.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has considered reviewing, or amending, the zoning of housing land in areas where development is unlikely to proceed, to enable more realistic planning for social housing.
answer_text: Under the two-tier system framework established by the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, responsibility for preparing Local Development Plans (LDPs) rests with district councils as local planning authorities. Decisions in relation to the housing need and the quantity and location of zoned housing land, are therefore the responsibility of Councils when preparing a LDP for their area. The preparation of a LDP also provides an opportunity to review existing zoned land and consider whether this should be brought forward as part of the LPP and if new sites will be required.Six Plan Strategy (PS) documents have been adopted to date with a further three published in draft form. Councils with adopted PS documents in place are currently working toward publishing Local Policy Plans (LPP) that will designate land for various uses, including housing and/or affordable housing. All local development plan documents, including LPP, are prepared with input from statutory consultation bodies, including Government Departments and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. They are also subject to public consultation and Independent Examination by the Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) to determine if the requirements of the legislation have been met and the plan is ‘Sound’. One of the twelve tests of Soundness is that “The strategy, policies and allocations are realistic and appropriate, having considered the relevant alternatives and are founded on a robust evidence base”.  The Department must consider the recommendations of the PAC and direct the council to adopt a development plan document as prepared or with modifications. The statutory review/revision process established through the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 includes a requirement for a council to review their adopted LDP at least every five years. These provisions provide a mechanism for addressing emerging issues, such as affordable housing land supply, to ensure the plan remains realistic and deliverable. In the event a council consider a plan revision is necessary, this shall follow the same process as LDP preparation, including public consultation and Independent Examination.My department will continue to work with councils to ensure that LDPs provide an adequate and deliverable supply of housing land, taking account of Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) objectives, including prioritising the use of previously developed and undeveloped land within the urban footprint and reduced use of greenfield land.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps is he taking to ensure that beef and chicken imported from South America under the EU Mercosur Deal does not lead to lower food safety standards.
answer_text: The terms of any free trade agreement between the EU and Mercosur are a matter for the concerned parties to agree.  There is no indication that the EU is preparing to change or lower its food safety standards.  In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the import of animal products into Northern Ireland is under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
