question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs,whether he will consider a full review of animal welfare in prisons.
answer_text: No. I am satisfied that the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 provides strong protections for animals across all settings, including prisons. Any animal welfare concerns should be reported promptly to the relevant statutory agency for investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the villages in North Antrim that have no wastewater capacity; and (ii) to detail when NI Water first became aware of this issue.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The villages in North Antrim serving a population >250 PE (population equivalent) that have no wastewater capacity, and how long that has been the case, are shown in the table below:Village PE >250 PE No wastewater capacity since: Armoy2018Dervock2016Mosside2018Stranocum2021For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any (i) legal; or (ii) policy, distinction that exists between prison officers and other civil servants, especially in relation to sick pay and pension entitlements.
answer_text: Prison officers are subject to the same terms and conditions of employment as all other civil servants. These are set out in their letters of appointment, the Staff Handbook and the Code of Ethics. In relation to pension entitlements, under current pension scheme rules i.e. the 'alpha' pension scheme, prison officers are treated in the same way as other eligible civil servants. Civil Servants who were appointed to the Civil Service here prior to the introduction of alpha pension scheme may be in one of the legacy pension schemes (classic, classic plus, premium, nuvos). For prison officers in post prior to 3 September 1989, any service held by them in the legacy 'classic' scheme in excess of 20 years reckons for pension at double its length up to the maximum reckonable limit, and the normal pension age for classic pension benefits is 55. These features of the legacy classic pension scheme did not extend to non-prison officer scheme members.Civil Service HR policies are kept under regular review and are subject to consultation with Central Trade Union Side, which includes the POA (Prison Officers’ Association).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether prison officers, employed by the Northern Ireland Prison Service, are formally considered members of the Northern Ireland Civil Service; and (ii) to detail the official employment status of prison officers.
answer_text: Prison officers, employed by the NI Prison Service, are civil servants and therefore members of the Civil Service here.Civil servants, including prison officers, are office holders rather than employees. This is recognised in Article 236 of the Employment Rights (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 which makes provision for “employment under or for the purposes of a government department or any officer or body exercising on behalf of the Crown functions conferred by a statutory provision”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how post-traumatic stress disorder is classified within the health service, including whether it is treated as a mental illness, a disability or both.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients. Decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice. In recent years my Department has made significant investment to enable GPs to improve their telephony systems and employ additional staff to manage their systems efficiently.My Department has issued guidance on completion of fit notes to GPs in 2024. doh-fit-note-guidance-july-2024.pdf & doh-fit-letter.pdf.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 35171/22-27, to detail what percentage of students who graduated were (i) Northern Ireland residents; and (ii) international students, in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to (i) streamline; or (ii) reform, consultation processes to expedite decision-making on local planning applications.
answer_text: I recognise the important role played by statutory consultees in the planning process and my Department has been working closely with them and local planning authorities through the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum (PSCF) to streamline processes and improve performance.Significant progress has been achieved to date. The PSCF has raised the profile and the complexities of the work conducted by statutory consultees; introduced a good practice Operating Principles for Planning Authorities and Consultees’ document.; identified potential over-consultation issues; created the impetus for additional resources being allocated to statutory consultees and has introduced a transparent system whereby statutory consultee performance is monitored on a quarterly and annual basis. The most recent published information shows that 79% of consultation requests were responded to on-time during 2025/26, the best response rate since 2017/18. See links below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/system/files/2025-10/statutory-consultations-annual-performance-report-2024-25_0.pdfhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/system/files/2025-10/planning-consultations-management-information-tables_202425.odsStatutory consultees are only one of many key stakeholders who must play their part in improving the planning system across the board. To this end, my Department has introduced legislation to allow planning authorities to introduce a validation checklist with the aim of improving the quality of applications entering the system thus reducing the amount of time that consultees spend on consultation and re-consultation responses.  My officials have also been conducting analytical work which has highlighted and provided further insights into delays in the planning system many of which are beyond statutory consultee control. Officials have recently met with all local planning authorities to discuss issues and identify process improvements.I will continue to work with colleagues across all sectors to streamline and improve processes around the statutory consultation process and other planning processes to ensure planning decisions are expedited as efficiently as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to reduce delays in responses from infrastructure consultees, including her Department, the Department for Communities and NI Water, during the processing of local planning applications.
answer_text: Statutory consultees play an important role in the planning system and my Department has been working closely with them and local planning authorities via the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum (PSCF) to streamline processes and improve performance. Whilst the performance of individual consultees is primarily a matter for their sponsoring Departments,  all planning statutory consultees including the Department for Communities and NI Water are members of the PSCF, and I would refer the member to my previous response in AQW 36368 22-27 where I outlined the key actions being undertaken through this Forum to improve the performance of all statutory consultees.In terms of the performance for all statutory consultees, my Department monitors and reports on performance and I would refer the member to the Table 4a and 4b in the attached link.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/system/files/2025-10/statutory-consultations-annual-performance-report-2024-25_0.pdfOver the past 3 years, my Department, acting as the Roads Authority statutory consultee, has consistently achieved an 80% response rate to local planning applications consultations within 21 days which is in line with our business plan targets. Officials have been working to streamline and improve the efficiency of planning consultations and have worked with local councils to put in place standing advice on common roads and transport issues that frequently arise in certain low risk types of planning applications. In addition, to maintain and further improve our current level of performance in a challenging resource environment, my Department is currently reviewing our existing delivery mechanisms to identify recommendations that will support the ongoing, sustainable and effective delivery of our responsibilities. Similarly, work is ongoing to address existing vacancies within my Department’s Rivers directorate but the marketplace for engineering staff is extremely competitive, leading to challenges recruiting staff to fill these vacancies.  Steps are however, being taken to mitigate and address performance including some limited overtime working and exploring the potential for external consultant support. I will continue to work with colleagues across all sectors and all statutory consultees to streamline and improve processes around the statutory consultation process to ensure planning decisions are expedited as efficiently as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider adopting a more flexible approach to road gritting during periods of snow and ice, particularly on rural roads, which are not classed as an A road, but are heavily used by vehicles, including school buses.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by my Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes and can be viewed on the Department’s website below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed. Route treatment is primarily based on traffic volume not road classification, with approximately only 9% of the network classed as A road. Once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is assured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. This Secondary Salting Network currently gives priority to 80 rural schools across the North.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any consideration they have given to providing support for traders affected by the closure of the Bann Bridge, Kilrea, given that the Department for Infrastructure, the Department for Communities, and the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs have each indicated that they have no responsibility for offering such support.
answer_text: The Executive Office has no vires to make funds available for this purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the disappearance of 11 koi carp from a pond in Hydebank Wood, whether the NI Prison Service reported the matter to the PSNI for assessment of whether a crime may have been committed.
answer_text: As stated in a recent Assembly Question, following evidence of predator activity at the pond, which is located outside the secure perimeter of the Hydebank Wood College itself and after discussion with relevant Agencies, it is believed that a predator such as a mink, heron or otter may have taken the koi carp, as they have been seen in the area.  On that basis it was not considered appropriate to report the matter to the PSNI, as to do so would represent an egregious waste of police resources whenever the PSNI, and wider justice system, is already facing severe budgetary and capacity pressures. I take my duty to ensure the efficient use of public money with the upmost seriousness and would urge all other Members to do the same.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will review its current policy for revoking a public service vehicle licence for drivers aged 65 and over, who fail to complete an annual medical examination on time, in light of the ongoing driver shortages across the sector.
answer_text: It is a legal requirement that taxi drivers must be medically fit to drive. If a medical questionnaire is not provided at the time of application, the application will be rejected, as the Department cannot determine the applicant’s medical fitness to drive.Given this legal requirement, I have no plans to review the current policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of taxi drivers who have had their public service vehicle licence revoked, in the last three years, where the only outstanding requirement was the annual medical examination.
answer_text: The information you have requested is not available. However, following a change to the DVA’s commercial licensing system in June 2023, figures have been available from that point for all taxi driver licences revoked due to failure to supply a medical report.From 1 June 2023 to 25 November 2025, 99 taxi driver licences were revoked for not providing medical evidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department is giving to introducing greater flexibility for taxi drivers whose public service vehicle licence lapses, due to an outstanding medical assessment, particularly in cases where all other licensing requirements remain valid.
answer_text: It is a legal requirement that a taxi driver must be medically fit to drive. Medical standards for taxi drivers are higher than those required for ordinary car drivers.  All new applicants and those renewing who are aged 45 or over, are required to have a medical form completed by their GP to assess that they meet these standards.  Flexibility is already built into the process as initial reminders are issued 16 weeks in advance, with subsequent reminders during this period, to ensure that there is sufficient time for applicants to send the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) the required information, and for DVA to complete mandatory checks. Given this legal requirement, I have no plans to review the current policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the Supreme Court judgement in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant), to outline any action he will take to protect the Christian ethos of schools and ensure that every child, who wishes to, continues to receive instruction rooted in the Christian faith.
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 has significant implications for our schools. I will consider the judgment in full before determining any essential remedies that both respect the law and safeguard the role of religion in education. However, it is vital to underline that the Court did not strike down the existing legislation. Both Religious Education and collective worship can continue in schools.  Indeed, there is a legal obligation that they must continue. Article 21(2) Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 makes this clear: controlled schools must provide undenominational Religious Education based on the Holy Scriptures and collective worship that is not distinctive of any denomination. These statutory duties remain unchanged.The case is not about whether Christianity should be the main or primary faith that pupils learn about in schools in Northern Ireland. Christianity has historically been, and continues to be, the main religion in Northern Ireland. Furthermore, the Court has explained that “it is within the Department’s margin of appreciation in planning and setting the curriculum for the greater part of religious education to focus on knowledge of Christianity”.My department is already reviewing the overall school curriculum for the first time in almost 20 years; it is therefore timely to reform the RE syllabus. Under the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006, the Department of Education is responsible for the RE Core Syllabus.  The order states that the syllabus should be prepared by “a group of persons appearing to the Department to be people who have an interest in the teaching of religious education in grant-aided schools.”  I will set out the process for reforming the RE syllabus in due course.  The area of collective worship requires further consideration. While parents have the right to withdraw their child, the Supreme Court found that current arrangements did not meet legal requirements. I will explore options to ensure the right to withdrawal is effective, standardised and legally compliant.I have written to schools and advised them that guidance will be provided in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority's maintenance department will visit Ballycastle Integrated Primary School to inspect the occurrences of damp in the school building.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that the Area Maintenance Manager will contact the principal of Ballycastle Integrated Primary School to arrange to meet on site to discuss the issues with damp and leaks in the classroom.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the average length of time between applying for free school meals and being placed on the register in the last 12 months; and (ii) why parents are not refunded the cost of meals incurred during the intervening period when their application for free school meals is subsequently approved.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the processing and verification of applications for free school meals (FSM). It has confirmed that the average time to process a FSM application in the 2024/25 school year was 1.12 days compared to 1.05 days for the 2025/26 school year to date. Once an application is approved, in the vast majority of cases, a child will receive FSM from the following Monday. Article 58 (1) of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 is the legislation under which FSM are provided to pupils. This only gives the power to provide a meal and does not provide for any payment in lieu of a meal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why Ballycastle Integrated Primary School and Nursery Unit was required to meet the costs of installing security blinds following an incident in March 2025.
answer_text: The installation of window blinds in both controlled and non-controlled sectors is a school cost under the Common Funding Formula.This approach is consistent with other schools across the schools’ estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when applicants to the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund will be formally notified of the outcome of their applications, given that groups were advised they would receive confirmation by 19 November 2025.
answer_text: Due to the high level of interest in this scheme it is taking longer than anticipated to review all applications received. Applicant organisations are expected to receive notifications of the outcome of their Stage One Expressions of Interest this week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any contingency plans in place for occasions where there is a reduction in the supply of veterinary medicines.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when initial advice will be given to schools, in relation to the provision of Christian-based religious education and collective worship, in light of the UK Supreme Court judgement, In the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant).
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 has significant implications for our schools. I will consider the judgment in full before determining any essential remedies that both respect the law and safeguard the role of religion in education.  I have requested further legal advice on the matter and I have written to schools to advise that guidance will be provided over the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how his Department and its arm's-length bodies ensure that as a condition of receiving funding, all members and volunteers of community groups supported by his Department undergo and maintain a valid AccessNI check; and (ii) any enforcement or monitoring mechanisms are in place to verify compliance.
answer_text: My Department does not require funded organisations, including those supported by the Department’s arm’s-length bodies, to carry out AccessNI checks for all members and volunteers as a condition of grant funding. However, recipients must provide assurance that they have an appropriate Safeguarding Policy in place and confirm that they can operate effective procedures and controls to protect children and young people.It is the responsibility of each funded organisation to determine the level of vetting required for their staff and volunteers, in line with their safeguarding obligations and relevant legislation. Compliance is monitored through grant assurance processes, which include checks that safeguarding policies and procedures are in place and are being implemented effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department’s current procedures, such as grant terms and conditions and guidance documents, which require that funded community groups implement AccessNI checks for all personnel.
answer_text: Part of Voluntary Community Division / Community Empowerment Division’s Grant Funding Agreements with the Voluntary and Community Sector organisations involves confirmation that required policies are in place relevant to the project that is being delivered, such as a ‘Safeguarding Policy’.While confirmation of Organisations’ Policies are requested, it is the organisations’ responsibility to determine the policies relevant to the project being delivered; it is also their responsibility to ensure that any additional works, checks or other obligations such as Access NI checks are carried out.These policies and procedures can be checked through the Department’s monitoring of the project deliverables, or at any other point as deemed necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 33146/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 33146/22-27 was answered on 12 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to extend the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme to include adults on the ADHD waiting list who are seeking private diagnosis and treatment after been on the waiting list for at least two years.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme opened on Monday 2 June 2025.  The Scheme allows patients, who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland, and are on a Health and Social Care (HSC) treatment waiting list for longer than one year, the option to seek and pay for treatment in the Republic of Ireland or wider European Union and have the cost reimbursed, up to the cost of the treatment to the HSC in Northern Ireland. Treatment must be one which would have been received within the HSC, and is subject to prior approval. The current initiative is targeting long term waits for treatment and therefore the parameters of the Scheme have been set to reflect this.There is currently no commissioned service for ADHD in Northern Ireland. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting times across Trusts.  In response, my Department has commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability. The final report is expected by end November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will write to all schools in relation to allowing pupils to wear overcoats in inclement weather.
answer_text: The School Uniforms Guidelines, which issued to all grant-aided and independent primary and post-primary schools on 12 November 2025, cover the wearing of outdoor clothing and coats.  All schools that the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill will apply to have therefore been written to.In terms of education policy or legislation, the appropriate place to address such matters is in these guidelines which will become statutory once the Bill gains Royal Assent.  Guideline 3 requires seasonal considerations to be reflected in school uniform policies e.g. through the addition of a generic coat in winter.  Guideline 6 provides that all pupils should be permitted to wear a non-branded coat in a range of colours advised by the school and informs schools that the Department considers it unreasonable to limit the outdoor coat to one which is branded. In relation to storage, the Department’s School Building Handbook makes provision for cloakrooms in all schools. Furthermore, the Nursery Handbook provides that a cubicle/cage large enough to contain shoes and wellington boots is provided below each coat hook. The Post Primary Handbook states that pupils’ cloakroom accommodation be provided in separate, suitably located spaces conveniently located throughout the building as well as lockers, equivalent to the number of cycle storage spaces for the school to accommodate those who choose to cycle to school. There is also allowance in the Post Primary Handbook for lockers to be provided for all pupils in lieu of cloakrooms, if a school prefers to have secure storage. I look forward to seeing a positive change in relation to suitable outdoor clothing, where this is needed, from the outworkings of the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill and associated guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to ensure that GPs (i) recognise private adult ADHD diagnoses obtained outside Northern Ireland; and (ii) comply with shared care protocols for prescribing medication.
answer_text: GPs are independent contractors who provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations.  As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of their individual patients.  The legacy Health and Social Care Board issued correspondence on a number of occasions to private providers and general practice, most recently in June 2021, regarding requests for HSC prescriptions following private consultations. The purpose of this correspondence was to remind both sectors of their clinical responsibilities in relation to prescribing of medications to patients under their care.  This guidance still applies and has been supplemented in September 2024 by guidance issued by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) ‘Private (non-HSC) Prescribing Requests Guidance for Primary Care Prescribers’. This guidance provides a reminder that the legal responsibility for prescribing lies with the prescriber who signs the prescription, and it is the responsibility of the individual prescriber to prescribe within their own level of competence and scope of practice.Whilst there is no obligation on GPs to prescribe, in instances where a GP considers that the medication being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competences, does not breach the terms of their contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council (GMC) guidance on Good Medical Practice, then they may choose to prescribe.As clinical legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.If a GP considers that ongoing prescribing of a medicine recommended by a private practitioner is appropriate for supply through the HSC service, then under the HSC terms of service they may issue an HSC prescription.A private sector ADHD diagnosis does not preclude GPs entering into shared arrangements. However, it remains at their discretion.I can confirm that work has been undertaken by my Department to help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across our five Health and Social Care Trusts, subject to future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting list for an adult ADHD assessment; and (ii) any steps being taken to reduce the waiting list, in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) for adults in Northern Ireland.  ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams.  There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting times across Trusts.In response, the Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. The ADHD needs assessment report was received by the Department at the end of November 2025 and is currently being considered by officials, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to provide an update on the adult ADHD pilot scheme being trailed at Causeway Hospital; and (ii) whether there are any plans to expand the pilot scheme across all of the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) for adults in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT). The Trust had initiated a pilot study across all of the NHSCT (and not just in the Causeway Locality), in a bid to determine the proportion of adults being referred for potential ADHD who would meet the criteria for such an assessment, were this available. The pilot is not associated with any subsequent activity being undertaken to provide care or treatment to individuals in respect of suspected ADHD. (ii)The processes and approaches to the screening, assessment, and care and treatment of people referred for potential ADHD, have been shared by all Trusts with the regional ADHD Oversight Group, chaired by the Department of Health (DOH). This information will be captured within the DOH ADHD Needs Assessment which was commissioned in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. The ADHD needs assessment report was received by the Department at the end of November 2025 and is currently being considered by officials, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider including a requirement for schools to (i) allow pupils to wear suitable outdoor clothing, such as coats; and (ii) provide safe storage for these items during the school day, in future education policy or legislation.
answer_text: The School Uniforms Guidelines, which issued to all grant-aided and independent primary and post-primary schools on 12 November 2025, cover the wearing of outdoor clothing and coats.  All schools that the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill will apply to have therefore been written to.In terms of education policy or legislation, the appropriate place to address such matters is in these guidelines which will become statutory once the Bill gains Royal Assent.  Guideline 3 requires seasonal considerations to be reflected in school uniform policies e.g. through the addition of a generic coat in winter.  Guideline 6 provides that all pupils should be permitted to wear a non-branded coat in a range of colours advised by the school and informs schools that the Department considers it unreasonable to limit the outdoor coat to one which is branded. In relation to storage, the Department’s School Building Handbook makes provision for cloakrooms in all schools. Furthermore, the Nursery Handbook provides that a cubicle/cage large enough to contain shoes and wellington boots is provided below each coat hook. The Post Primary Handbook states that pupils’ cloakroom accommodation be provided in separate, suitably located spaces conveniently located throughout the building as well as lockers, equivalent to the number of cycle storage spaces for the school to accommodate those who choose to cycle to school. There is also allowance in the Post Primary Handbook for lockers to be provided for all pupils in lieu of cloakrooms, if a school prefers to have secure storage. I look forward to seeing a positive change in relation to suitable outdoor clothing, where this is needed, from the outworkings of the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill and associated guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of koi carp missing from HMP Hydebank; (ii) whether the missing carp where discovered at the same time; (iii) whether there was a net covering the pond that the carp were kept in; and (iv) the total value of the missing carp.
answer_text: (i) 	A total of 11 koi carp have gone missing from a pond in the grounds of Hydebank Wood College.(ii) 	The carp went missing from the pond on two separate occasions.(iii) 	There is no netting covering the pond as there have never been any reported incidents of predation before; however, there are other safeguards in place around the perimeter of the pond.(iv) 	It is difficult to estimate what the market value of the missing carp would have been as the price would be influenced by factors including type, size, genetic lineage and gender.   However, as many of the fish were donated to the NI Prison Service any such value would be notional.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the timescale for the agreement of powers to suspend the Police Ombudsman; and (ii) whether they will be in place before the next Ombudsman is appointed.
answer_text: Primary legislation is required to introduce a power to suspend certain statutory officeholders whose terms and conditions are laid out in the statute governing their role, including the Police Ombudsman. We have tasked officials to identify an appropriate legislative vehicle by which to introduce such a power, and the range of offices for which it is required. Once this work has been completed and full Bill instructions prepared, timescales will be clearer. Although the power is not in place before appointment of the next Ombudsman, the Candidate Information Booklet for the current Police Ombudsman competition stated that:The Ombudsman is established under section 51 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1998 and Schedule 3 to that Act sets out the relevant provisions in relation to the post. If amendments are made to these provisions during the term of the person appointed to the post, those amendments will apply in relation to the postholder.We ensured this was included so the subsequent statutory provisions would apply, once enacted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether heating systems in schools are controlled centrally, meaning that schools cannot adjust radiators.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department will consider a compensation scheme for traders affected by the closure of the Bann Bridge, Kilrea.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount the current Police Ombudsman was paid whilst not at work, as a result of (i) sickness absence; and (ii) other forms of non-annual leave absence, in the last 28 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps being taken to find a long-term solution to avian influenza, such as vaccine programmes, in the light of an outbreak in Country Fermanagh.
answer_text: Preventative vaccination of poultry and captive birds against avian influenza is not recommended as an effective disease control measure at this juncture due to a number of factors such as trade impacts, surveillance required and challenges with vaccine deployment. Therefore, outside of zoo animals, preventive vaccination for avian influenza is not currently permitted in Northern Ireland.Research is ongoing, however, into the feasibility of wide scale vaccine use. A cross-Government and industry avian influenza vaccination task force, which includes DAERA, was established in February 2023 with an initial report completed in July 2025. The report recommends a series of actions, including the potential for a UK field vaccination trial, to resolve essential knowledge gaps before any final recommendations are made on the widespread use of preventative vaccination.  Any future decisions on avian influenza disease control measures for Northern Ireland, including the use of vaccination, will be based upon the latest scientific, ornithological, and veterinary advice considering recommendations from the taskforce.Further information on the cross-Government and industry avian influenza vaccination task force is available here https://www.gov.uk/government/groups/bird-flu-avian-influenza-vaccination-task-force
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of how the declining numbers of taxi drivers impacts a safe, diverse and thriving night-time economy.
answer_text: My Department, together with Tourism NI and Belfast’s Business Improvement Districts, has commissioned research into the night-time economy. Early findings indicate that stakeholders view dependable late-night transport, especially taxis, as vital for ensuring a safe and thriving night-time economy. According to surveys, 61% of people identify poor late-night transport as the biggest obstacle to enjoying the city after 10pm. The shortage of taxis restricts both economic and cultural activity, preventing tourists and locals from fully participating in evening events.To address these issues, Minister Kimmins and I have worked with Executive Colleagues and Belfast City Council to secure funding for Translink’s one-year pilot scheme for late-night public transport. This initiative is supported by the local taxi industry. I remain committed to collaborating with Minister Kimmins and other stakeholders, including the Purple Flag Committee, to tackle ongoing challenges, such as increasing the number of available taxis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the Assembly passing a motion on Supporting Service Leavers, Veterans and their Families on 4 November 2025, when he will appoint a departmental armed forces liaison officer.
answer_text: I thank the member for his question; it is important we continue our support for veterans and I welcome the endorsement from many in this house on the motion.I can confirm that I will be appointing an Armed Forces liaison officer within the department. I want to do this right and respect the security and sensitivity that this role will require and I am happy to update him once further progress is made.I will also be making an announcement in due course on further provisions that I and my department will be making for our veterans, which I will update this house on also.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment made of the potential impact on animal health and welfare in the event of access to veterinary medicines being restricted after the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland, including any potential impacts on animal health and welfare, falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). I will continue to engage with the UK Government on the need to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines into Northern Ireland beyond the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025.I welcome the UK Government’s publication of the Protecting Animal Health policy in June 2025, and the practical solutions it offers to prevent restrictions on access to veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.  This policy underpins the rollout of two new support schemes, designed to provide a responsive, adaptable, and flexible approach to addressing supply challenges with veterinary medicines, including unforeseen issues.I believe these schemes offer sustainable solutions and represent a significant step forward in securing the future of our local veterinary medicines supply.In the period ahead, I will continue to engage with the UK Government and the Veterinary Medicines Working Group on the key details, governance, and oversight arrangements to ensure these schemes deliver the outcomes they are intended to achieve.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail whether (i) financial; or (ii) logistical, support will be provided to Northern Ireland-based suppliers to mitigate the impact of new regulatory requirements for veterinary medicines from 1 January 2026.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland, including any decision to provide support to suppliers impacted by the new regulatory requirements, falls under the Direction and Control of the Defra Secretary of State.	I will continue to engage with the UK Government on the need to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland beyond the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025.I welcome the UK Government’s publication of the Protecting Animal Health policy in June 2025, and its clear commitment to supporting industry as it adapts to the new veterinary medicines legislation. These regulatory changes will require adjustments from Marketing Authorisation Holders and other stakeholders operating in Northern Ireland. Encouragingly, UK Government data indicates that the majority of Marketing Authorisation Holders intend to continue supplying most of the veterinary medicinal products currently available in the Northern Ireland market.Looking ahead, I will maintain close engagement with both the UK Government and the Veterinary Medicines Working Group to help safeguard the continued supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the Northern Ireland Rapid Response Contingency Plan for Asian Hornet was last updated.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Rapid Response Contingency Plan for Asian Hornet was adapted from the DEFRA original and published in 2020. The Contingency Plan had been scheduled for review this autumn and a revised plan is currently being drafted, informed by the recent Asian hornet incursion in Dundonald.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement that has taken place with (i) the UK Government; and (ii) EU counterparts, to prevent disruption to the availability of prescription veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland from 1 January 2026.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).	Since taking up office I have had regular engagement with the UK Government on the future supply of veterinary medicines into Northern Ireland from 1 January 2026.  I have raised concerns directly with successive Secretaries of State, including Steve Reed OBE MP and Emma Reynolds MP, and maintain regular bilateral meetings with Baroness Hayman of Ullock, where this issue is routinely discussed.  I also sit on the UK Government’s Veterinary Medicines Working Group, jointly chaired by Baroness Hayman, which includes veterinary experts, industry representatives, and political stakeholders.  In this forum, I can continue to provide my expertise and advice to the UK Government on potential measures to prevent disruption to the future supply of veterinary medicines. I will continue to engage with the UK Government on the need to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail whether (i) koi carp have gone missing from a pond at Hydebank Wood, and (ii) the pond is covered by CCTV.
answer_text: Koi Carp have recently gone missing from a pond in the grounds of Hydebank Wood College. After discussion with relevant agencies, it is believed that a predator such as a mink, heron or otters may have taken them, as they have been seen in the area.The area where the pond is situated is not covered by CCTV.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any contingency plans in place to support (i) farmers; (ii) pet owners; and (iii) veterinary practices, in the event of GB suppliers being no longer permitted to ship veterinary medicines after 31 December 2025.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland (NI), including the development of any contingency plans relating to this, fall under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).		I will continue to engage with the UK Government on the need to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland.I welcome the UK Government’s publication of the Protecting Animal Health policy in June 2025, and the practical solutions it sets out to support the continued supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland, including those coming from Great Britain.  This policy underpins the launch of two new support schemes, designed to offer a responsive, adaptable, and flexible approach to managing supply challenges—particularly those that are unforeseen.I believe these schemes, announced in June, provide sustainable solutions and mark a significant step forward in securing the long-term future of our local veterinary medicines supply beyond 31 December 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps taken to ensure continuity of supply for veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland, after the end of the current grace period on 31 December 2025.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).I will continue to engage with the UK Government on the need to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland.	I welcome the publication of the UK Government’s new Protecting Animal Health policy in June 2025 and the practical solutions which it provides to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.	  	This policy supports the rollout of two new support schemes, which are designed to provide a responsive, adaptable and flexible means of addressing supply issues that arise with veterinary medicines, including unforeseen issues. 		I believe that these new schemes offer sustainable solutions and represent a significant step forward in securing the future of our local veterinary medicines supply. 		Over the time ahead I will continue to engage with both the UK Government and the Veterinary Medicines Working Group.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department is taking to strengthen the regulation and enforcement of laws governing the use of fireworks, particularly in residential areas.
answer_text: I have no current plans the review the existing fireworks licensing arrangements.  The existing regulations provide a robust framework that seeks to ensure fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use by the public.The licensing system aids partners, including PSNI, to identify unlicensed and illegal fireworks use.  Offences committed under the firework regulations may result in a fine of up to £5,000, or a prison sentence, or both.Any concerns about the misuse of fireworks should be reported to the PSNI.In recognition of the public concern around the misuse of fireworks, I have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of her Department's latest update that repairs to Kilrea Bridge will take 14 weeks, whether there will be a compensation scheme introduced for those businesses impacted.
answer_text: I fully appreciate the impact of the ongoing closure and the understandable frustration of the local community, including businesses in the area and thank them for their patience while we work to deliver a safe and effective solution. I want to reassure you that we are working at pace to strengthen the road and will continue to explore every opportunity to accelerate progress and minimise disruption.My Department regrets any inconvenience experienced by the local community and businesses due to the works and associated road closure.  My Department, however,  has no powers tooffer compensation to businesses for pure economic loss resulting from the exercise of its statutory duty to maintain the public road network.I am pleased to confirm that a specialist contractor has now been appointed and work to repair the damage will commence on Monday 10 November. The 14-week programme is longer than originally anticipated, due to the difficult ground conditions on site. The solution requires specialist equipment to drill between the two approach walls beneath the existing road surface. Tie-bars will then be inserted into these drilled holes to structurally connect the retaining walls on either side of the road.  The contractor has advised that the presence of large boulders will make it more difficult for the drilling operation. As a result, it will take longer to drill, and the installation of each tie-bar will be more time-consuming.Officials are continuing to engage with the contractor to explore options to reduce the timescale and minimise disruption. We will keep the local communities and elected representatives up to date on the timeline throughout the works. My Department will continue to provide updates on the repair works on our website at: Kilrea Bridge, B64 Bann Road, Kilrea – Closure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the measures in place to assist apprentices in securing an employer to support their training; and (ii) any support is offered to employers who take on apprentices.
answer_text: My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes are demand-led, with employers establishing apprenticeship opportunities in line with business need. 		The Department actively encourages employers to take on apprentices through a range of promotional activities, including an annual Apprenticeship Week and twice yearly advertising campaigns across TV, radio, and social media, timed to coincide with Apprenticeship Week and academic results. The Department also engages directly with potential employers and learners at events such as job fairs and career insight days. 	These efforts aim to raise awareness of the benefits of apprenticeships, increase visibility of available programmes, and boost participation across all sectors.		The Department funds the directed, off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship, investing over £48 million annually across both programmes. This funding ensures that employers are not financially liable for the delivery of apprenticeship training, making the recruitment of apprentices a more feasible and attractive option.		To further support employers, an incentive payment is available upon the successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework. This payment ranges from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area and represents a 20% increase under ApprenticeshipsNI 2025 compared to the previous ApprenticeshipsNI 2021 programme. The incentive is designed to assist with the broader costs of supporting and mentoring apprentices. Employers in the North of Ireland are also exempt from paying Class 1 National Insurance contributions for apprentices under the age of 25, subject to certain conditions.		Both apprenticeship programmes continue to grow, with participation at an all-time high. For example, the most recently published statistics show that total starts on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme increased by 70% between 2019/20 and 2023/24.		Higher Level Apprenticeship starts at FE colleges have increased by almost 200% from 2017/18 to 2023/24, while HLA starts at our universities have risen by over 130% from 2018/19 to 2022/23.		Apprenticeships are delivered by a regional network of training providers who are able to respond to current and anticipated demand from employers.  The number of training providers delivering the ApprenticeshipsNI programme was recently expanded by a further 10 organisations, helping to further increase connections between employers and potential apprentices.		Finally, the Department’s Apprenticeship Action Plan recognises the need to encourage more employers to recruit apprentices and includes deliverables such as piloting Apprenticeship Navigators to support SMEs employing their first apprentices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) training; and (ii) professional development, provided to course tutors delivering apprentice programmes.
answer_text: Tutors employed by private training organisations, Further Education Colleges and Higher Education Institutions contracted to deliver the Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeship (HLA) programmes must fulfil the qualification criteria set by awarding bodies, Further and Higher Education policy, and industry standards.My Department ensures that the quality of Directed Training delivery in the ApprenticeshipsNI programme is through a structured monitoring and improvement process, supported by regular Education Training Inspections (ETI). This is grounded in ongoing self-evaluation and planning, guided by the ETI’s ISEF for Work-Based Learning.All ApprenticeshipsNI Training Providers are required to submit an annual, evidence-based Quality Improvement Plan to the Department. This is also the case for HLAs delivered by the FE Colleges and CAFRE.  Ongoing quality assurance and improvement processes are informed by gradings and reports from the Department’s monitoring procedures and annual ETI formal inspections and evaluations. Finally, the Annual Provider Review is the core mechanism to assess quality in those higher education providers funded by my Department.  All HLA training providers are members of the Quality Assurance Agency for Higher Education (QAA), which is regularly commissioned to undertake quality reviews for individual institutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all of the discharges from Ballyclare Wastewater Treatment Works, in the last six months, including (i) the dates of discharge; (ii) the volume of discharge; (iii) whether the discharge was planned or unplanned; and (iv) the chemical analysis of the discharges.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:Ballyclare wastewater treatment works discharges final treated effluent on a daily basis, as permitted by its discharge consent issued by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA);the latest sampling information available shows that for the six-month period from March 2025 to August 2025, Ballyclare wastewater treatment works discharged between 1,800 m3/day and 11,000 m3/day, which is within the daily limit set by NIEA;the daily discharge from Ballyclare wastewater treatment works is planned; andthe discharge from Ballyclare wastewater treatment works is monitored to identify the levels of Ammonia, Biochemical Oxygen Demand (Allylthiourea) and Suspended Solids. From April 2025 to mid-October 2025, the levels identified for the three parameters, from the samples taken, were within the limits set by NIEA. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the performance of the current Police Ombudsman.
answer_text: As Minister of Justice, I have no role in assessing the performance of the Police Ombudsman, as a corporation sole.My Department engages with the Office of the Police Ombudsman on matters of governance and resourcing, as set out in the Management Statement and Financial Memorandum between the Department and the Office.  In that regard, I held the Annual Accountability Meeting with the Ombudsman and her Chief Executive on 18 March 2025, during which we discussed the Office’s performance in detail.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans his Department has (i) to replace or restore the missing and damaged sets of stepping stones cross the Shimna River in Tollymore Forest Park; and (ii) to preserve this feature of the park.
answer_text: The stepping stones across the Shimma river in Tollymore Forest Park are recognised as unique attraction. In recent years the stepping stone crossing has suffered damage due to storm events and erosion, including one of the stones being dislodged and washed downriver.  The missing stone has been recovered by Forest Service and will be re-instated.  Forest Service has commissioned the works to repair the stepping stone crossing, which includes obtaining the necessary statutory environmental approvals for work in the Shimma river due its designation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department’s (i) current; and (ii) planned measures for the maintenance and enhancement of Tollymore Forest Park, with particular reference to (a) its tourism potential; (b) visitor infrastructure; (c) heritage conservation; and (d) its contribution to community wellbeing.
answer_text: The primary visitor attractions in Tollymore Forest Park are the path networks and the ability to walk in a mature and diverse forest environment.  The health and wellbeing benefits provided by the forests are widely recognised. Local people and out of state tourists make approximately 200k visits to Tollymore Forest Park annually..  Forest Service operates an intensive maintenance programme for the upkeep and enhancement of the forest park and its facilities. In particular, the primary facilities and key attractions to visitors is the comprehensive path network incorporating the iconic Shimma river corridor and the touring caravan park provision as gateway to the Mourne Mountains.  As part of the ongoing upkeep and enhancement works Forest Service has recently commissioned restoration works on the historic arches.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to (i) the Tea House; and (ii) Clanbrassil Barn, at Tollymore Forest Park, to detail (a) the dates within the last 15 years when any significant building, renovation, or refurbishment works were carried out; (b) the nature and scope of those works; (c) the total cost of the project; and (d) how the building has been used since the completion of the respective works.
answer_text: Renovation and refurbishment works were completed by Forest Service in both the Tea House and Clanbrassil Barn at Tollymore Forest Park in 2015 under the NI Executive’s Economy and Jobs Funding Initiative. The significant works carried out in the Tea House included the installation of energy efficient windows. Extensive renovations of the Clanbrassil barn were also undertaken. These included re-roofing and flooring of the building, restoring the original stonework and upgrading toilet facilities. The overall costs for these projects in Tollymore Forest Park was £640,000. Following the works the Clanbrassil Barn continued to be used primarily as a customer service facility, with one area used as base for staff involved in the service provision.  The Tea House building ground floor is used as a storage area for the operational needs of the Forest Park and the first floor is used occasionally to accommodate events organised internally and externally.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
