question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when his Department will be in a position to recruit a bee inspector.
answer_text: DAERA does not specifically recruit for bee inspectors. Bee inspection duties are carried out by appropriately trained staff from within the agriculture inspectorate, as a part of their wider range of duties. Competitions are held regularly to maintain the appropriate level of agriculture inspectorate resource to fulfil legislative responsibilities, including for honeybee related inspections.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to provide appropriate training and protective equipment, such as bite guards and spit visors, to staff in mainstream schools supporting pupils with special educational needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised their Children and Young People’s Services (CYPS) Directorate employs a range of proactive strategies to de-escalate and defuse potentially challenging situations in educational settings. Accredited restricted physical intervention training is provided to staff working in Special Schools, Education Otherwise than at School (EOTAS) centres and to staff working within Specialist Provisions attached to mainstream settings.As with all Personal Protective Equipment (PPE), it is the responsibility of each individual school to assess the specific risk and determine the PPE requirements accordingly. Following a risk assessment, schools must procure the necessary PPE through the EA’s approved procurement system and ensure that the required items are provided to the relevant staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the directions given to schools regarding school meal menu options and ingredients, in order to allow schools more flexibility to provide meals and reduce waste.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the day-to-day operation and delivery of the school meals service in all schools other than in voluntary grammar and grant-maintained integrated schools, where all aspects of catering provision are the responsibility of each schools’ Board of Governors. In schools where the EA provides the school meals service, schools themselves are not involved in areas such as menu development or ingredients used. Where the EA provides the school meals service, it uses a harmonised menu across all nursery and primary school settings. This approach ensures that menus align with the relevant nutritional standards, that there is a consistent product supply chain in place for these schools that can benefit from better advance forecasting of product requirements and that the school meal offering is of the same standard across settings that represents value for money for parents.The EA offers a degree of flexibility in its food offerings in post-primary schools to reflect the cafeteria-style service offered.  It does provide a harmonised food offering in these schools in its themed food counters. The EA has advised that the above approach helps to minimise food waste whilst continuing to provide a healthy nutritious meal to children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress to reduce diagnostic waiting times.
answer_text: I acknowledge that the current waiting times for diagnostics are lengthy across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need, and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding. As part of the Elective Care Framework, Implementation and Funding Plan, £13m has been secured for endoscopy expansion and £12m to increase imaging capacity. Endoscopy waiting times have shown progress in recent years. In August 2025, endoscopy waiting lists were 58% lower than the peak in June 2022, representing 23,300 reduction in the number of patients waiting.  The Regional Endoscopy centres in Lagan Valley Hospital and Omagh Hospital, have scoped approximately 17,000 patients to date which has helped reduce regional variation in waiting times.Newly commissioned CT and MRI scanners have been funded at the Southern and Northern Trust’s Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDCs), which will help reduce and equalise waiting times across the region. Improvement in CT waiting times has been possible using both RDC activity and additional waiting list initiative activity which has been delivered in-house and in the independent sector. From the end of March 2025 until end of October 2025, the number of patients waiting more than two weeks for red flag CT reduced by 44% and the number of patients waiting more than 4 weeks for an urgent CT reduced by 42%. The RDC MRI scanner in Causeway became operational the week of 13 October 2025, and the South Tyrone MRI scanner is expected to be operational in November 2025. Targeted work has already commenced to improve on the current regional MRI waiting times utilising this RDC capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the suitability assessment that was conducted before Bailey, the emotional support dog, was placed inside Magilligan Prison; (ii) whether there is an audit trail for this assessment; and (iii) the number of prison officers in Magilligan Prison to have completed emotional support dog deployment or awareness training.
answer_text: Bailey was an emotional support dog registered to the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS). All NIPS establishments are suitable for their use and deployment. Bailey’s introduction to Magilligan prison was graduated to assess any potential risks to his emotional and physical welfare and measure his interactions with all persons on site.There is an audit trail for the risk assessment.There are no Prison Officers trained as the training was cancelled when Bailey left HMP Magilligan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider introducing legislative changes to require tractors and other agricultural vehicles, which do not exceed 25mph, to display an amber warning beacon when travelling on all public roads, not just unrestricted dual carriageways.
answer_text: The Road Vehicles Lighting Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2000 (the 2000 Regulations) were made with the intent of ensuring that vehicles are equipped with the necessary lighting to enhance visibility and safety on the roads.  Regulation 20 of the 2000 Regulations requires any vehicle, including tractors and other agricultural vehicles, which have a maximum speed not exceeding 25mph, to display an amber warning beacon when on any carriageway of an unrestricted dual-carriageway road.Whilst I remain committed to road safety, my resources are limited and I'm focusing them on interventions where evidence shows they will make a difference. My officials are not aware of any evidence that the proposed intervention would improve road safety. Therefore I have no plans to amend or introduce regulations which will require such vehicles to display amber warning beacons when being used on any other public roads at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 32215/22-27, to detail whether her Department (i) plans to carry out an economic impact assessment on the closure of Kilrea Bridge; and (ii) has considered implementing a compensation scheme for those impacted.
answer_text: I fully acknowledge the inconvenience caused by the closure and the impact it has had on the local business and residential community.The closure undoubtedly presents challenges to local business and the community, however my Department does not carry out economic impact assessments or provide compensation for economic losses resulting from road closures, and it does not have the powers to do so.I assure you my Department is working at pace to take forward temporary repairs to allow the bridge to be reopened as soon as possible. See my Department’s webpage for updates regarding Kilrea Bridge; Kilrea Bridge, B64 Bann Road, Kilrea – Closure | Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Pathology Blueprint Programme.
answer_text: The Pathology Blueprint Programme was established by my Department in 2022 as part of a strategic initiative to transform and modernise Health and Social Care pathology services, which has also included significant investment in laboratory infrastructure and workforce in recent years. The Blueprint Programme aims, subject to business case approval and funding, to establish a single management structure for all pathology and blood transfusion services in the form of a new regional pathology services agency by April 2027.  It forms a key part of the diagnostic capacity element of my Three Year Plan to stabilise, reform and deliver health and social care, which I published last year. The Programme Team has engaged extensively during this period with Health and Social Care clinical, scientific and managerial staff as well as with relevant professional bodies and staff representative organisations in the scoping and appraisal of options and benefits, the design of a high-level operating model and the development of an Outline Business Case (OBC) which will be submitted to my Department for consideration in the coming months.  I will be in a position to provide a further update following the business case review process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to the training provided to prison officers in respect of emotional support dogs, to detail (i) the number of officers trained; (ii) the provider of the training; (iii) whether training records are maintained; and (iv) whether the training is accredited.
answer_text: One Officer has received specific training in respect of emotional support dogs, receiving a Diploma in animal assisted therapy & activities.  The training, which is accredited, was provided by BSY. All staff training records are maintained by the Prison Service College.Staff, working with emotional support animals make “best use” of their presence in reaching any person undergoing difficulties, working in collaboration with partners to maximise these opportunities within prisons.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the benefits of the Partnership Panel, which facilitates meetings between Ministers and councillors.
answer_text: The Partnership Panel for Northern Ireland provides a forum for Ministers and councillors to discuss strategic policy and operational matters of mutual interest. I believe that further work needs to be done to maximise the effectiveness of the body.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of primary schools that have been (i) built, (ii) extended; and (iii) refurbished, since 2010.
answer_text: Major Capital Works Programme ProjectsThere have been twenty-one new build primary schools and four new build special schools and one special school refurbishment project completed since 2010 under the Major Capital Works Programme.School Enhancement Programme ProjectsThere have been thirteen primary school projects completed under the first call to the Department’s School Enhancement Programme (SEP1) since 2010.  These projects generally involved elements of both extension and refurbishment.Fresh Start Agreement ProjectsThere have been five new build primary schools and one primary school extension completed under the Fresh Start Programme since 2010.Minor Works ProgrammeA number of minor works refurbishment projects have also been undertaken since 2010.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps they have taken to address the absence of legal powers to suspend a holder of the office of Ombudsman.
answer_text: We have met with officials to consider how best to introduce a power to suspend office holders under our appointing authority. Work is now ongoing to enable bill instructions to be prepared leading to the introduction of primary legislation providing for the suspension of all office holders who have been appointed under statute.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any future plans to increase the protection of all hospital staff against attacks from patients by increasing security or using body cameras.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts have confirmed they have a 24/7 security service and security officers present in the Trust’s acute hospital settings. There are currently no plans from any HSC Trusts to increase the number of security staff and any plans to do so would be subject to the required funding.With regards to Body Worn Cameras (BWCs) the Southern Trust confirmed that BWCs are currently in use by nursing staff at Bluestone Mental Health Inpatient Facility and by Security Porters in Craigavon Area Hospital and Daisy Hill Hospital. There are future plans to assess the potential of rolling out BWC usage to key locations.  Northern Trust had advised that senior staff working in the Emergency Department at Antrim Area Hospital are currently taking part in a 12-week trial to pilot the use of body-worn cameras. The trial commenced on 1 September 2025.Belfast Trust and Western Trust are all planning to implement a pilot for body worn cameras. South Eastern Trust confirmed they have no plans to carry out such action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 32337/22-27, to detail when she first became aware of the call for a public inquiry.
answer_text: I first became aware of the call for a public inquiry following public reporting that MI5 had admitted, in documents submitted to the Investigatory Powers Tribunal, that it had unlawfully obtained communications data from the mobile phone of former BBC Northern Ireland home affairs correspondent Vincent Kearney in 2006 and 2009.As previously stated, these matters relate to powers and oversight mechanisms that fall outside the devolved space and would therefore be for the Secretary of State to address.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what initiatives he has introduced to curb illegal dog fighting, in the last two years.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 assigns The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) with statutory responsibility for enforcing the law in relation to animal fighting.  Under the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983, five types of dogs are designated as “dogs bred for fighting” and are subject to prohibitions or ownership restrictions.  Enforcement of this legislation is a matter for local councils.  My Department continues to liaise closely with the PSNI and local councils, when needed, to support the enforcement of animal welfare and dog control legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether her Department will install keep left signs on the roads near tourist hotspots, such as the Giants Causeway and the Dark Hedges, to ensure both pedestrian and driver safety.
answer_text: Reported instances of driving on the wrong side of the road at these locations are fairly rare. Officials monitor the road network on an ongoing basis and receive information on road traffic collisions from the PSNI on all incidents involving personal injury.  There have been no instances reported where driving on the wrong side of the road is cited as a cause of collision and therefore my department has no plans to install signage at these locations.Advice is provided through NI Direct for visitors driving in the North, including the requirement to drive on the left.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31779/22-27, whether a review is undertaken of the suitability of taxi drivers, who have been disqualified from providing home-to-school transport services, to continue holding a taxi driver’s licence, given that in the last five years, 124 taxi drivers have been disqualified from providing this service.
answer_text: It is a legal requirement for the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA), as the licensing authority, to be satisfied that an applicant meets the necessary requirements before a taxi driver’s licence can be granted. All taxi driver licence applicants in the north are required to provide an Enhanced Disclosure Certificate (EDC), which outlines any convictions relevant to their application. If an EDC is not provided, the application is cancelled, and no taxi driver licence is granted. In addition to the application process, licence holders must inform the DVA of any convictions incurred during the validity of their licence.The DVA may also be notified by the PSNI under the Common Law Police Disclosure (CLPD) scheme, where there is a public protection risk. Notifications may occur at the point of arrest or charge, rather than awaiting conviction. Although such notifications are rare, they enable the Department to respond swiftly to information indicating potential harm to children or vulnerable adults by suspending or revoking taxi driver licences.Home-to-school transport services, including the associated criteria and conditions for delivery, are administered and managed by the Department of Education.  These criteria and conditions differ from the DVA’s licensing requirements and therefore, I do not consider a broader review of taxi driver licensing arrangements is necessary.

The Member may wish to contact the relevant district council to discuss this issue.

My officials are finalising the application process for the Grassroots Strand and I hope to be in the position to make an announcement in the coming months.I recently announced the Olympic Legacy Fund offering community clubs the chance to apply for capital funding for projects with a total project cost ranging between a minimum of £1,000 to a maximum £50,000. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any initiatives she has introduced to curb illegal dog fighting, in the last two years.
answer_text: Dog fighting has no place in our communities. Those involved show a complete disregard for the welfare of animals and for the law. I condemn all forms of animal cruelty, including the barbaric and illegal practice of dog fighting, a vile offence that causes immense suffering to animals and is often linked to wider criminal activity.From a legislative viewpoint, dog fighting in Northern Ireland is addressed under two main pieces of legislation. The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 imposes restrictions on dogs being of a type to be bred for fighting and includes associated offences. The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 also explicitly lists animal fighting as a criminal offence. DAERA is the lead department for this legislative framework. Where there is evidence that points towards this type of offending, police are committed to working alongside partner agencies such as local councils and DAERA to identify, investigate and bring those responsible before the courts. None of the agencies involved have reported any legislative gap in the current provisions available to counter dog or other animal fighting; should such gaps be identified, I stand ready to work with colleagues in DAERA, local government and PSNI to ensure they are closed.I would implore those involved to stop and encourage anyone with knowledge of this vicious activity to report it immediately to the Police Service of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what actions he has taken to date to increase disabled car park spaces at (i) Antrim Area Hospital; (ii) Causeway Hospital; and (iii) Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: The aim of the Department of Health’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision and management of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. The policy states that an adequate number of parking spaces should be provided for people with a disability and that the provision of parking for people with a disability should comply with current legislation and should take account of other best practice guidance.	Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine to enable them to take local issues and circumstances into account.	The Northern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust recently opened a new purpose built, 271 space car park, at Antrim Area Hospital, increasing the percentage of designated disabled car parking spaces from 6% to 10% of total provision. These spaces are located as close as possible to the hospital entrance.   Causeway Hospital currently has 6% of its total parking provision designated for disabled use. The Northern HSC Trust has plans to extend the car park provision at Causeway Hospital, with an additional 90 standard car parking spaces being added. Following this a selection of the current standard spaces, located closest to the hospital entrance, will be redesignated to disabled car parking spaces.Following a recent review of disabled car parking space provision at the Royal Victoria Hospital, the Belfast HSC Trust, have redesignated 20 standard car parking spaces to disabled car parking spaces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps taken by her Department to combat (i) rural crime; and (ii) rural thefts, over the last two years.
answer_text: A partnership approach is central to addressing crime, including agricultural crime, in all regions. The Department of Justice chairs the Rural Crime Partnership (RCP) with members including the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), the Ulster Farmers’ Union, NFU Mutual, the Young Farmers’ Club of Ulster, the Federation of Small Businesses, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs). The RCP shares information on emerging and/or re-occurring crimes, to support a coordinated response and preventative approach to addressing rural crime. RCP members regularly engage with the public via social media to raise awareness of rural crime issues and to encourage rural communities to report such crimes. My Department recently participated in Rural Crime Week NI, a local media campaign led by the PSNI, which provided an opportunity to raise awareness of the diverse issues faced in our rural communities and help provide information and advice to people who may need or want support. At a local level, PCSPs, which are statutory bodies funded jointly by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, play a key role in addressing crime, including agricultural crime, and building confidence locally, through engagement and consultation with communities, including rural areas. PCSPs were established under the Justice Act (NI) 2011 and play a pivotal role in supporting communities in identifying solutions to issues of local concern.They also deliver a range of initiatives to raise awareness and support rural communities and businesses to protect their property and goods.  These initiatives include trailer/equipment marking events, safety talks and workshops provided to schools, speaking to local farming groups around crime prevention etc. and such actions have helped to continue the downward trend in recorded rural and agricultural crime. Over recent months Mid & East Antrim PCSP members attended the Ballymena Livestock Market to promote the Farm Watch and Text Alert Schemes, provide free trailer marking and Datatag retrofitting on quad bikes. Causeway Coasts & Glens PCSP members also took part in a rural focused crime prevention information event held at Kilrea Livestock Mart, where over 50 farmers were provided with crime prevention advice packs containing forensic property marking kits specifically designed for rural equipment, close shackle padlocks, high visibility jackets and slap bands, and leaflets with a guidance to rural security.There are also existing robust mechanisms in place for law enforcement agencies to work collaboratively through the Joint Agency Task Force (JAFT).  Tackling rural crime remains one of the six key priorities of the JATF. The JATF is a highly effective model for tackling cross-border organised crime, with strong practical and strategic collaboration between law enforcement agencies, including PSNI and An Garda Síochána. Notwithstand

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the appropriate Secretary of State should call a public inquiry into potential unlawful surveillance by MI5 of journalists in Northern Ireland, following the McCullough Review.
answer_text: The actions of MI5 against journalists in Northern Ireland does not fall within my remit as Justice Minister. This matter is the responsibility of the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland. I do recognise, however, that potential MI5 surveillance of special status groups was not covered as part of the recent McCullough Review, and would share the concern, expressed by many, about the IPT’s recent discovery that the Security Service illegally obtained communications data from the phone of a then-BBC journalist in Northern Ireland. Freedom of expression is a core human right in the UK and must be respected.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the issues caused by Storm Amy, will he consider developing a mobile communication platform for the Education Authority, to enable agile and fast communication with schools across Northern Ireland, when it is required.

To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the late communication by the Education Authority with schools in relation to closures due to Storm Amy, will he consider conducting a review to identify lessons in contingency planning and timely communication.

It is essential that we learn from each incident to ensure that contingency plans and emergency protocols are appropriate to address future storms or weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to consult on introducing legislation that will prevent those convicted of domestic abuse or violence against women and girls, to change their name via Deed Poll.
answer_text: At present, my Department has no plans to introduce legislation that would specifically prevent individuals convicted of domestic abuse or violence against women and girls from changing their name via Deed Poll.During the consultation on the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, stakeholders did not identify this issue as a priority, and it has therefore not been incorporated into the Strategy’s first three-year action plan.However, I remain committed to ensuring that appropriate measures are in place to protect victims and the wider public and have therefore asked my officials to keep this issue under review.  More broadly, it is important to recognise that sexual and domestic abuse can be intrinsically linked, and the Crime and Policing Bill currently making its way through Westminster will introduce new UK-wide restrictions on certain registered sex offenders who wish to apply for a specified replacement identity document in a new name.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is (i) making enquires on; and (ii) investigating, animal welfare issues within any prisons.
answer_text: Under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, my Department is responsible the enforcement of the legislation as it applies to farmed animals.  My Department does not provide comment when any investigations take place, regardless of setting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the criteria used by her Department when deciding whether removed street lights will be replaced; and (ii) how public safety concerns are factored into that decision-making process.
answer_text: When street lighting is deemed to have reached the end of its life and needs to be replaced, the area is assessed and new lighting is provided only in those areas that meet my Department’s policy for the provision of lighting, RSPPG E072 which can be accessed via the link below.DfI Street Lighting Policy - RSPPG E072Where lights have been removed for safety reasons, they will be prioritised for replacement, subject to the locations meeting the above criteria and the necessary funding being available.The policy aims to ensure replacement lighting is installed where it provides the greatest public benefit, particularly where road or infrastructure safety is concerned, as well as reducing future maintenance and energy costs and environmental impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will instruct an inquiry following the McCullough Review, which found that there was no evidence of systemic unlawful surveillance of journalists by the PSNI.
answer_text: While the McCullough Review found no evidence of systemic surveillance of journalists, which is welcome, it did not amount to a clean bill of health, with several findings of poor or unlawful practice.  This is not solely a matter of operational policing, for which the Chief Constable is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, but also impacts wider issues of confidence. The Policing Board has stated that it views the Report as part of fulfilling its duties under Section 59 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 2000.  The Board has made clear it wishes to carefully review the findings of the McCullough Review, and to receive a formal response from the Chief Constable on remedial action and plans for implementing the recommendations, before deciding on next steps. I believe it is important that the Board is given the time and space to fully consider the report and its preferred way forward. As I have said previously, I share the serious concerns which have been expressed by the Board in relation to this issue and stand ready to support the Board in whatever actions it considers necessary.  In addition, the Investigatory Powers Tribunal has yet to report, after which a further update to McCullough will be made. Ahead of the Tribunal findings and the Board’s considerations, it would be premature to comment on what actions may be required on my part.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the reasons why prison inmates in Northern Ireland are allowed to smoke in their cells, when smoking in cells has been banned in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: The Smoking (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 came into effect on 30 April 2007, with prisons being brought under the same smoke-free legislation as other workplaces.  An exemption was made for smoking in cells on the basis that they are considered a prisoner’s home, recognising the unique operational environment of prisons. While the health risks of smoking are understood, it was felt at that time that a ban of smoking in cells in Northern Ireland would require the management of large numbers of nicotine dependent people in a secure setting, with associated risks for order and control.A number of factors, including the COVID pandemic, led to a delay in implementing similar cessation approaches in Northern Ireland that have been deployed elsewhere.The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) are, however, in the process of implementing a proof-of-concept for the use of e-cigarettes within an area of Magilligan Prison.  This is scheduled for implementation in December 2025 and subject to satisfactory evaluation will inform full roll-out across the wider NIPS Estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any support will be offered to schools or families facing increased travel costs or logistical challenges due to the closure of Kilrea Bridge, Bann Road,Kilrea.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport. It has advised that due to the closure of Kilrea Bridge, it has made some temporary changes to routes and timings for pupils travelling to school on EA and private hire transport, however these services remain largely unaffected.School transport services operated by Translink have been more significantly impacted. Bus services from Ballymena, Cullybackey, and Rasharkin have been diverted through Portglenone and an additional shuttle bus service is operating at school times to minimise disruption to pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the projected timeline for the assessment and repair of the retaining wall approaching Kilrea Bridge.
answer_text: The Kilrea Bridge was closed on 20 September in the interest of public safety due to extensive cracking on the retaining wall located at the south-west end of the Bridge. My Department is currently working at pace to develop a scheme to repair the wall and is assessing if a temporary solution can be implemented in the interim to allow the road to be reopened as quickly as possible, while a permanent solution is developed.  Regrettably, I am currently not able to provide a timeframe at this stage. A signed diversionary route is currently in place.  I fully appreciate the impact of the closure and the understandable frustration of the local community, including businesses in the area and I apologise for the inconvenience caused.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any contingency plans in place for school transport affected by the closure of the Kilrea Bridge, Bann road, Kilrea.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport. It has advised that due to the closure of Kilrea Bridge, it has made some temporary changes to routes and timings for pupils travelling to school on EA and private hire transport, however these services remain largely unaffected.School transport services operated by Translink have been more significantly impacted. Bus services from Ballymena, Cullybackey, and Rasharkin have been diverted through Portglenone and an additional shuttle bus service is operating at school times to minimise disruption to pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether an economic impact assessment has been carried out on local businesses affected by the closure of the Kilrea Bridge.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of an economic impact assessment having been carried out on local businesses affected by the closure of the Kilrea Bridge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether microchipping of cats will also extend to those found in a prison locality.
answer_text: As set out in my Animal Welfare Pathway 2025-27, my Department is currently gathering an evidence base, prior to consulting on the mandatory microchipping of cats. Therefore, no policy decisions have been made, including if cats in specified localities should be exempt from any requirements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline whether a corporate body can be the legal owner of a dog as designated on a dog licence.
answer_text: The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 states that a person to whom a dog licence is issued is deemed to be the keeper of the dog.  The dog licence application form, as set out in The Dogs (Licensing and Identification) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012, must be completed by a named person.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what statutory body has overall responsibility for animal welfare in a prison setting.
answer_text: Under The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, my Department is responsible for enforcing statutory obligations relating to the welfare of farmed animals while local councils are responsible for legislative obligations pertaining to non-farmed animals, regardless of the setting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of mega clinics.
answer_text: Mega clinics alleviate pressure across the system by targeting large groups of patients in a ‘one stop shop’ which involves accessible and comprehensive surgical and anaesthetic preoperative assessment in a single appointment. The mega clinic caters to patients waiting for common procedures across a range of surgical specialties including general surgery, gynaecology, ENT and orthopaedics which typically can have long waiting lists.The mega clinic model has been running for a number of years in Northern Ireland and these clinics bring together surgical, anaesthetic, nursing, allied health professional and administrative teams. In many cases, patients leave the clinic with a date for their procedure at one of the regional elective care centres within three months of assessment.The mega clinics are patient centred and are beneficial for patients seeking timely surgical evaluations without delays typically associated with the multi-departmental visits. The integrated approach ensures streamlined care, where consultation, diagnostic and treatment planning happen in one visit. The clinics are designed to handle a large volume of patients efficiently, effectively, alleviating pressure on our healthcare system and ensuring timely care for more people. The establishment of the mega clinic model is having a positive impact on reducing the number of people waiting for both assessment and treatment in Northern Ireland. The Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan has provided £10m of non-recurrent funding to allow the on-going provision of mega clinics this year. From April to August 2025, approximately 3,175 patients have been seen at these clinics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps he will take to ensure the Chief Constable has the funds to pay the universal compensation offer to the PSNI officers and staff affected by the 2023 data breach.
answer_text: ANSWERIt is deeply disappointing that as “a result of PSNI as an organisation not seizing opportunities to better and more proactively secure and protect its data, to identify and prevent risk earlier on, or to do so in an agile and modern way”,  the public purse will have to pay out in the region of £119m in compensation and legal fees to those impacted by the 2023 data breach. In relation to steps taken within my department in response to the PSNI Data Breach, my officials approved the business case, subject to the required financial resources being secured. They have had detailed discussions with their counterparts in Treasury in an attempt to secure that additional funding. In addition, I wrote to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury to ask for access to the Treasury Reserve to meet the associated costs. The Chief Secretary has since responded, stating that there will be no funding provided from the Reserve to fund the costs of the PSNI Data Breach incident. This is disappointing given the exceptional and unavoidable nature of these costs, which I believe met the conditions for a Reserve claim. It will now be a matter for the Executive to consider how this financial pressure is to be managed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department engaged directly with the Police Federation of Northern Ireland prior to her proposal to extend the scope of penalty notices to include assaults on police officers.
answer_text: The proposals included in the consultation on the expansion of Out of Court Disposals, including Penalty Notices which are provided for in the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, were developed by a cross justice working group led by the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) and included members from my Department, the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) and the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS).  It was also agreed by the Criminal Justice Board (CJB) that my Department would take forward those proposals to public consultation. The consultation offers members of the public and wider society, including those involved in the justice system, to put forward their views on the proposals.  My department did not engage directly with the Police Federation or any other external stakeholder on these proposals.  However, views captured via previous engagement between PSNI colleagues and the Police Federation were fed into the cross- justice working group and helped shape proposals.  PSNI has advised that the Police Federation were consulted in relation to the recent changes to Community Resolution Notice arrangements in August 2024 which provide PSNI officers with discretion to deal with certain offence types by way of Out of Court Disposal where the nature of offending is of an appropriately low-level. The cross-justice working group led by PSNI was instrumental in progressing with these changes.  I understand that the Federation did not report concerns regarding the inclusion of assault on police, which has been included on the list of offences which can be dealt with by way of a CRN since the scheme was introduced in 2016. PSNI has advised that, given that the option of using a CRN for the offence of assault on police has been available from 2016 and that the Justice Act (NI) 2011 which provides for Penalty Notices includes offences for resisting, obstructing and impeding a Constable, it considered the inclusion of assault on police as a natural progression. Therefore, the decision was taken to propose including it as an offence which can be dealt with by way of a penalty notice. In doing so this broadens options available to PSNI when responding to offending behaviours, but also retains the right to prosecute where the offence is deemed more serious.  It should also be noted that amongst the factors which are considered when deciding whether and out-of-court disposal is appropriate are the views of the victim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the concerns raised regarding the placement of an emotional support dog in Magilligan Prison, whether she will initiate an independent review of how this matter has been handled.
answer_text: The placement and use of emotional support dogs in Northern Ireland prisons is an operational matter for the Prison Service. When concerns were expressed about Bailey, the Service asked for an independent assessment by Animal Welfare Officers from Mid & East Antrim Borough Council.This review has already provided the necessary assurance around Bailey’s placement, wellbeing and care. Specifically, the review found that:Bailey was in excellent health – presenting as alert, active and comfortable, with no signs of neglect or stress, and with care exceeding minimum standards.His accommodation was clean and safe, and allegations that he was housed in a windowless room or in unsuitable conditions were incorrect.Safeguarding was fully in place, with continuous supervision and no concerns about his safety or wellbeing.Bailey had 24/7 access to on-call veterinary care should he need it and also had regular interaction with another dog to provide social stimulation and companionship.The review concluded that “the concerns raised by external organisations regarding Bailey’s welfare are unfounded. Based on direct observations and welfare officer reports, Bailey is in excellent condition, appropriately housed, and properly safeguarded.”I have no plans for any further review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she, or her Department, has provided information to any PSNI officer (i) in writing; (ii) verbally, which states that publishing images of suspects or defendants would pose a risk of vigilante or paramilitary attack to them.
answer_text: The publication of images of suspects or defendants is entirely an operational matter for the Chief Constable.As the Member is aware, the Chief Constable is operationally independent from both me as Justice Minister and my Department and is accountable to the Policing Board. In respecting that independence, it would be inappropriate for me or my officials to provide any information, whether in writing or verbally, suggesting that the publication of images of suspects or defendants could pose a risk of vigilante or paramilitary attack.  You may, therefore, wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable or the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of taxi drivers, currently providing home to school transport for pupils on behalf of the Education Authority, who have not yet received their higher Access NI clearance.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that all taxi drivers who deliver home to school transport for pupils on behalf of the EA must have Enhanced Access NI clearance in place prior to being deployed on a service and it will not award a contract to a private operator if this is not in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the circumstances in which a child with special educational needs has to travel 58 miles each way to their school, a round journey which takes 2 hours 40 minutes every day.
answer_text: School placements and transport arrangements for pupils with a statement of Special Educational Needs are determined on an individual basis, to meet that child’s specific needs. There are circumstances in which a pupil may have to travel a distance to school, however I cannot comment on this specific journey as it may identify a child.  The Education Authority provides transport assistance for over 13,000 pupils who have additional SEN transport needs and it is important to understand that the school transport network is not unlimited. The EA must balance journey times for individual pupils with making best use of the available capacity on school transport services. This is to ensure that those pupils who need transport to school are able to avail of it. Where the EA is unable to provide suitable transport or a seat on a vehicle, it may offer parents an allowance in lieu of transport. The journey to school can be longer than might be expected for pupils with SEN who need a specialist placement. Children travelling together on a service will often need picked up from their home addresses and it takes time for pupils to board the bus and get safely secured into their seats. I want children with Special Educational Needs to be able to attend a school which is as close to their home as possible. However, this is dependent on placements being available in their local area. My Department and the EA have a shared vision of every school in Northern Ireland eventually being able to offer specialist provisions so that all children can attend school as close to home as possible.

answer_text: My Department funds the directed, off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship delivered under the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes.Training providers are funded for delivering the training. Under the ApprenticeshipsNI programme, employers are eligible to receive an incentive payment upon the apprentice’s successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship.Departmental funding in support of both programmes over the last three years is set out in the table overleaf.  This includes funding in relation to Covid intervention support for apprenticeships.2022-23(£,000)2023-24(£,000)2024-25(£,000)ApprenticeshipsNI22,96227,24137,670Higher Level Apprenticeships6,7408,76510,570Covid Apprenticeship Support5,037(1,102)56Total34,73934,90448,296			Notes		Covid Apprenticeship Support relates to my Department’s Recruit and Return, Retain, Result intervention programmes.  	The negative spend in 2023-24 against Covid Apprenticeship Support is due to an over accrual in 2022-23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the support her Department provides for employers who take on apprentices with insurance costs.
answer_text: Whilst there is no specific funding available towards insurance costs for employers who take on apprentices, my Department fully funds the directed, off-the-job training element of an apprenticeship, investing over £45 million annually across the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes. This funding ensures that employers are not financially liable for the delivery of apprenticeship training.Businesses are also eligible for an employer incentive payment upon the apprentice’s successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework, ranging from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area. This incentive is designed to assist employers with the broader costs of supporting and mentoring apprentices. 	Employers in the north are also exempt from paying Class 1 National Insurance contributions for apprentices under the age of 25, subject to certain conditions being met. I want to help more employers and individuals benefit from our apprenticeship model.  I recently published an Apprenticeship Action Plan which aims to ensure that apprenticeship pathways bring real value to businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how a village can take steps to make its case for added wastewater capacity works, when it is already at full capacity.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information from them.NI Water encourages early engagement to consider any constraints and try to identify a solution at an early stage.  NI Water also advised that it continues to support development, wherever possible, and developments within constrained areas may  be approved where:The site has extant planning with no conditions relating to capacity constraints; orThe site is a redevelopment and there is no intensification of foul sewage discharge post development compared to the sewage discharge from the site within the last 5 years.In addition, I am taking a three-pronged approach to address wastewater capacity. Firstly, I continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, I have brought forward a new Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill and thirdly, I have completed a consultation on proposals for developer contributions. That consultation has now closed and a consultation response paper will issue in due course.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of money raised from the apprenticeship levy, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Office for National Statistics (ONS) estimate the North’s Apprenticeship Levy receipts to be £62 million in 2020/21, £69 million in 2021/22, and £73 million in 2022/23, the most recent time period for which statistics are available. Estimates are based on an apportionment of revenues across Wales, Scotland, England and the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of dogs living permanently in a prison in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The use of dogs by the Northern Ireland Prison Service is an operational matter for the relevant Governor in each prison establishment. There are currently three dogs living permanently within our Prisons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she has taken with the Minister of Finance to secure the funding necessary to settle the PSNI data breach litigation claims.
answer_text: Under normal circumstances it would fall to the body which committed the data breach to fund any fines or claims arising.  Nevertheless, given the quantum involved, the costs of settling the data breach legal claims are clearly not affordable from within the PSNI allocation or the Department of Justice (DoJ) budget under any circumstances without additional funding from the Executive or HM Treasury.I have, therefore, engaged from the outset with the Minister of Finance and Executive colleagues to secure the vital funding needed to settle these claims.This has included reporting pressures and submitting bids as part of the annual budget setting process and in-year monitoring rounds, as well as formal correspondence associated with the business case approval. In addition, I have consistently raised this issue at Executive meetings and highlighted the critical need to secure funding.My officials have also continued to engage regularly with their Department of Finance (DoF) counterparts in order to secure the necessary future funding and I have also raised this with Executive colleagues on each occasion which budget is discussed.I am aware that DoF officials and the Minister of Finance remain in contact with HM Treasury officials in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of (i) pay; and (ii) employer pension contributions the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland has received during any periods of sickness absence or any other form of leave, outside of entitled annual leave, over the last 24 months.
answer_text: Pay and pension contributions for the Police Ombudsman are administered by the Office of the Police Ombudsman (OPONI).  Accordingly, you may wish to contact OPONI directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he has given to exploring obstacles and barriers to men entering the teaching profession.
answer_text: I do not consider there are any obstacles and barriers to men entering the teaching profession.I recognise that that there are not enough male teachers in the workforce or choosing teaching as a profession. I consider that it is important for pupils to have male teachers as role models to help break down the stereotype of teaching being a predominately female profession.We can collectively raise and promote the public profile of teaching as an attractive career prospect for all, both males and females.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of direct engagements he has had (a) in writing; and (b) by other correspondence with (i) HM Treasury; and (ii) other central government departments, about the impact of changes to the agricultural property relief and business property relief on farmers.
answer_text: A joint letter was issued to the Chancellor on 22nd November 2024 by the First Minister, deputy First Minister, Finance Minister and I outlining our concerns about the significant impact of the planned inheritance tax changes on Northern Ireland farms and businesses. While my direct engagement with HM Treasury has been limited given the Department of Finance leads on Executive engagement with Treasury and on matters of taxation, I have raised my concerns directly with other central government departments, specifically Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).I wrote to the then Defra Secretary of State, Steve Reed MP on 11th February 2025, again on 12th March 2025 and once more on 30th May 2025 to call on him to ask the Treasury to consider the mitigating measure to remove inheritance tax liabilities where inherited farmland is retained for farming. I also asked to meet with Mr Reed MP to discuss this option.  I have also highlighted my concerns about the impact of inheritance tax changes at Inter-Ministerial Meetings since the UK Government announcement was made in October 2024 and most recently in August 2025, I raised the issue at a bi-lateral meeting with the then Minister for Food Security and Rural Affairs, Daniel Zeichner MP.I am disappointed that the UK Government has not decided to introduce any mitigating measures ahead of the inheritance tax policy changes coming into effect in April 2026 but I will continue to raise the issue and highlight the impact on NI farming families.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of taxi drivers who have convictions for (i) sexual; and (ii) domestic violence, offences.
answer_text: It is a legal requirement for the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA), as the licensing authority, to be satisfied that an applicant meets the necessary requirements before a taxi driver’s licence can be granted. All taxi driver licence applicants in the North are required to provide an Enhanced Disclosure Certificate (EDC) which provides details of convictions relevant to their taxi driver licence application. If an EDC is not provided, their application is cancelled, and no taxi driver licence is granted. In addition to the application process, licence holders must inform the DVA of any convictions incurred during the currency of a licence.The information contained in the EDC is considered when determining a taxi driver licence application. In accordance with AccessNI Code of Practice, the EDC is then subsequently destroyed. Therefore, the DVA does not hold the information required to answer this question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the longest return journey from home to their place of education that a pupil with special education needs has been asked to undertake this academic year, by (i) miles; and (ii) estimated travelling time.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that in 2025/26, the longest journey from home to school for a pupil with Special Educational Needs is 58.4 miles each way. The estimated travelling time of this journey is 1 hour 20 minutes each way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the emotional support dog in HMP Magilligan (i) has a dedicated handler; and (ii) lives in the prison or goes to a family home after a shift.
answer_text: While he emotional support dog does not have a dedicated handler, he is under continuous supervision by prison staff as part of a wide range of safeguarding measures.The dog resides within the prison. His accommodation was found to be suitable, clean and safe by a recent Mid and East Antrim Borough Council Animal Welfare Visit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the decisions by PSNI officers to charge and remand suspects to the next available court is strictly a matter within a PSNI officers operational independence.
answer_text: My Department has responsibility for the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE NI). Articles 39 ‘Duties of custody officer after charge’ and 48 ‘Bail after arrest’ of PACE NI set out the provisions for the duties of custody officers in relation to the detention of a person in custody.  They state that the custody officer shall order the release of suspects charged, either on bail or without bail, and set the conditions for doing so.  The exercise of the duties of custody officers is an operational  matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there is a legal definition for a therapy dog in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The regulation of dogs is not a matter for the Department of Justice.  The Northern Ireland Prison Service does not currently use therapy dogs within prison settings; however, they have for several years used emotional support dogs to support people in prison settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of inspections conducted on the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland by the Criminal Justice Inspectorate of Northern Ireland, over the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWER I would direct the Member to the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland website, where all reports are published in full, including those conducted on the Office of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland: Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland Archives - CJI NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what is the name of the dog that is housed within Magilligan Prison; and (ii) what breed is it.
answer_text: ANSWER Magilligan’s emotional support dog is called Bailey and he is a cocker spaniel.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any consideration that has been given to public transport infrastructure on St Patrick's Link Road, Ballymena, to assist in servicing the new Northern Regional College and any future amenities in the area.
answer_text: St Patrick’s Link was funded by the Department for Communities and my department managed its delivery as part of the wider St Patrick’s Regeneration Programme.The design of the St Patrick’s Link aligns to the guidance provided within the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges and within the Department’s Bus Stop Design Guide. The provision of any new public transport services in the area will be an operational decision for Translink in response to emerging demand.However I am aware of the recent media coverage regarding public transport provision in the area and my department is actively engaging with Translink to consider suitable bus facilities for the new Northern Regional College and potential future amenities in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of children with special educational needs who rely on taxis to get to school; and (ii) are the taxi drivers who provide such transport subject to any additional vetting, over and above the taxi driver licence requirements as set by the Driver and Vehicle Agency.
answer_text: 4,648 pupils with a statement of special education needs avail of taxi services for all or part of their journey to and from school.In addition to the taxi driver licencing requirements, all drivers delivering home to school transport services on behalf of the Education Authority must hold an Enhanced Access NI certificate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to the comfort dog housed within Magillgan Prison, to outline (i) what risk assessments have been completed in advance; (ii) are there any inmates who have unsupervised access to the dog; and (iii) is there a policy in place that ensures that no inmate with convictions for violence, sexual abuse or cruelty to animals has access to the dog.
answer_text: ANSWER:  Bailey is an emotional support dog registered to the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS). All areas of NIPS establishments are safe for their use and deployment. His introduction to site was planned and graduated to assess any potential risks to his emotional and physical welfare and measure his interactions with all persons on site.It is widely recognised that the use of emotional support dogs can offer significant therapeutic benefits and are used across the justice system, including supporting victims and by supporting children/young people and their loved ones before they give evidence at trial.In prisons, the presence of the dogs can offer an additional way to support people with poor mental health and addiction issues, help promote positive changes in behaviour and reduce stress.  Support dogs can also help prisoners enhance their social skills, self-esteem, and self-confidence.  These benefits can contribute to reducing the risk that someone might re-offend and prevent further victims of crime in the future.Bailey’s welfare is Magilligan’s primary concern and, accordingly, he is not left unsupervised with prisoners at any time.Prior to Bailey’s arrival, Magilligan identified all prisoners with any offence relating to animals, and they are barred from any contact with him. This risk assessment process is completed on all transfers to Magilligan.The Governor of Magilligan arranged for Animal Welfare Officers from Mid & East Antrim Borough Council to visit Magilligan to review all aspects of Bailey’s care and wellbeing.  They found:Bailey is in excellent health – presenting as alert, active and comfortable, with no signs of neglect or stress, and with care exceeding minimum standards.His accommodation is clean and safe, and allegations that he is housed in a windowless room or in unsuitable conditions are incorrect.Safeguarding is fully in place, with continuous supervision and no concerns about his safety or wellbeing.Bailey has 24/7 access to on-call veterinary care should he need it and has regular interaction with another dog to provide social stimulation and companionship.The review concluded that “the concerns raised by external organisations regarding Bailey’s welfare are unfounded. Based on direct observations and welfare officer reports, Bailey is in excellent condition, appropriately housed, and properly safeguarded.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) why the Easy Read guide to the consultation of her proposal to extend penalty notices describes the violent offences it relates to as being lower level violent crimes, when the proposal includes assault occasioning actual bodily harm; and (ii) for her assessment of whether crimes covered by assault occasioning actual bodily harm are lower level.
answer_text: Many of the criminal offences which exist in Northern Ireland are broadly defined and can cover a wide spectrum of offending behaviour. Assault Occasioning Actual Bodily Harm (AOABH) is one such offence.  This is reflected in the fact that the most serious AOABH cases are prosecuted in the Crown Court which has greater sentencing powers to deal with the most serious cases.  The majority of cases involving AOABH are, however, prosecuted at magistrates’ courts where some cases at the lowest end of the spectrum result in non-custodial disposals such as community sentences and monetary penalties.   The proposals regarding the expansion of Out of Court Disposals were developed in conjunction with key justice stakeholders including the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), which led the working group through which the proposals were developed.  The working group was supported by the Public Prosecution Service (PPS), the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS), and officials from my Department.  The Criminal Justice Board (CJB), which comprises senior leaders across the justice system including myself as Justice Minister, the Permanent Secretary, Lady Chief Justice, Chief Constable of PSNI, the Director of Public Prosecutions, the Director of NI Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) and other senior justice officials agreed that my Department would consult on the proposals to help inform policy development. The Easy Read version of the consultation document employs simple language, short sentences, a clear structure, and relevant images to effectively convey the meaning of the text and highlight key points. However, it is important to note that the document cannot serve as a literal translation of the entire original consultation paper, which may result in a loss of nuance and balance from the original text. Nevertheless, the Department believes that the language used in the document is not inaccurate; rather, it is a simplified version of the original content.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will conduct a review of the planning and consultation undertaken in relation to the regeneration of the St Patrick's Barracks site, Ballymena, to learn any potential lessons, taking into account that the St Patrick's Link Road and Northern Regional College lack bus lay-bys or set down and pick up infrastructure.
answer_text: The design of the St Patrick’s link road was carried out in accordance with the Department for Infrastructure’s (DfI) road design guidelines applicable at the time of the planning application.  Therefore, the Department for Communities will not be conducting a review of the planning and consultation undertaken.DfI advises that bus laybys are not generally recommended for urban roads with 30mph speed limits, such as St Patrick’s Link Road, and the absence of a lay-by on a road does not preclude future provision of public transport services.  Indeed, there are examples of other recently constructed roads in Northern Ireland where Translink operates buses safely without them.  The provision of any new public transport services in the area will be an operational decision for Translink in response to emerging demand, and my Department is committed to working with them and all stakeholders in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether an assessment has been made as to whether her Department's proposal to extend out of court penalty tickets to the offence of assault on police will increase or reduce the deterrent value that the criminal justice system would provide, in terms of attacks on PSNI officers.
answer_text: The proposals to expand the use of Penalty Notices to the offence of assault on police were developed through a cross justice working group led by the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).  The maximum penalties for those convicted at courts in Northern Ireland of the offence of assault on police remain unchanged by proposals within the consultation regarding the expansion of Out of Court Disposals.  The proposals aim to provide PSNI with greater flexibility in dealing with cases which they consider to be suitable for an Out of Court Disposal, based on the particular facts and circumstances of each case.  Penalty Notices were introduced in the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 as a means to divert first time or non-habitual offenders away from prosecution at court.   The offences of assaulting, resisting, obstructing or impeding a constable in the execution of his duty, or a person assisting a constable in the execution of his duty, are provided for in section 66(1) of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1998.  The assault offence within section 66(1) is commonly referred to as assault on police. All offences under section 66(1) can be prosecuted at either the Crown Court or the magistrates’ courts.  The maximum penalty upon conviction in the Crown Court for all offences under section 66(1) is two years, and six months in the magistrates’ courts.  Resisting, obstructing or impeding police are offences for which the PSNI can presently issue a Penalty Notice. Assault on police, along with, resisting police, and obstructing police, are also offences for which PSNI can issue a Community Resolution Notice (CRN).Any proposals brought forward which would make assaults on police, offences which PSNI can issue a Penalty Notice for, will be subject to final policy direction.  Any final policy will also take into account any relevant changes made by the Sentencing Bill, which I plan to introduce to the Assembly by the end of 2025.   That Bill will seek to create a new offence of assaulting a person in the course of providing services to the public, performing a public duty, or delivering a public service, which will apply to police officers assaulted in the course of duty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has (i) requested; and (ii) received senior counsel advice on operational independence and any impact it has on her capacity to answer questions in relation to her role as Minister of Justice.
answer_text: I have not requested or received senior counsel advice on operational independence. That independence has not had any impact on my role as Minister of Justice, as lines of accountability and independence are integral the tripartite arrangements for policing. The ability of police leadership to make operational decisions free from any political interference is an important example of the checks and balances that underpin and protect a free and democratic society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will withdraw her consultation on the proposed expansion of Out of Court Disposals, including Penalty Notices in the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, and reissue a more accurate Easy Read guide to the consultation.
answer_text: I have no plans to withdraw the consultation on the proposed expansion of Out of Court Disposals, including Penalty Notices in the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, which launched on 7 July.  The consultation forms part of my Department’s Programme for Government commitments to explore options for expanding the use of Out of Court Disposals to help speed up the time taken to dispose of those cases and reduce pressure on courts and other justice agencies.  It is a significant element of the Speeding Up Justice programme which is seeking to minimise avoidable delay in the criminal justice system and will be a key tool in reducing demand on the formal system from low level offending and help redirect scarce resources to more serious offences, such as serious sexual offences. The consultation sets out a range of proposals in relation to often complex, interlinked, issues which reflect the challenges within our criminal justice system. These proposals were developed in full partnership with a range of key stakeholders from across the justice system, including the PSNI. Providing an Easy Read version of the consultation is not a statutory requirement but rather a matter of good practice.  My Department made the decision to commission its production for the Out of Court Disposals consultation to seek to encourage and maximise participation. This aligns with, for example, guidance from the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland, which emphasises the importance of considering how best to communicate information to children and young people, individuals with learning disabilities, and minority ethnic communities.The Easy Read document employs simple language, short sentences, a clear structure, and relevant images to effectively convey the meaning of the text and highlight key points. However, it is important to note that the document cannot serve as a literal translation of the entire original consultation paper, which may result in a loss of nuance and balance from the original text. Nevertheless, the Department believes that the language used in the document is not inaccurate; rather, it is a simplified version of the original content.
